recqzugSOIGMSKpGv.orig_title: Rechtliche Anforderungen bei KI-Nutzung | Der Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz Niedersachsen
recqzugSOIGMSKpGv.gen_title: Niedersachsen data protection authority publishes AI Act guidance for professional users
recqzugSOIGMSKpGv.summary:
On 27 April 2026, the Niedersächsischer Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz (Niedersachsen State Data Protection Authority) issued practical frequently asked questions addressing legal and data protection requirements for artificial intelligence system use under the EU AI Act.

The authority recognises that AI systems have become integral to professional and private contexts across the region. The EU AI Act establishes a unified legal framework applicable across member states, containing binding obligations for developers, providers and users of AI models and systems. The authority identifies the Act's dual purpose as promoting the development of trustworthy AI systems whilst protecting fundamental rights of citizens.

The Niedersachsen authority has made available practice-oriented FAQ guidance specifically designed for professional users in business and public administration. Denis Lehmkemper, the state data protection officer, states that the authority receives increasing enquiries concerning the Act's scope of application, affected use cases and data protection-related considerations. The authority finds that the FAQ addresses the most significant questions on legal and data protection aspects of AI use, with particular focus on the AI Act's provisions and their interaction with existing data protection legislation, including the GDPR.

The authority notes the guidance is subject to continuous updating and expansion to address emerging questions. The complete FAQ is available on the authority's website at lfd.niedersachsen.de/ki. The authority invites suggestions and proposals for additional topics to be covered in the evolving guidance.

recWkeGrl04JKicSr.orig_title: Opinion 10/2026 on the draft decision of the Dutch Supervisory Authority regarding the Controller Binding Corporate Rules of the SLB Group
recWkeGrl04JKicSr.gen_title: EDPB issues opinion on SLB Group controller binding corporate rules approval
recWkeGrl04JKicSr.summary:
On 15 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board adopted Opinion 10/2026 on the draft decision of the Dutch Supervisory Authority regarding the approval of Controller Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) for the SLB Group, exercising its role under Article 64(1)(f) GDPR to ensure consistent application of data protection standards across international transfers.

The EDPB noted that BCRs serve as a mechanism enabling groups of undertakings to transfer personal data to third countries where no adequacy decision exists under Article 45(3) GDPR, provided they demonstrate appropriate safeguards and enforceable data subject rights under Article 46(2) GDPR. The EDPB stated that BCRs must meet minimum requirements specified in Article 47(2) GDPR and must be approved by the competent supervisory authority, the "BCR Lead", through the consistency mechanism outlined in Articles 63 and 64(1)(f) GDPR.

The EDPB acknowledged the efforts undertaken by organisations to maintain GDPR compliance in a global environment and affirmed the important role of BCRs in framing international transfers. The Board confirmed its commitment to supporting companies in establishing BCRs that meet strengthened protection standards introduced by the GDPR compared to the prior Directive 95/46/EC framework. The opinion indicated that the EDPB Recommendations 1/2022 on the Application for Approval and elements and principles for Controller BCRs, adopted on 20 June 2023, supersede previous working documents and establish contemporary standards for BCR assessment.

The EDPB clarified that its opinion addressed only whether the BCRs afforded appropriate safeguards consistent with Article 47 GDPR and the Recommendations, and did not assess other GDPR obligations referenced in the BCRs. The Board emphasised that data exporters subject to GDPR retain responsibility, consistent with the Court of Justice judgment in C-311/18, to assess whether the level of protection required by EU law is respected in the relevant third country, including through supplementary measures where necessary.

rectlzajWoKWpzrYW.orig_title: Le rapport d’activité du DPO : piloter la conformité et valoriser ses actions
rectlzajWoKWpzrYW.gen_title: CNIL recommends data protection officer activity reports as compliance management tool
rectlzajWoKWpzrYW.summary:
On 27 April 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL) published guidance recommending that data protection officers (DPOs) prepare annual activity reports to document compliance efforts and report to senior management, whilst noting that such reports are not legally mandatory under the General Data Protection Regulation.

The CNIL states that although DPOs face no legal obligation to produce activity reports, doing so constitutes a recommended good practice that aligns with their core responsibilities. The authority identifies several benefits of activity reporting: direct contribution to compliance management and accountability to the highest level of organisational leadership; evaluation and tracking of the organisation's data protection maturity over time; and capacity to communicate compliance achievements internally and externally.

The CNIL finds that activity reports should serve as essential compliance management tools, functioning as operational dashboards with historical records of actions taken. The authority recommends that reports, whether quarterly, semi-annual or annual depending on organisational activities, should: diagnose principal processing activities and high-impact projects by reference to the record of processing activities, impact assessments, audit results, data breaches and complaints; evaluate legal, financial and reputational risks; document measures taken to achieve regulatory compliance and promote data protection practices; and identify barriers to improving data protection maturity. The CNIL determines that report preparation creates opportunity for essential self-assessment questions regarding completed priority actions, processing activities requiring particular attention, and adequacy of technical and organisational measures relative to processing risks.

The CNIL recommends DPOs present activity reports to senior management at least annually, setting out a comprehensive overview of activities, advice and recommendations alongside key performance indicators concerning data subject rights requests, response timeframes, data breaches and document templates. The authority notes such reporting does not compromise DPO independence. The CNIL further recommends DPOs adapt key elements from management-facing reports for internal staff communication through representative bodies and colleagues, strengthening DPO visibility and promoting data protection culture within the organisation.
rectlzajWoKWpzrYW.body: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rectnpKoj5m0zPykc.orig_title: Размещение персональных данных на сайте вуза без нарушений: официальные разъяснения - Национальный центр защиты персональных данных Республики Беларусь
rectnpKoj5m0zPykc.gen_title: Belarus personal data authority issues guidance on publishing student and graduate information on university websites
rectnpKoj5m0zPykc.summary:
On 25 April 2026, the National Centre for Personal Data Protection of the Republic of Belarus issued explanatory guidance on the lawful processing and publication of personal data relating to applicants, students and graduates on the websites of higher education institutions. The guidance addresses the particular sensitivity of publishing personal data online, given the difficulty of complete removal of distributed data and the risk of creating digital profiles without consent.

The Centre, in coordination with the Ministry of Education of the Republic of Belarus, identified specific lawful bases and permitted use cases for publishing such personal data. Under article 6 (paragraph twenty) of the Law on Protection of Personal Data and article 55(3) of the Education Code of the Republic of Belarus, higher education institutions may publish lists of admitted and enrolled students without consent where necessary to fulfil legislative obligations. The Centre determines that the permissible scope includes surname, given name, patronymic, scores achieved and information regarding benefits provided, though sensitive details such as disability status or orphan status are to be indicated only as "benefit" without further specification.

The guidance establishes additional permissible cases for publication: composition of the institution council and student council leadership, where such publication is provided for in the institution's charter (relying on article 6 paragraph twenty of the Data Protection Law); composition of councils on prevention of neglect and juvenile offences; information about notable or successful graduates; participation in educational events; diplomas and commendations; foreign student reviews; news content concerning institutional activity; completion of internships; olympiad admission; and dormitory residence.

The Centre notes that where publication of council composition is not mandated by the institution's governing documents, explicit consent from data subjects is required. The guidance was prepared to establish uniform practice across higher education institutions, protect the rights of data subjects, and prevent unlawful distribution of personal data of applicants, students and graduates. Additional methodological documents addressing personal data processing in other sectors remain available on the Centre's website.
rectnpKoj5m0zPykc.body: National Centre for Personal Data Protection (Belarus)

recKnVuvPgxKln46z.orig_title: Belgium adopts a new national cyber crisis response plan
recKnVuvPgxKln46z.gen_title: Belgium adopts revised national cyber crisis response plan by Royal Decree
recKnVuvPgxKln46z.summary:
On 27 April 2026, the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium confirmed that Belgium adopted a comprehensively revised national cyber crisis response plan by Royal Decree, replacing the framework established in 2017. The CCB recorded 556 cybersecurity incident reports in 2025, of which 8 carried national impact, with public administration and healthcare identified as the most frequently targeted sectors.

The revised plan is grounded in two legislative instruments: the Law of 26 April 2024 establishing a framework for the cybersecurity of networks and information systems of public interest, which transposed the European NIS2 Directive into Belgian law, and the Law of 25 May 2007 on civil security. The CCB notes that this legislative foundation ensures the plan functions as an operational instrument in real crisis situations, not merely as a reference document.

The plan establishes a structured response framework for events requiring management, coordination or support at national level. The CCB identifies that it defines the role of each party without encroaching on the specific remit of individual actors and provides stakeholders with a common framework for concerted action. The plan covers national preparedness objectives and activities, tasks and responsibilities of cyber crisis management authorities, crisis management procedures integrated into the general national emergency management framework, channels for information exchange between stakeholders, national preparedness measures including exercises and training, relevant public and private sector stakeholders, and national arrangements ensuring Belgium's effective participation in coordinated crisis management at European level.

The CCB states that the plan prioritises protection of the country's critical sectors. In the event of a crisis, it enables competent bodies, each acting within the limits of its legal mandate, to work together seamlessly with clear understanding of respective responsibilities and information flows. The CCB notes that the 2026 revision is fully aligned with the European drive to strengthen digital resilience and incorporates explicit mechanisms to ensure Belgium can participate effectively in coordinated cyber crisis management at EU level in accordance with NIS2 Directive requirements. The CCB confirms that for security reasons, detailed operational content of the plan remains non-public.

recrLvgbCDZ0uqwxX.orig_title: Opinion 12/2026 on the draft decision of the Spanish Supervisory Authority regarding the Controller Binding Corporate Rules of the Santander Group
recrLvgbCDZ0uqwxX.gen_title: EDPB issues opinion on Spanish supervisory authority's draft decision to approve Santander Group controller binding corporate rules
recrLvgbCDZ0uqwxX.summary:
On 15 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board adopted Opinion 12/2026 on the draft decision of the Spanish Data Protection Authority regarding the approval of controller binding corporate rules (BCRs) for the Santander Group, addressing compliance with Article 47 GDPR and the EDPB's Recommendations 1/2022 on the elements and principles of controller BCRs.

The EDPB's role under Article 64(1)(f) GDPR is to issue an opinion where a supervisory authority proposes to approve BCRs, ensuring consistent application of the GDPR throughout the European Economic Area. The EDPB confirmed that BCRs serve as an important mechanism for framing international transfers of personal data in the absence of an adequacy decision under Article 45(3) GDPR. Under Article 46 GDPR, controllers or processors may transfer personal data to third countries only where they have provided appropriate safeguards and enforceable data subject rights are available. The EDPB recognises that a group of undertakings may provide such safeguards through legally binding BCRs that expressly confer enforceable rights on data subjects and meet the specific requirements listed in Article 47(2) GDPR.

The EDPB noted that BCRs must be subject to approval from the competent supervisory authority, referred to as the BCR Lead, in accordance with the consistency mechanism set out in Articles 63 and 64(1)(f) GDPR. The EDPB's opinion addresses whether the Santander Group's BCRs afford appropriate safeguards by meeting all requirements of Article 47 GDPR and the EDPB Recommendations 1/2022, which superseded prior Article 29 Working Party guidance. The EDPB clarified that its review and the supervisory authority's assessment do not address other GDPR obligations mentioned in the BCRs beyond those related to Article 47 GDPR.

The EDPB further recalled that under CJEU judgment C-311/18, responsibility rests with the data exporter subject to GDPR to assess whether the level of protection required by EU law is respected in the third country of concern, potentially with assistance from the data importer.

recCftKCsdKrbImbu.gen_title: British Columbia Information and Privacy Commissioner reports 86% surge in privacy complaints
recCftKCsdKrbImbu.summary:
On 24 April 2026, Michael Harvey, Information and Privacy Commissioner for British Columbia, reported to the Select Standing Committee on Finance and Government Services that the Office of the Information and Privacy Commissioner (OIPC) has experienced unprecedented increases in demand for its services. The Commissioner reported that privacy and access complaints received increased by 86 per cent in the past year, requests for review rose by 50 per cent, and privacy breaches reported to the office increased by 28 per cent, representing approximately 3,900 files received annually. The Commissioner noted that file volumes have more than doubled over the past five years, with the latest year-over-year increase characterised as a "hockey stick" pattern in data visualisation terms.

The OIPC has implemented several procedural efficiencies to manage the surge. The Commissioner identified the creation of an early resolution investigator role, which has resolved approximately 104 more files than initially anticipated. The office is also making greater use of discretion to decline or discontinue investigations in cases where proceeding will not meaningfully protect privacy or support accountability. Additional process improvements under consideration include submission limits on files and restrictions on the number of active files individuals may maintain concurrently at the OIPC.

The Commissioner identified artificial intelligence large language models as a potential contributing factor to the complaint increase. The OIPC finds that some individuals may be using LLMs to analyse freedom of information responses and generate complaints based on the models' suggestions. The Commissioner noted that known issues with LLMs, including their tendency to hallucinate, exaggerate, and extrapolate, result in many such complaints being more complex than typical matters. Despite closing 36 per cent more investigation files compared to the previous year, the OIPC's investigation queue remains elevated given the 60 per cent year-over-year increase in file count, creating a gap between incoming demand and case resolution capacity.
recCftKCsdKrbImbu.body: Office of the Information and Privacy Commissioner for BC

recYW31eiEvFQYgF0.orig_title: Council of Europe Committee on New and Emerging Technologies holds inaugural meeting
recYW31eiEvFQYgF0.gen_title: Council of Europe establishes Committee on New and Emerging Technologies with inaugural meeting
recYW31eiEvFQYgF0.summary:
On 23 April 2026, the Council of Europe's Committee on New and Emerging Technologies (CDNET) held its inaugural meeting in Strasbourg from 15 to 17 April, with the EU Agency for Fundamental Rights (FRA) participating as part of the EU delegation.

The three-day meeting combined organisational work with substantive engagement on emerging technology governance. The FRA notes that the meeting included a one-day thematic conference titled "Algorithmic Age: Human Rights, Democracy and Rule of Law", during which the FRA presented results from its research on online content and hate speech. The FRA also provided updates on its ongoing work in these areas as part of its contribution to the Committee's inaugural session.

The Committee's establishment reflects growing institutional focus on the intersection of technological development and fundamental rights protection. The FRA's participation underscores the relevance of fundamental rights considerations to the Committee's remit. Over the two remaining days of the inaugural meeting, CDNET members focused on organisational matters, including the structuring of the Committee's work and the establishment of its operational framework. The meeting brought together representatives from Council of Europe member states and relevant EU institutions to align on priorities for monitoring and responding to new and emerging technologies, particularly artificial intelligence and big data processing, which present distinct challenges for data protection, privacy and human rights compliance.

recqgwaDwA2afc9Oz.orig_title: デジタル関係制度改革検討会 テクノロジーベースの規制改革推進委員会（第4回）｜デジタル庁
recqgwaDwA2afc9Oz.gen_title: Japan's Digital Agency concludes technology-based regulatory reform committee after fourth meeting
recqgwaDwA2afc9Oz.summary:
On 19 March 2026, Japan's Digital Agency held the fourth and final meeting of its Technology-Based Regulatory Reform Promotion Committee, chaired by Hiroshi Esaki, professor at Tokyo University's Graduate School of Information Science and Technology.

The Digital Agency reports that its technology-based regulatory reform initiative has achieved substantial progress in eliminating analogue regulatory barriers that impede digital technology implementation. The Agency states that 98 per cent of national regulations identified as requiring analogue-based review have been completed, whilst local government participation in regulatory modernisation has expanded significantly through Digital Agency support programmes. The committee noted that the initiative, which began in 2021, has operated across ten meetings to establish a framework addressing technological advancement in regulatory environments.

The Digital Agency reports that its regulatory reform approach centred on three coordinated activities: a national regulatory review plan addressing approximately 10,000 regulatory provisions, with roughly 1,000 identified as requiring technical verification; development of a Technology Map categorising analogue regulations against applicable digital solutions; and establishment of a Technology Catalogue documenting products and services for regulatory implementation. The Agency conducted large-scale technical verification across 13 categories and 32 projects during fiscal year 2023, covering 7 regulatory types. The Digital Agency states that the Technology Catalogue now contains approximately 218 listed products and services, encompassing cybersecurity information and implementation records.

The committee was informed that regulatory review mechanisms have been integrated into operational processes, with approximately 80 per cent of pilot implementations advancing to active use. The Digital Agency notes that the Kitakyushu city case study demonstrated sustained regulatory review embedding within procedures, representing transition from temporary prototypes to permanent process integration. The Agency reports that the committee's work will conclude following this meeting, with future activities focused on continued regulatory modernisation efforts and preparation for emerging technological development cycles. Officials indicated the initiative would serve as a reference case for government responses to technological advancement in regulatory contexts.

recH1uYdDSuIBiCpI.gen_title: German data protection authorities discuss digital violence legislation and rising complaint volumes
recH1uYdDSuIBiCpI.summary:
On 23 April 2026, the Conference of Independent Data Protection Authorities of the Federation and the Länder (DSK), alongside sector-specific data protection supervisors, convened to exchange views on a draft law submitted by the Federal Ministry of Justice addressing civil and criminal protections against digital violence.

The DSK discussed how the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and adjacent provisions of European data and digital law, including the Digital Services Act and the AI Regulation, address the creation and dissemination of deepfakes. The Federal Ministry of Justice's proposed legislation aims to impose stricter penalties for individuals who violate people's privacy, with particular emphasis on criminalising sexually explicit deepfakes and unauthorised digital trespass, notably the unauthorised surveillance through GPS trackers, which the authorities note appear with increasing frequency in complaints from affected parties.

A second substantial matter addressed at the meeting concerned the handling of significantly elevated complaint numbers. The DSK determined that a marked increase in complaints has been recorded not only among the independent data protection authorities of the Federation and the Länder, but also within sector-specific oversight bodies responsible for broadcasting, regional media authorities, and religious institutions. The exchange between DSK representatives and data protection specialists from regional media authorities, churches, and broadcasting bodies occurs twice annually, with the 22 April meeting representing one such scheduled coordination.

The DSK comprises independent data protection authorities of the Federation and the Länder and is tasked with safeguarding fundamental data protection rights, achieving uniform application of European and national data protection law, and jointly advancing its further development. The participation of sector-specific supervisory bodies reflects the multi-layered approach to data protection oversight across Germany's regulatory landscape.

rec620QQC8VyXVTZ1.orig_title: eSafety and OAIC working together to protect privacy and safety for all Australians
rec620QQC8VyXVTZ1.gen_title: OAIC and eSafety Commissioner establish formal cooperation agreement on privacy and online safety
rec620QQC8VyXVTZ1.summary:
On 23 April 2026, the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC) and the eSafety Commissioner entered into a Memorandum of Understanding to coordinate regulatory responses where privacy and online safety intersect, formalising existing collaborative efforts and establishing formal communication pathways between the two regulators.

The MOU recognises that certain online harms require integrated regulatory action across both privacy and safety domains. eSafety Commissioner Julie Inman Grant stated that the agreement formalises cooperation on issues including age assurance requirements now mandatory under Australian online industry codes and standards, and compliance with Social Media Minimum Age obligations designed to protect children from abuse and age-inappropriate material. Ms Inman Grant noted that the agreement comes at a time when emerging technologies such as artificial intelligence are amplifying risks, and regulators are increasingly requiring industry to deploy age-assurance technologies that meet regulatory obligations whilst respecting privacy rights in the Australian context.

Australian Information Commissioner Elizabeth Tydd stated that the MOU will advance work led by the Privacy Commissioner, Carly Kind, and will assist the OAIC in monitoring and responding to emerging online privacy risks. Ms Tydd noted that the agreement formalises cooperation between the two agencies to address privacy and safety challenges in the digital landscape, enabling the regulators to share information and expertise and to address specific harms side by side. The OAIC stated that both agencies will work collaboratively to deliver on their statutory functions as set out in the Online Safety Act.

The agreement recognises that implementation of privacy and safety protections often requires parallel consideration of both regulatory frameworks. Ms Inman Grant emphasised that balancing and promoting both privacy and safety protections for all Australians was an established principle for both regulators, with formal recognition through the MOU now setting out how the agencies will continue to coordinate their regulatory efforts.
rec620QQC8VyXVTZ1.body: Office of the Australian Information Commissioner

recQj2SEVIDMQogve.orig_title: Statement by Deputy Commissioner Marc Chénier to the Senate Standing Committee on Social Affairs, Science and Technology on Bill S-5
recQj2SEVIDMQogve.gen_title: Office of the Privacy Commissioner supports Bill S-5 digital health interoperability with privacy safeguards
recQj2SEVIDMQogve.summary:
On 22 April 2026, Deputy Commissioner Marc Chénier of the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada delivered a statement to the Senate Standing Committee on Social Affairs, Science and Technology endorsing Bill S-5, the Connected Care for Canadians Act, conditional on adequate privacy protections for health information exchange.

The OPC stated that Bill S-5 aims to address persistent challenges in the health sector arising from technical incompatibilities between systems, which result in incomplete medical records, duplication of histories and tests, and delays in care. The Deputy Commissioner noted that the Bill would support responsible innovation by accelerating adoption of common standards to enable secure information sharing across health platforms and improve interoperability. The OPC determined that a connected, secure system allowing health information to be used efficiently would enable governments, health administrators and researchers to make evidence-based decisions improving health care delivery.

The OPC confirmed that this objective aligned with a 2022 resolution on securing public trust in digital health care endorsed by the OPC and its provincial and territorial counterparts. The Deputy Commissioner acknowledged that health information constitutes one of the most sensitive categories of personal information, with potential harms from data breaches including discrimination, stigmatisation, and financial and psychological distress.

The OPC expressed support for Bill S-5's interoperability provisions, which specify that access, use and exchange of personal health information are not required where prohibited by privacy laws. However, the OPC recommended that the proposed data-blocking prohibition also be subject to a similar exception. The Deputy Commissioner noted that many requirements and standards, including privacy and security requirements, remain subject to future regulations, and stated it would be important for the OPC to be consulted on development of any regulations pertaining to privacy and security. The OPC indicated it would continue collaborating with provincial and territorial counterparts to facilitate regulatory alignment in the interconnected health sector. The statement highlighted that health technology vendors remain subject to applicable private and public-sector privacy laws, including the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, and emphasised the importance of modernising that legislation.

rec5Hd1dkNhFOH08g.gen_title: ENISA releases National Cybersecurity Assessment Framework Tool 2.0 for Member State evaluation
rec5Hd1dkNhFOH08g.summary:
On 22 April 2026, the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) published the National Cybersecurity Assessment Framework (NCAF) Tool 2.0, an instrument designed to support EU Member States in assessing and strengthening their national cybersecurity capabilities.

ENISA developed NCAF 2.0 as a key EU instrument to enable Member States to evaluate their existing cybersecurity frameworks and identify areas for improvement across their national infrastructure and governance structures. The tool provides a structured methodology for Member States to benchmark their cybersecurity maturity and establish baseline assessments of national capabilities.

The framework serves as a mechanism for Member States to conduct comprehensive evaluations of their cybersecurity posture, allowing for consistent measurement and comparison of national capabilities across the EU. ENISA positions NCAF 2.0 as a supporting mechanism to strengthen the overall cybersecurity resilience of Member States by providing standardised assessment criteria and guidance for national cybersecurity improvement programmes.

The release of NCAF 2.0 reflects ENISA's ongoing role in supporting EU cybersecurity policy implementation and Member State coordination. The tool is intended to assist national authorities and relevant stakeholders in understanding the current state of their cybersecurity capabilities and establishing priorities for strategic development and investment in cybersecurity infrastructure and competencies.

rec5A3pzWLNuqw61B.orig_title: The Belgian Data Protection Authority and its UK counterpart enter a cooperation agreement.
rec5A3pzWLNuqw61B.gen_title: Belgian DPA and UK ICO sign cooperation memorandum on data protection regulation
rec5A3pzWLNuqw61B.summary:
On 22 April 2026, the Belgian Data Protection Authority (Belgian DPA) and the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) signed a Memorandum of Understanding establishing a framework for cooperation in the regulation of personal data protection laws. The signing took place at the Privacy Symposium in Venice, Italy.

The Belgian DPA states that the MoU creates a joint statement of intent providing both parties with a framework to foster productive cooperation whilst adhering to their legal differences and statutory independence. The agreement establishes cooperation in response to an increased need for cross-border coordination, as both authorities recognise the complexities arising from the international circulation and exchange of personal data. The Belgian DPA notes that the framework is intended to streamline regulatory approaches when addressing similar issues and to promote consistency in the administration of comparable data protection laws.

The intended international cooperation covers consultation on each party's regulatory activity to better protect individuals within the scope of applicable data protection and privacy laws, and supporting organisations in compliance with personal data protection obligations. The Belgian DPA indicates that both authorities aim to strengthen existing relations by promoting the exchange of information, experience and best practice to assist one another in the regulation of data protection laws. The agreement sets out broad principles of collaboration by identifying the role and functions of each party and the scope of cooperation. The Belgian DPA identifies parallel investigations or enforcement actions as a priority issue. The Belgian DPA notes that collaboration may include initiatives such as the sharing of experiences and best practices, as well as the exchange of information, excluding personal data, about potential or ongoing investigations regarding potential contraventions impacting both jurisdictions.

The Belgian DPA emphasises that international cooperation has consistently been a value it has held, and that establishing reliable partnerships with other data protection authorities represents a key step in improving data protection in an evolving digital landscape. The Belgian DPA identifies international cooperation as a key pillar of its 2026–2028 strategic plan.

recCcgwC5RRTvk90P.orig_title: Known Attacks and Their Impacts on AI Systems
recCcgwC5RRTvk90P.gen_title: Japan AI Safety Institute publishes taxonomy of 12 attack types on AI systems
recCcgwC5RRTvk90P.summary:
On 22 April 2026, the Japan AI Safety Institute (AISI) released the second edition of its guidance document classifying known attacks on artificial intelligence systems and their impacts across training, model, input and output attack surfaces.

AISI identifies 12 attack types spanning four distinct attack surfaces. The training environment encompasses data poisoning attacks, which intervene in model training processes, and model poisoning attacks targeting the trained model itself. The model attack surface includes side-channel attacks exploiting model files, weights and runtime memory, as well as post-training model manipulation and code injection attacks. Input attack surfaces cover evasion attacks, prompt injection and prompt stealing, whilst output surfaces face model extraction attacks and training data gathering attempts.

AISI finds that attacks on AI systems generate impacts categorised within the confidentiality, integrity and availability framework. Confidentiality breaches include model leakage, training data leakage, application information leakage, input information leakage, reference information leakage and output information leakage. Integrity impacts encompass safeguard bypass, model malfunction, system compromise, interpretability malfunction and internal data corruption. Availability impacts comprise computational waste, task abandonment, failure of unlearning and unintended refusal.

The guidance document notes real-world consequences of such attacks. AISI observes that leaked training data from AI systems used in medical diagnosis violates privacy, whilst abnormal AI outputs in automated driving systems can cause traffic accidents. The institute states that as AI systems evolve, attacks exploiting their specific characteristics are increasingly reported, creating issues such as training data leaks and abnormal outputs. AISI emphasises that AI-specific security measures are essential, and the document outlines attacks and impacts reported in academic papers and other sources to facilitate examination of countermeasures. The taxonomy assumes conventional cybersecurity measures are already in place and focuses on attacks requiring special attention when systems incorporate AI components.

recyXAkdECTWMBhHe.orig_title: Lignes directrices pour la recherche scientifique et certification : les derniers documents adoptés par le CEPD
recyXAkdECTWMBhHe.gen_title: EDPB adopts guidelines on scientific research data processing and Europrivacy certification criteria
recyXAkdECTWMBhHe.summary:
On 22 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) adopted guidelines on the processing of personal data for scientific research purposes and issued opinions on updated Europrivacy certification criteria.

The EDPB's scientific research guidelines aim to provide clarity and legal certainty for researchers whilst facilitating GDPR compliance and protecting fundamental rights. The EDPB identifies six essential indicative factors to determine whether processing constitutes scientific research under GDPR: a methodical and systematic approach, respect for ethical standards, verifiability and transparency, autonomy and independence, research objectives, and the capacity to contribute to or innovatively apply existing scientific knowledge. When these six criteria are met, activities are presumed to constitute scientific research. Where they are not, the controller must justify and demonstrate why the activities qualify as scientific research under the GDPR.

The EDPB determines that further processing for scientific research purposes is presumed compatible with the initial purpose of personal data collection, meaning controllers need not perform the compatibility test required by GDPR (Art. 6 GDPR). However, controllers must ensure the legal basis for the initial processing is also appropriate for subsequent scientific research processing. The EDPB permits "broad consent" when research purposes are not fully known at collection, provided controllers implement ethical standards and additional safeguards. Controllers may alternatively request consent for distinct research projects as purposes become known (dynamic consent), or combine both approaches.

Regarding data subjects' rights, the EDPB clarifies that the right to erasure and the right to object may be limited in the scientific research context. The EDPB provides examples determining when exercising erasure rights would render impossible or severely compromise research objectives, and when controllers may reject opposition to processing, particularly where processing is necessary for executing a public interest task.

The EDPB addresses multi-entity involvement in scientific data processing, requiring controllers to assess and document responsibility allocation between entities. The guidance specifies when entities qualify as controllers, joint controllers, or processors. Finally, the EDPB explains how controllers may evaluate appropriate technical and organisational measures, including anonymisation and pseudonymisation, and provides examples of additional safeguards based on research activity risks: independent or ethical oversight, secure processing environments, privacy-enhancing technologies, publication protections, confidentiality commitments, and conditions governing further use.

The guidelines are subject to public consultation until 25 June 2026.
recyXAkdECTWMBhHe.body: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

reciHvP8DwGrggF8p.orig_title: „Wir geben unseren Sicherheitsbehörden die nötige Schlagkraft im digitalen Raum“
reciHvP8DwGrggF8p.gen_title: Germany mandates three-month IP address retention for law enforcement investigations
reciHvP8DwGrggF8p.summary:
On 22 April 2026, the Federal Ministry of the Interior announced that the German Federal Government has adopted a draft law requiring internet service providers to retain IP address data for three months to strengthen criminal investigations in the digital sphere.

The draft legislation addresses investigative gaps created by the current practice of internet access service providers retaining IP address records for only a few days, if at all. The Federal Ministry of the Interior states that by the time law enforcement agencies receive notice of criminal activity, submit requests, and conduct queries, such data are typically already deleted, preventing the identification and prosecution of offenders. The ministry emphasises that IP addresses are often the only trace left by perpetrators in digital crimes, including child sexual abuse material distribution, cyber-enabled fraud, and terrorism-related offences.

The Federal Ministry of the Interior notes that the three-month retention obligation is limited to data strictly necessary for identifying the connection holder. The retained information comprises: the technical mapping of an IP address to a connection, the time and duration of that assignment. The ministry confirms that no content data, communication history, or location data will be stored, and that personality or movement profiles cannot be created from the mandated data.

The draft law also expands law enforcement powers in two further respects. The Federal Ministry of the Interior states that where prosecution authorities have knowledge of a criminal offence but no specific suspect has yet been identified, they will be authorised to order telecommunications providers to preserve specified traffic data through a "preservation order" mechanism. Additionally, the legislation clarifies that law enforcement may conduct cell site location information (Funkzellenabfrage) inquiries in cases involving "offences of significant importance", including advance-fee fraud, organised fraud, serial burglary or theft, and aggravated assault. The Federal Ministry of the Interior indicates this restores an investigative tool that had become practically unavailable following a January 2024 Federal Court of Justice decision restricting its application to only the most severe offences.

recXZUgTGMz4qgZlo.gen_title: Romania Constitutional Court declares civil servant asset disclosure rules unconstitutional
recXZUgTGMz4qgZlo.summary:
Romania's Constitutional Court found that statutory provisions requiring civil servants to disclose the income of their spouse and dependent adult children, and requiring publication of such declarations on employer and integrity authority websites, violate the right to private and family life and protection of personal data under the Romanian Constitution and EU law.

On 22 April 2026, the EU Agency for Fundamental Rights published the case reference for Romania / Constitutional Court / no. 297, which concerned a challenge brought by D.L.C., a Romanian civil servant who was fined for failing to disclose her husband's income in a mandatory asset declaration. D.L.C. argued that her husband's salary was protected by confidentiality under his employment contract and that he was under no obligation to provide her with this information. She contended that the disclosure requirements violated her right to private and family life, and that in some family circumstances one spouse may lack access to or knowledge of the other's income.

The Constitutional Court found that the provisions of Law no. 176/2010 making disclosure of a spouse's and dependent children's income mandatory were unconstitutional. The Court also found that Article 12(6) of Law no. 176/2010, requiring publication of asset declarations on the websites of the employing institution and the National Integrity Agency, contravened the fundamental right to privacy protected by Article 26 of the Romanian Constitution (Art. 7 and 8, Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union).

In reaching this conclusion, the Constitutional Court referenced the Court of Justice of the European Union judgment in Case C-184/20 (1 August 2022), which established that European law, including the General Data Protection Regulation (Art. 6(1)(c) GDPR), read in light of the Charter of Fundamental Rights (Arts. 7, 8, 52(1)), precludes public disclosure of personal data relating to the spouse or close relatives of a declarant. The Constitutional Court determined that confidentiality and data protection rights are enshrined in EU treaties and the Charter, and that the GDPR, fully applicable since 2018, establishes a comprehensive legal framework for personal data processing across all EU Member States.

recfQrNQZ1QUXEEOu.orig_title: S-SDLC et DevSecOps en Europe : Enjeux actuels et perspectives d’avenir
recfQrNQZ1QUXEEOu.gen_title: ANSSI positions secure software development lifecycle and DevSecOps as strategic priorities for 2025–2027
recfQrNQZ1QUXEEOu.summary:
On 22 April 2026, the ANSSI (Agence nationale de la sécurité des systèmes d'information) published findings from a European-wide study examining secure software development lifecycle (S-SDLC) and DevSecOps practices, establishing these disciplines as strategic priorities in response to evolving regulatory requirements and persistent supply chain threats.

The ANSSI conducted the study to analyse market conditions, key actors and operational needs across public and private sector organisations against a backdrop of increasing attacks targeting software supply chains, growing complexity in continuous integration and continuous deployment (CI/CD) pipelines, and expanding regulatory obligations under the Cyber Resilience Act (CRA), NIS2 Directive and Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA). The authority identified S-SDLC and DevSecOps as essential responses to these threats and compliance demands for the 2025–2027 period.

Beyond diagnostic findings, the ANSSI's study proposes strategic orientations and industrial policy recommendations designed to sustain DevSecOps practices across organisations. The authority highlights an emerging consideration: the expansion of artificial intelligence within development environments. The ANSSI identifies MLSecOps—an approach integrating security and compliance requirements natively into machine learning models and their operational deployment—as a critical emerging requirement, particularly within CI/CD pipelines where AI integration is accelerating.

The study includes concrete deliverables supporting implementation, comprising technical reminders, generic risk analysis frameworks and roadmaps tailored to organisations of varying sizes and maturity levels. The ANSSI's positioning of S-SDLC and DevSecOps as non-negotiable components of organisational security strategy reflects the authority's assessment that software supply chain security is no longer discretionary but represents a fundamental strategic imperative in the contemporary threat environment.

recA6zbIgAiXCQCTN.orig_title: EDPB Letter to the European Commission regarding INGO registration requirements
recA6zbIgAiXCQCTN.gen_title: EDPB raises data protection concerns over Israel's NGO registration requirements with European Commission
recA6zbIgAiXCQCTN.summary:
On 20 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board addressed a letter to the European Commission expressing concerns regarding Israel's Guidelines for the Registration of Organisations and Issuance of Recommendations for the Foreign Employees, which impose registration requirements on non-governmental organisations based in the European Economic Area that provide humanitarian aid in the Palestinian Territories.

The EDPB states that the Israeli Guidelines require NGOs to submit substantial volumes of personal data belonging to their staff, including information concerning spouses and children. According to the letter, the Guidelines mandate submission of curricula vitae, copies of passports, certificates of good conduct, marital status information, and personal data relating to family members. Additionally, the EDPB notes that NGOs are required to provide lists of their donors together with undefined "background" information and the exact amounts of donations made.

The EDPB identifies multiple potential conflicts with the General Data Protection Regulation. The Board states that the Guidelines may create issues in relation to the principles of proportionality, necessity and transparency of processing activities. The EDPB further notes concerns regarding the requirement for a valid legal basis for such processing as imposed by Article 6 GDPR, and the ability of data subjects to exercise their rights under the Regulation. The Board emphasises that NGOs subject to the GDPR must comply with the rules and conditions set out in Chapter V of the GDPR governing international data transfers.

The EDPB references that Israel benefits from an adequacy decision under Article 45 GDPR, which places a specific monitoring duty on the European Commission. Consequently, the EDPB requests that the Commission clarify whether data flows from NGOs to Israeli competent authorities would fall within the scope of the existing adequacy decision, and whether the Commission has taken or plans to take action given that the implementation of the Israeli legislative change may undermine principles enshrined by the GDPR.

recJzAp0UpjfDtUjn.orig_title: RentTech platforms must stop unfair and excessive personal information collection, says Privacy Commissioner
recJzAp0UpjfDtUjn.gen_title: OAIC finds InspectRealEstate breached privacy principles through excessive rental data collection
recJzAp0UpjfDtUjn.summary:
On 1 April 2026, the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC) issued a determination finding that InspectRealEstate Pty Ltd, operating the 2Apply rental technology platform, collected excessive personal information and did so by unfair means, in breach of Australian Privacy Principles 3.2 and 3.5 (Privacy Act 1988 (Cth)).

The OAIC's year-long investigation concluded that 2Apply collected personal information not reasonably necessary for its functions or activities, including prospective renters' gender, student status, citizenship status, visa expiry dates and previous living history. The OAIC finds that InspectRealEstate also breached APP 3.5 by collecting personal information by unfair means. Privacy Commissioner Carly Kind noted the significant power imbalance in the rental property market, observing that renters often lack genuine choice when making applications and face housing precarity if they refuse to provide personal data.

In a first for the OAIC, the determination analysed the design and structure of the 2Apply form through the concept of online choice architecture. The Commissioner finds that the platform deployed "confirmshaming", which uses emotive language to pressurise users into providing information; biased framing, presenting choices in ways that emphasise supposed benefits or downsides; and bundled consent, requesting consent for multiple purposes in a single request. These practices unfairly pressured individuals into choices misaligned with their preferences.

The determination requires InspectRealEstate to cease collecting the specified personal information categories within 60 days and to engage an independent privacy reviewer at its own expense to assess whether other personal information collected remains reasonably necessary (APP 3.2), evaluate the online choice architecture of the 2Apply form (APP 3.5), and review data retention practices and policies (APP 11.2). Although the decision pertains specifically to InspectRealEstate, Commissioner Kind emphasised the need for other rental technology providers to adapt their practices consistently with the findings. The OAIC has provided the determination to real estate peak bodies to inform agents, property managers and landlords of the decision's implications for their own information collection practices.
recJzAp0UpjfDtUjn.body: Office of the Australian Information Commissioner

recGph7HjvbaKaCdN.orig_title: Vydali jsme ÄtvrtletnÃ pÅehled hrozeb NÃKIB - Q1 2026
recGph7HjvbaKaCdN.gen_title: Czech cyber authority reports 70 incidents in Q1 2026, highest annual figure
recGph7HjvbaKaCdN.summary:
On 22 April 2026, the Czech National Cyber and Information Security Office (NÚKIB) published its quarterly threat overview for Q1 2026, documenting over 70 cybersecurity incidents, the highest number recorded in the preceding twelve months.

NÚKIB identified a marked increase in cyber incidents driven primarily by a January wave of distributed denial-of-service attacks conducted by pro-Russian hacktivist groups and a relatively high number of intrusion incidents. The authority recorded a total of seven significant incidents during the quarter, the majority affecting state institutions, with all remaining incidents classified as less significant. Alongside the overall rise in incident volume, NÚKIB notes that the severity of incidents handled also increased moderately.

The authority determined that no ransomware attacks targeted entities operating under the higher obligations regime established under the new cybersecurity law during the quarter. Ransomware incidents did, however, affect providers of regulated services operating under the lower obligations regime, with incident response coordinated through the National CERT (CSIRT.CZ). NÚKIB also identified heightened activity by pro-Russian hacktivist actors in January, manifested in DDoS attacks and increased attacker interest in VNC interface attacks against programmable logic controller devices secured with weak or absent passwords, alongside exploitation of other internet-exposed services. The authority notes these activities did not produce pronounced impact on Czech entities.

NÚKIB issued a warning in March concerning inadequately secured VNC services exposed to the internet, linked to a marked increase in devices compromised by the REDHEBERG botnet across Czech territory. At the time of the quarterly threat overview publication, the authority documented over 3,200 compromised devices within the Czech Republic. The threat overview also encompasses cyber threats without direct bearing on Czech security but with potential direct or indirect effects on Czech cybersecurity, including a global cyber espionage campaign conducted via the Signal application, extensive exploitation of the advanced iPhone compromise tool designated Coruna, and cyber espionage spear-phishing campaigns against European states leveraging a vulnerability in Microsoft Office software.

Two international cybersecurity events occurred in March: the NATO Cyber Champions Summit, attended by senior representatives from NATO allied countries and key partners from the Indo-Pacific region to strengthen international cooperation in cyber security and defence, and Prague Cyber Security Conference 2026, which confirmed its role as a significant European platform for strategic debate on cybersecurity.

recslJsXCWBHebASL.orig_title: FCA announces second cohort for AI Live Testing
recslJsXCWBHebASL.gen_title: FCA selects eight firms for second cohort of AI Live Testing programme
recslJsXCWBHebASL.summary:
On 21 April 2026, the Financial Conduct Authority announced the selection of eight firms for the second cohort of its AI Live Testing initiative, a regulatory sandbox programme designed to enable safe and responsible deployment of artificial intelligence applications in UK financial services.

The FCA selected Aereve, Coadjute, Barclays, Experian, Go-Cardless, Lloyds Banking Group (Scottish Widows), UBS and Palindrome to participate in the programme. The authority is working with Advai, a London-based AI assurance specialist, to deliver technical support and monitoring throughout the testing period. Jessica Rusu, the FCA's chief data, information and intelligence officer, stated that the initiative reflects the regulator's commitment to supporting responsible AI deployment while maintaining consumer and market protection.

The FCA notes that applications submitted by the second cohort reflect diverse use cases across emerging AI technologies, including agentic AI, small language models and neurosymbolic AI. Participating firms are testing both consumer-facing and business-to-business applications, encompassing AI-enabled investment support, credit score insights, agentic payments, anti-money laundering detection and Know Your Customer processes. The authority finds that this diversity demonstrates the fast-evolving nature of AI deployment across UK financial services.

Applications for the second cohort opened in January 2026, with firms commencing testing in April. The FCA states that testing will conclude by the end of 2026, with an evaluation report to be published in the first quarter of 2027. The authority also intends to publish a good and poor practice report for AI in financial services later in 2026 to support industry adoption. The FCA's approach reflects its commitment to avoiding additional regulations for AI by applying existing regulatory frameworks, as set out in its response to the Prime Minister's letter dated 16 January 2025. The first cohort, announced previously, comprised Gain Credit, Homeprotect, NatWest, Monzo, Santander, Scottish Widows and Snorkl.

rec8gGAVNt4ZzlRCx.orig_title: The publication of health data in the media constitutes a violation of Law No. 124/2024 “On the Protection of Personal Data”
rec8gGAVNt4ZzlRCx.gen_title: Albanian IDP warns that media publication of health data violates personal data law
rec8gGAVNt4ZzlRCx.summary:
On 21 April 2026, the Information and Data Protection Commissioner's Office of Albania issued a public statement determining that the publication and circulation in media and social networks of health information concerning identifiable individuals in Gjirokastër Municipality constitutes a violation of Law No. 124/2024 "On the Protection of Personal Data".

The IDP finds that health-related data constitute special categories of personal data under Article 9 of Law No. 124/2024, the processing of which is prohibited in principle and permitted only in limited, legally defined cases. The authority determines that publication of such data in the absence of a legal basis and clear, concrete, overriding public interest constitutes unlawful processing contrary to the legislation in force. The IDP emphasises that the exercise of freedom of expression and information cannot infringe the right to respect for private life and protection of personal data, as guaranteed by Article 35 of the Albanian Constitution, Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights, and Law No. 124/2024.

The IDP notes that such publications, particularly when unconfirmed, inaccurate or unnecessary for the public interest, seriously harm the dignity and reputation of individuals and can have direct consequences in their personal and professional lives. The authority finds that treatment of health data requires special care and increased responsibility in a public space where information circulates rapidly. The IDP determines that the publications are contrary to Article 43 of Law No. 124/2024, Instruction No. 02/2025 "On the protection of personal health data", and Instruction No. 07/2025 "On the protection of personal data in the written, electronic and/or audiovisual media", which require verification of information and prohibit publication of sensitive data without legal basis and without clear, proportionate and justified public interest.

The IDP calls on media organisations, portals and social network users to exercise increased caution in handling health data, verify information accuracy, and avoid dissemination and publication of such data. The authority requires immediate deletion of all content related to this case from all platforms. The IDP states that failure to comply constitutes a violation of Law No. 124/2024 and may result in the application of administrative measures and relevant sanctions.

recYEPWScaIk8tc3Y.orig_title: AI is changing the economics of vulnerability discovery
recYEPWScaIk8tc3Y.gen_title: CERT-EU identifies AI-driven shift in vulnerability discovery economics and exploitation timelines
recYEPWScaIk8tc3Y.summary:
On 21 April 2026, CERT-EU published analysis of how artificial intelligence is fundamentally altering the pace and economics of vulnerability discovery and exploitation across Union entities, with mean time to exploit newly disclosed vulnerabilities now occurring an estimated seven days before patches are released.

CERT-EU notes that advanced AI models, including Anthropic's Claude Mythos Preview, represent a generational jump in autonomous exploit development capability. The authority observes that when Anthropic benchmarked Claude Mythos Preview against vulnerabilities in Firefox 147's JavaScript engine, the model developed working shell exploits in 181 instances out of several hundred attempts, compared to just twice for its predecessor Claude Opus 4.6. CERT-EU states that open-weight models are narrowing the capability gap to frontier systems, meaning these capabilities will progressively become accessible to a wider range of actors, including malicious ones. According to Google's M-Trends 2026 report, the mean time to exploit newly disclosed vulnerabilities has dropped to an estimated negative seven days, compared to 63 days in 2018, breaking the traditional patch cycle.

CERT-EU finds that exploitation of vulnerabilities in internet-facing software remains the highest-impact initial access vector against Union entities for the second consecutive year, as documented in its Threat Landscape Report 2025. The authority notes that in April 2026, HackerOne suspended new submissions to its Internet Bug Bounty programme after AI-generated vulnerability reports overwhelmed both triage and remediation resources, whilst the cURL project shut down its bug bounty programme in January 2026 for similar reasons. CERT-EU states that the same AI capabilities strengthening attackers can also strengthen defences, with defenders who adopt AI-augmented workflows systematically gaining real ground. The authority indicates it has built and tested an internal AI-powered penetration testing pipeline and is integrating AI-assisted analysis across its services to support Union entities under Regulation (EU, Euratom) 2023/2841. CERT-EU recommends eight concrete actions: reducing attack surface, maintaining rigorous hygiene, adopting AI-powered security testing responsibly, strengthening detection and response, accelerating zero-trust adoption, building cross-functional teams, aligning with emerging frameworks, and iterating continuously.

recGSfA9sujBTqsdB.orig_title: Opinion on the Proposal for a Regulation establishing a common system for the return of third-country nationals staying illegally in the EU
recGSfA9sujBTqsdB.gen_title: EDPS publishes Opinion on EU return regulation for illegally staying third-country nationals
recGSfA9sujBTqsdB.summary:
On 28 May 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) published Opinion 9/2025 on the European Commission's Proposal for a Regulation establishing a common system for the return of third-country nationals staying illegally in the Union, which seeks to repeal Directive 2008/115/EC, Council Directive 2001/40/EC and Council Decision 2004/191/EC.

The EDPS acknowledges the need for more effective enforcement of EU and national migration and asylum laws. However, the EDPS finds that data protection constitutes one of the last lines of defence for vulnerable individuals, including migrants and asylum seekers approaching EU external borders. The EDPS considers that the Proposal's comprehensive approach must be based on full respect for fundamental rights, including the right to data protection and privacy of persons seeking international protection and other migrants.

The EDPS recommends that an in-depth fundamental rights impact assessment be carried out in light of the Proposal's impact on privacy and personal data protection rights. The EDPS further recommends alignment of information about data subject rights under applicable Union data protection law, to be provided pursuant to Article 24 of the Proposal, with relevant provisions of already-adopted legal acts in migration and asylum.

Regarding access to effective remedy, the EDPS stresses that limitation of information about the reasons in fact of the return decision to the essence of the grounds should not be applied as a rule but only in exceptional cases where strictly necessary, such as where disclosure would contradict State security interests. The EDPS highlights the need for additional safeguards where data relating to criminal convictions of third-country nationals is transferred to third countries. The EDPS also recommends further specification of the conditions under which personal data of children may be transferred to the third country of return. The EDPS notes that this Opinion does not preclude future additional comments or recommendations, particularly if further issues are identified or new information becomes available.

recaK9MYAVd2pMe7B.orig_title: EDPS supports targeted VAT data access to fight fraud at EU level but warns against blurring administrative and criminal boundaries
recaK9MYAVd2pMe7B.gen_title: EDPS backs targeted VAT data access for EPPO and OLAF but cautions against blurring administrative and criminal law boundaries
recaK9MYAVd2pMe7B.summary:
On 8 January 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor issued its Opinion on the European Commission's Proposal to amend Regulation (EU) No 904/2010, which would grant the European Public Prosecutor's Office and the European Anti-Fraud Office direct and centralised access to VAT information to combat intra-Community value added tax fraud at EU level.

The EDPS recognises the urgency of combatting large-scale cross-border VAT fraud, particularly Missing Trader Intra-Community schemes, which the authority finds continue to cause significant losses to public finances across the Union. The EDPS acknowledges that specific and limited direct access to certain VAT information by the EPPO and OLAF can be necessary to ensure effective investigations and enforcement of VAT rules.

However, the EDPS underlines that the Proposal sits at a sensitive crossroads between administrative VAT cooperation and criminal law enforcement. The authority emphasises that processing personal data for administrative purposes and for criminal enforcement purposes follows distinct legal regimes and principles, and this separation must be carefully reflected in the legal framework. The EDPS considers that the Proposal should more clearly explain and underline the exceptional nature of providing direct access to administrative databases for law enforcement purposes. The authority states that further clarification is warranted to avoid any risk that the Proposal could be misconstrued or later relied upon as precedent for more general application.

The EDPS further emphasises that robust legal, technical and organisational safeguards are indispensable to compliance with EU data protection law principles of purpose limitation, data minimisation and accountability (Regulation (EU) 2018/1725). The authority notes that whilst some technical and organisational details may be specified through implementing acts, core parameters must already be defined in the basic legislative act. Wojciech Wiewiórowski, Supervisor, stated that exceptional access to administrative databases for law enforcement purposes must remain precisely that, and must be clearly circumscribed and carefully safeguarded.

recyx9OmCBxNEVAe9.orig_title: Extension of interim rules to combat child sexual abuse online must address shortcomings and prevent indiscriminate scanning
recyx9OmCBxNEVAe9.gen_title: EDPS issues Opinion 7/2026 on extension of interim child sexual abuse online rules
recyx9OmCBxNEVAe9.summary:
On 17 February 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) issued Opinion 7/2026 on the European Commission's Proposal to extend Regulation (EU) 2021/1232, the interim rules permitting data processing to combat child sexual abuse material (CSAM) online, from its scheduled expiry on 3 April 2026 until 3 April 2028.

The EDPS recognises that combatting child sexual abuse is an objective of general interest acknowledged by the Union. The interim framework allows providers to undertake voluntary measures against CSAM whilst inter-institutional negotiations continue on a longer-term legal framework. However, the EDPS identifies clear shortcomings in the current Regulation (EU) 2021/1232 that the extension presents an opportunity to address.

The EDPS has provided specific recommendations to ensure greater legal certainty, particularly regarding the lawfulness of processing under Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR). The authority emphasises the necessity for effective safeguards against general and indiscriminate scanning, consistent with the principles of necessity and proportionality (Art. 5 GDPR). Wojciech Wiewiórowski, EDPS Supervisor, stated that temporary measures must not circumvent fundamental rights, and that the extension offers the appropriate moment to address concerns raised during discussions on a long-term regulation to prevent indiscriminate scanning and establish clear legal bases for personal data processing.

The EDPS references its previous Opinions from 2020 and 2024, as well as a 2022 Joint Opinion issued with the European Data Protection Board (EDPB), which consistently emphasise that any detection solution must be targeted and carefully balance the privacy rights of all users. The EDPS operates under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 and serves as the independent supervisory authority monitoring personal data processing by EU institutions and bodies, advising on relevant policies and legislation affecting privacy.

rec3YtkXWymPed6k6.orig_title: NÃKIB zveÅejnil PrÅ¯vodce, kterÃ½ usnadnÃ zÃ¡pis sluÅ¾eb cloud computingu do katalogu
rec3YtkXWymPed6k6.gen_title: Czech National Cybersecurity Authority publishes cloud computing catalogue registration guide
rec3YtkXWymPed6k6.summary:
The National Cybersecurity Authority of the Czech Republic (NÚKIB) has published a guidance document to assist cloud computing service providers in meeting registration requirements for the national cloud computing catalogue under Decree No. 505/2025 Coll.

On 20 April 2026, NÚKIB released the guide entitled "Guide to Documenting Requirements for Cloud Computing Service Registration", designed to help providers navigate the technical and security requirements established by Decree No. 505/2025 Coll., which entered into force on 1 January 2026. The guidance aims to improve clarity around registration requirements and enable providers to submit complete and high-quality applications on first submission.

NÚKIB states that the guide familiarises providers with fundamental concepts, the legal framework, and general principles set out in the decree. The document outlines basic requirements for cloud computing service registration applications and identifies common deficiencies that may prolong or complicate the registration process. NÚKIB notes that the decree establishes the technical and security requirements necessary for cloud computing services to be recorded in the cloud computing catalogue.

The guidance includes detailed requirements applicable to different security levels within the cloud computing framework. NÚKIB has created separate supporting documents for each security level, enabling providers to obtain clear guidance when preparing documentation corresponding to the specific security level of their offered service. The authority states that this structured approach provides providers with targeted support during the documentation preparation phase.

Providers can access the guide and supporting documents for individual security levels through NÚKIB's website at the regulatory support materials section. The publication represents NÚKIB's effort to standardise the registration process and reduce administrative friction for cloud service providers seeking catalogue inclusion.

rec2qarXKTxWLkPvb.orig_title: Projekt na podporu implementace NIS2 financovanÃ½ EU oficiÃ¡lnÄ zahÃ¡jen
rec2qarXKTxWLkPvb.gen_title: Czech Republic launches EU-funded NIS2 implementation support project
rec2qarXKTxWLkPvb.summary:
On 15 April 2026, the Czech National Cybersecurity Authority (NÚKIB) officially launched a project to support implementation of the NIS2 Directive, financed by the European Union through the Technical Support Instrument (TSI) managed by the Task Force for Reform and Investments (SG REFORM) under the European Commission.

The project is being delivered by the Estonian e-Governance Academy (eGA) in cooperation with the European Commission's SG REFORM unit. NÚKIB states the project aims to strengthen institutional and administrative capacity to enable the authority to adapt its organisational structures and service delivery in response to the significantly expanded scope of obligations under the NIS2 Directive (Directive on the security of network and information systems).

NÚKIB notes that NIS2 implementation in the Czech Republic has increased the number of regulated entities from approximately 400 to around 6,000, representing a roughly fifteen-fold expansion. The authority states the directive now covers nearly all economic sectors, including healthcare and financial services, which depend on secure and resilient digital infrastructure. Adam Kučínský, director of the regulatory department at NÚKIB, states that with this significant increase in regulated entities, it is essential that NÚKIB has appropriate organisational capacity and operational tools to conduct oversight effectively across this expanded scope of responsibility.

Lukáš Kintr, director of NÚKIB, notes that the Czech Republic is already established as one of Europe's strongest cybersecurity jurisdictions, with solid institutional foundations for cyber governance, with NÚKIB playing a key role in legislation development, incident response, monitoring cyber threats and international cooperation. The project will contribute to further strengthening the national cybersecurity system in preparation for full NIS2 implementation.

The project focuses on practical outputs including sectoral analysis, assessment of current cyber threats, supply chain security support, change management support and capacity development activities. Taimar Peterkop, senior expert and project lead at eGA, states the goal is to help translate NIS2 requirements into functioning organisational processes, strengthen internal capacity and enable effective cooperation with regulated entities, allowing NÚKIB to manage the expanded scope and strengthen the Czech Republic's long-term cyber resilience.

recSGOg11JEueyJ7x.orig_title: EDPS strengthens DPO role: new guidance and binding rules to protect DPO independence across EU institutions
recSGOg11JEueyJ7x.gen_title: EDPS adopts binding rules on DPO dismissal consent and supervisory guidance across EU institutions
recSGOg11JEueyJ7x.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has issued supervisory guidance and binding rules to strengthen data protection officer independence and protection across all EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies, setting out procedural requirements for prior consent to DPO dismissal.

On 18 December 2025, the EDPS issued Supervisory Guidance clarifying the role, position and tasks of data protection officers within EU institutions under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. The EDPS states that the Guidance provides practical direction on DPO designation, institutional positioning, independence guarantees and assigned responsibilities. The authority notes that all EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies are required by EU law to appoint a DPO.

Building on this Guidance, the EDPS adopted Decision 01/2026 on 16 January 2026, establishing binding Rules governing prior consent requirements for DPO dismissal (OJ L226/199, 29.01.2026). The EDPS determines that these Rules create a uniform procedural framework that EU institutions must follow when seeking prior EDPS consent before dismissing a DPO before the end of their designation term. The authority finds that the Rules and Guidance together clarify the EDPS's expectations and applicable procedural requirements, and both documents apply immediately.

The EDPS states that both instruments are to be reflected in the internal practices and decision-making of all EU institutions regarding DPO position and protection. Wojciech Wiewiórowski, EDPS Supervisor, stated that DPOs have served as a cornerstone of data protection governance within EU institutions since 2002, and that the updated Guidance and Rules aim to strengthen the DPO role by ensuring independent, consistent and effective application of EU data protection law. The authority characterises the measures as reinforcing the DPO's position as an internal safeguard for personal data protection.

recNXFTihZ6NzLuuV.gen_title: EDPB adopts guidelines on scientific research data processing and approves first European data protection seal
recNXFTihZ6NzLuuV.summary:
The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) has adopted guidelines clarifying the processing of personal data for scientific research purposes, established a team to accelerate finalisation of anonymisation guidelines, and approved the first European data protection seal under Article 42(5) GDPR.

On 21 April 2026, the EDPB adopted the guidelines at its plenary session to provide researchers with clarity on compliance with GDPR whilst protecting individuals' fundamental rights. The EDPB states that scientific research depends on processing personal data and has contributed to significant breakthroughs benefiting society. The guidelines aim to facilitate innovative research by helping researchers navigate GDPR requirements.

The EDPB identifies six key orientating factors to determine whether processing constitutes scientific research: methodical and systematic approach; compliance with ethical standards; verifiability and transparency; autonomy and independence; research objectives; and potential to contribute to existing scientific knowledge or apply existing knowledge in new ways. The Board notes that if research activities meet these six factors, they may be considered scientific research. Controllers must otherwise justify and demonstrate why activities should be treated as scientific research under GDPR. Further processing for scientific research is deemed compatible with the original purpose of data collection, meaning controllers are not required to perform a purpose compatibility test under GDPR. However, the legal basis for initial processing must remain appropriate for further scientific research processing. Controllers may rely on broad consent where research purposes are unknown at collection, provided they implement additional safeguards and comply with ethical standards. Dynamic consent for individual research projects as purposes become known is also permissible, as is a combination of both approaches. The EDPB clarifies individuals' rights, including rights to erasure and objection, which may be restricted if personal data processing is necessary for scientific research purposes. The Board provides examples of when erasure would likely prevent or seriously jeopardise research objectives and when controllers may reject objections where processing is necessary for public interest tasks. The guidelines address multi-entity scenarios, requiring assessment and documentation of how responsibilities are distributed between data controllers, joint controllers and processors.
