The EDPB explains how controllers may assess appropriate technical and organisational safeguards including anonymisation or pseudonymisation, with additional examples of measures such as independent oversight, secure processing environments and privacy-enhancing technologies. The guidelines will be subject to public consultation until 25 June 2026. The EDPB also established a specialist sprint team to complete anonymisation guidelines work by summer 2026 and approved updated Europrivacy certification criteria as a European data protection seal under Article 42(5) GDPR, representing the first such seal approval.

recvHizAUwmFKXCWE.orig_title: EDPB and EDPS support harmonisation of clinical trials under European Biotech Act, but call for specific safeguards for sensitive health data
recvHizAUwmFKXCWE.gen_title: EDPB and EDPS call for data protection safeguards in European Biotech Act clinical trials framework
recvHizAUwmFKXCWE.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor have adopted a joint opinion on the European Commission's proposal for a European Biotech Act, supporting harmonisation of clinical trials regulation whilst recommending specific protections for sensitive health data processing.

On 12 March 2026, the EDPB and EDPS issued their joint opinion on the proposed Biotech Act, which aims to strengthen Europe's biotechnology and biomanufacturing sectors by streamlining regulatory frameworks and updating clinical trials rules. The authorities welcome the proposal's objective to foster EU competitiveness and address fragmentation in the application of the Clinical Trials Regulation. The EDPB and EDPS particularly support the establishment of a single legal basis for personal data processing by sponsors and investigators, finding this will improve legal clarity across the Union.

However, the EDPB and EDPS emphasise that the sensitivity of health and genetic data processed in clinical trials demands a high standard of protection. They recommend clarifying whether actors involved in funding and conducting trials act as sole or joint data controllers to ensure clear allocation of responsibilities. The authorities find that the mandatory 25-year minimum retention period should expressly apply only to the clinical trial master file rather than all personal data processed during a trial.

The EDPB and EDPS further recommend that where the Biotech Act provides a legal basis for further processing of trial data by the same controller for other trials or scientific research, it must clearly define the purposes and establish specific safeguards for such processing. The authorities call for coherence with the AI Act to ensure sponsors' obligations complement existing AI requirements. The EDPB and EDPS determine that the CTR should explicitly require pseudonymisation whenever directly identifiable personal data is unnecessary. Regarding regulatory sandboxes, the authorities find that implementing acts must provide legal bases for personal data processing and derogations under Article 9(2) GDPR for sensitive data processing, whilst other sandboxes must remain based on GDPR legal bases.

recpa9NHB5nrTPEoa.orig_title: EDPB and EDPS support strengthening EU’s cybersecurity and easing compliance while protecting individuals’ personal data
recpa9NHB5nrTPEoa.gen_title: EDPB and EDPS adopt joint opinion supporting EU cybersecurity proposals with data protection safeguards
recpa9NHB5nrTPEoa.summary:
The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) and the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) have adopted a joint opinion on the European Commission's proposal for a Cybersecurity Act 2 (CSA2) and proposed amendments to the Network and Information Security 2 (NIS2) Directive, issued on 19 March 2026 in response to a formal consultation request dated 21 January 2026 under Article 42(2) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

The EDPB and EDPS support the general objective to strengthen the role of the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) and to facilitate uptake of cybersecurity certification, as well as measures to address risks to ICT supply chains. The authorities welcome clarification on how ENISA provides support to different stakeholders and specifically support the provision that ENISA's advice would be issued upon prior request from the EDPB, ensuring clear coordination and division of responsibilities. The EDPB and EDPS recommend adding the EDPS as a possible requestor of advice from ENISA.

The authorities determine that any decisions adopted by ENISA's Management Board regarding application of the EU Data Protection Regulation must be limited to very technical and practical details related to personal data processing, with prior consultation required from the EDPS before adoption. The EDPB and EDPS also recommend an explicit reference to the EDPS as an EU body with which ENISA should cooperate.

Regarding certification schemes, the EDPB and EDPS note that the scope of the European Cybersecurity Certification Framework and its relationship with GDPR certification requires further clarification. ENISA is required to consult with the EDPB before adopting certification schemes relating to security of personal data processing. Certification schemes for products, services and processes used in data processing operations should account for security controls that demonstrate fulfilment of GDPR requirements where possible.

The authorities recommend that the European Cybersecurity Skills Framework extend beyond cybersecurity professionals to include general workforce profiles. In line with a recent joint opinion on the Digital Omnibus Regulation, the EDPB and EDPS support establishment of a single-entry point for personal data breach notifications to reduce administrative burden without diminishing individual protection. The authorities welcome designation of European Digital Identity Wallets and European Business Wallets providers as essential entities under the NIS2 amendments.

recW3nz98cmwwqnZC.orig_title: EDPB and EDPS support streamlining AI Act implementation but call for stronger safeguards to protect fundamental rights
recW3nz98cmwwqnZC.gen_title: EDPB and EDPS adopt joint opinion on Digital Omnibus AI proposal, backing simplification with conditions on fundamental rights protection
recW3nz98cmwwqnZC.summary:
On 21 January 2026, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) and the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) adopted a joint opinion on the European Commission's Digital Omnibus on AI proposal, which seeks to streamline implementation of harmonised rules under the AI Act. The EDPB and EDPS support administrative simplification to address practical implementation challenges but find that certain proposed changes could undermine protection of individuals' fundamental rights and personal data.

The EDPB and EDPS support the creation of EU-level regulatory sandboxes to promote innovation and reduce burdens on organisations, particularly small and medium-sized enterprises. However, the authorities find that Data Protection Authorities (DPAs) must retain a central supervisory role in oversight of personal data processing. The EDPB and EDPS recommend direct involvement of competent DPAs in supervision of data processing within sandboxes and advise that the EDPB should hold observer status at the European Artificial Intelligence Board to ensure consistency.

On the proposal to expand processing of special categories of personal data for bias detection and correction, the EDPB and EDPS recommend restricting such use to circumscribed situations where the risk of adverse effects from bias is deemed sufficiently serious. The authorities advise against deletion of the registration obligation for high-risk AI systems, finding that removal would significantly undermine accountability and create incentives for providers to claim exemptions to avoid scrutiny.

The EDPB and EDPS support streamlining cooperation between fundamental rights authorities and market surveillance authorities but recommend clarifying that market surveillance authorities function as administrative points of contact without affecting DPA independence and powers. The authorities express concern regarding proposed postponement of core provisions for high-risk AI systems and invite co-legislators to consider maintaining the original timeline for transparency requirements given the rapid evolution of the AI landscape. The EDPB and EDPS also recommend that any new obligation on the Commission or Member States to foster AI literacy should complement, not replace, responsibilities of organisations developing and using AI systems.

recZcpKx38nspTl2D.orig_title: Sharing of personal data with the United States must be accompanied by comprehensive and effective safeguards
recZcpKx38nspTl2D.gen_title: EDPS issues opinion requiring comprehensive safeguards for EU-US personal data sharing on border security
recZcpKx38nspTl2D.summary:
On 17 September 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) issued an Opinion on the negotiating mandate for a framework agreement between the European Union and the United States on the exchange of information for security screenings and identity verifications. The proposed agreement would establish the legal structure and conditions for sharing information between competent authorities of EU Member States and the United States, with individual Member States empowered to conclude bilateral agreements for data exchange from their national systems. This framework is linked to the United States requirement for partner countries to conclude an 'Enhanced Border Security Partnership' with the U.S. Department of Homeland Security as a condition of participation in the U.S. Visa Waiver Program.

The EDPS determined that whilst border security is a legitimate aim, the interference with fundamental rights to data protection and privacy resulting from sharing personal data with a third country for border and immigration control purposes is comparable to interference caused by law enforcement data exchanges. Accordingly, the EDPS finds that comprehensive and effective safeguards regarding protection of personal data and other fundamental rights and freedoms of concerned individuals, regardless of nationality and place of residence, are of utmost importance.

The EDPS notes that the proposed framework agreement would establish an important precedent as the first EU agreement entailing large-scale sharing of personal data, including biometric data such as fingerprints, for border and immigration control by a third country. The authority stresses the need to ensure that envisaged processing of personal data does not exceed the limits of what is strictly necessary and proportionate. The EDPS makes specific recommendations to define the personal and material scope of data sharing as narrowly as possible and requires strict exclusion of any direct or indirect sharing and transfer of data from EU large-scale IT systems in the area of justice and home affairs, particularly those related to migration and asylum. Additionally, the EDPS recommends establishing clear accountability mechanisms requiring specific justification for each query, transparency of processing with corresponding information obligations for competent US and EU authorities, and availability of judicial redress in the United States regardless of citizenship (Regulation (EU) 2018/1725).

recIOqux0NdZ7mBA7.orig_title: Targeted modifications of the GDPR: EDPB & EDPS welcome simplification of record keeping obligations and request further clarifications
recIOqux0NdZ7mBA7.gen_title: EDPB and EDPS support GDPR record-keeping simplification for enterprises up to 750 employees
recIOqux0NdZ7mBA7.summary:
On 9 July 2025, the European Data Protection Board and European Data Protection Supervisor issued a joint opinion welcoming the European Commission's Proposal for a Regulation amending the GDPR as part of the fourth simplification Omnibus, whilst requesting further clarifications on threshold definitions and scope.

The EDPB and EDPS support the Proposal's general objective to reduce administrative burden for small and medium-sized enterprises and small mid-cap companies. The Proposal modifies Art. 30(5) GDPR to expand a derogation from record-keeping obligations to enterprises or organisations employing fewer than 750 people, up from the current 250-employee threshold, provided the processing does not create high risk to individuals' rights and freedoms as defined in Art. 35 GDPR.

The authorities welcome that the proposed modifications are targeted and limited in nature, and do not affect core GDPR principles and other obligations. The EDPS stated the authorities support reducing administrative burden for SMEs and small mid-cap companies as long as this does not lower protection of individuals' fundamental rights, particularly privacy and personal data protection. The EDPB Chair noted that the current derogation did not always achieve its goal in practice, and that the simplification will offer greater flexibility to these organisations in choosing appropriate compliance methods, whilst recognising that record-keeping remains a useful tool for demonstrating compliance with other duties such as transparency obligations and enabling data subject rights.

However, the EDPB and EDPS identified concerns requiring clarification from co-legislators. They request justification for the 750-employee threshold choice over the initially considered 500-employee threshold, particularly given impacts on other policy areas. The authorities also recommend that the exemption in Art. 30(5) should reference the newly introduced definitions of SME and SMC in Art. 4 GDPR, which include financial criteria alongside employee numbers, to ensure the derogation benefits its intended beneficiaries. Additionally, the EDPB and EDPS ask for clarification that the term 'organisation' falling within the proposed derogation does not include public authorities and bodies.

recl7pcdhKCYy6M2W.orig_title: Digital Omnibus: EDPB and EDPS support simplification and competitiveness while raising key concerns
recl7pcdhKCYy6M2W.gen_title: EDPB and EDPS adopt joint opinion on Digital Omnibus Regulation, opposing changes to personal data definition
recl7pcdhKCYy6M2W.summary:
On 10 February 2026, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) and the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) adopted a joint opinion on the Digital Omnibus Regulation proposal, which seeks to simplify the EU's digital regulatory framework and reduce administrative burden on organisations while assessing impacts on data protection and fundamental rights.

The EDPB and EDPS strongly oppose proposed changes to the definition of personal data under the GDPR and EUDPR, finding that these amendments go far beyond targeted technical modifications and would significantly narrow the concept of personal data in a manner inconsistent with Court of Justice of the European Union jurisprudence. The authorities urge co-legislators not to adopt these changes, noting that they would adversely affect individuals' protection levels and create legal uncertainty. The EDPB and EDPS further determine that the European Commission should not be entrusted to decide by implementing act what constitutes personal data following pseudonymisation, as this directly affects the scope of EU data protection law application.

The authorities welcome several proposed amendments. The EDPB and EDPS support increasing the risk threshold triggering data breach notification obligations to competent data protection authorities and extending notification deadlines, finding this would substantially reduce administrative burden without diminishing personal data protection. The authorities favour proposed common templates and lists for data breaches and data protection impact assessments. The EDPB and EDPS also endorse a new derogation permitting special category data processing for biometric authentication where verification means remain under individual sole control, and support harmonisation of the scientific research definition, which the authorities determine enhances legal certainty.

Regarding proposed amendments requiring refinement, the EDPB and EDPS recommend improvements to the derogation for incidental and residual special category data processing in AI system development, including clarified scope and enhanced safeguards throughout operational lifecycles. On automated decision-making provisions, the authorities find that proposed changes require clarification to ensure legal soundness. The EDPB and EDPS support the objective of addressing consent fatigue through automated and machine-readable indications of individual processing choices, determining that technical means can simplify controller compliance and support individual effectiveness in online choice-making.

recc27crzFjsKVJ7c.orig_title: Migration management: data protection is one of the last lines of defence for vulnerable individuals
recc27crzFjsKVJ7c.gen_title: EDPS issues opinion on EU returns regulation, recommends data protection safeguards for migrants
recc27crzFjsKVJ7c.summary:
On 28 May 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) published an Opinion on the Proposal for a Regulation establishing a common system for the return of third-country nationals staying illegally in the EU. The EDPS considers that an in-depth fundamental rights impact assessment must be carried out to identify and mitigate potential risks arising from the proposal's impact on individuals' privacy and personal data protection rights.

The EDPS makes three categories of recommendation. First, the authority recommends outlining specifically the legal possibility to limit disclosure of information about the reasons justifying a return decision, with such limitations applied only in exceptional cases where strictly necessary, such as where disclosure would be contrary to state security interests. Second, the EDPS recommends alignment of the proposal with applicable EU data protection legislation and other legal acts linked to the Pact on Migration and Asylum, including ensuring that third-country nationals subject to a return procedure receive information about their rights as data subjects (Regulation (EU) 2018/1725).

Third, the EDPS highlights the need for additional safeguards in case of transfers of personal data relating to criminal convictions of third-country nationals. The authority states that such transfers must be subject to a strict necessity test and must not lead to execution of a death penalty or any form of cruel and inhuman treatment. The EDPS further recommends that the conditions under which personal data of children may be transferred to the third country of return be specified, following a thorough assessment that the transfer is in the minors' best interest and will not endanger their well-being.

Wojciech Wiewiórowski, EDPS, stated that data protection, as a fundamental right listed in the Charter, constitutes one of the last lines of defence for vulnerable individuals, such as migrants and asylum seekers approaching EU external borders. The EDPS acknowledged the need for more effective enforcement of existing migration and asylum laws whilst emphasising that any proposed EU-wide approach must be based on full respect for fundamental rights, including data protection and privacy.

recVN8G3lNYjXHvK7.orig_title: International cooperation to fight crime should respect EU fundamental rights guarantees
recVN8G3lNYjXHvK7.gen_title: EDPS issues opinion on UN cybercrime convention, emphasising data protection safeguards
recVN8G3lNYjXHvK7.summary:
On 4 September 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) issued an Opinion on two proposals to authorise the European Union's signing and conclusion of the United Nations Convention against Cybercrime. The Convention establishes common rules to strengthen international cooperation in preventing and combating cybercrime, as well as in the collection of electronic evidence for criminal investigations and proceedings.

The EDPS recognised that investigating and prosecuting cybercrime is a legitimate aim requiring international cooperation and information exchange. However, the EDPS emphasised that given the varied legal systems across UN member states regarding fundamental rights and freedoms, the EU must ensure that cooperation under the Convention does not weaken data protection and privacy rights guaranteed under EU law.

The EDPS welcomed that the Convention does not oblige States Parties to transfer personal data in breach of their applicable data protection laws. The EDPS underlined that EU Member States, when implementing and applying the Convention, are required to carefully verify in each case whether the conditions set out in Chapter V of the Law Enforcement Directive are met before transferring personal data to a third country. Wojciech Wiewiórowski, EDPS, stated that the EU requires sustainable agreements for sharing personal data with non-EU countries for law enforcement purposes whilst maintaining fundamental rights protection.

The EDPS further noted that competent authorities of Member States must ensure that transfers of personal data to third countries that are Parties to the Convention remain fully consistent with international human rights obligations and the fundamental rights of individuals concerned. Where necessary, Member States are required to use grounds available to refuse cooperation.

The EDPS recommended that the effects of the Convention in practice be carefully assessed and that data protection experts be involved in its future reviews. The EDPS stated that any possible future attempts to introduce offences incompatible with EU law or values should be firmly opposed.

recXGrIyl0wzDsj04.orig_title: European Commission brings use of Microsoft 365 into compliance with data protection rules for EU institutions and bodies
recXGrIyl0wzDsj04.gen_title: European Commission achieves compliance with EU data protection rules on Microsoft 365 use, EDPS closes enforcement proceedings
recXGrIyl0wzDsj04.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) has concluded that the European Commission has remedied all infringements identified in relation to its use of Microsoft 365 and has closed enforcement proceedings following the Commission's implementation of corrective measures under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

On 11 July 2025, the EDPS issued a letter confirming closure of the enforcement proceedings after the Commission submitted a compliance report in December 2024 and provided additional information regarding measures implemented and scheduled in conjunction with Microsoft. The EDPS had previously issued a decision on 8 March 2024 identifying multiple infringements and imposing corrective measures on the Commission's use of the cloud platform.

The EDPS finds that the Commission has implemented substantial improvements across four key compliance areas. On purpose limitation, the Commission has explicitly specified the types of personal data processed and the purposes of processing, ensuring that Microsoft and sub-processors handle data solely based on documented instructions and for specified purposes in the public interest. The Commission has further ensured that processing outside the European Economic Area complies with third-country law providing essentially equivalent protection to that in the EEA.

Regarding transfers to third countries, the EDPS determined that the Commission has specified recipients and purposes for data transfers and ensured compliance with Article 47 of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. Technical and organisational measures now limit transfers outside the EU/EEA to countries listed in the amended contract, relying either on adequacy decisions or the derogation for important reasons of public interest under Article 50(1)(d) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

On disclosures and notifications, the EDPS notes that the Commission has established contractual provisions ensuring that only EU or Member State law may require Microsoft or sub-processors to omit notification of disclosure requests for data processed within the EEA. For data processed outside the EEA, third-country law may impose such requirements provided it offers essentially equivalent protection.

The EDPS has made the improvements available to other EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies through the Inter-Institutional Licensing Agreement with Microsoft. The EDPS states that closure of these proceedings does not imply assessment or confirmation of the Commission's overall compliance with other provisions of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 outside the scope of this examination.

recDQAzEHcYP1Wrn5.orig_title: EDPS unveils revised Guidance on Generative AI, strengthening data protection in a rapidly changing digital era
recDQAzEHcYP1Wrn5.gen_title: EDPS publishes revised guidance on generative AI use by EU institutions
recDQAzEHcYP1Wrn5.summary:
On 28 October 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) published revised guidelines on the use of generative Artificial Intelligence and processing of personal data by EU institutions, bodies, offices, and agencies (EUIs), reflecting developments in the technological landscape and evolving challenges posed by generative AI systems under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

The EDPS states that the updated guidance reinforces its commitment to advising EUIs on full compliance with their data protection obligations. Building on feedback from EUIs, the revised guidance provides clearer and more practical instructions for the responsible development and deployment of generative AI tools. The EDPS introduces a refined definition of generative AI for greater clarity and consistency, and a new action-oriented compliance checklist to help EUIs assess and ensure the lawfulness of their processing activities.

The guidance clarifies the roles and responsibilities of EUIs in determining whether they act as controllers, joint controllers, or processors in generative AI contexts. The EDPS provides detailed advice on lawful bases, purpose limitation, and the handling of data subjects' rights when processing personal data through generative AI systems. Wojciech Wiewiórowski, European Data Protection Supervisor, stated that the revision reaffirms the dual mission of enabling human-centric innovation within the EU whilst rigorously safeguarding individuals' personal data, ensuring that generative AI used by EU institutions serves the public interest without compromising Europe's data protection standards.

The EDPS notes that it issued these guidelines within its role as the independent data protection supervisory authority of the EUIs, and not within its role as a market surveillance authority under the EU's Artificial Intelligence Act. The EDPS indicates it will continue to track the evolution of generative AI and update its guidance where necessary to address emerging challenges. The supervisory authority's responsibilities and the rules for data protection in EU institutions are established under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

rec49vjB6U4eLGLLD.orig_title: EDPS participates in fourth Coordinated Enforcement Action: focus on the right to erasure of personal data
rec49vjB6U4eLGLLD.gen_title: EDPS launches fourth Coordinated Enforcement Action on right to erasure
rec49vjB6U4eLGLLD.summary:
On 7 April 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) announced its participation in the fourth Coordinated Enforcement Action organised by the European Data Protection Board (EDPB), focusing on the right to erasure of personal data across EU institutions and the broader European Economic Area. The EDPS is conducting this action alongside 30 data protection authorities from the EEA under the Coordinated Enforcement Framework established in 2022.

The EDPS will conduct a fact-finding exercise targeting EU institutions' data controllers through a questionnaire based on analysis of individual complaints regarding erasure requests. The action aims to assess how controllers comply with legal obligations under the right to erasure, identified in Article 19 of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 (EUDPR) for EU institutions and Article 17 of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) for EU/EEA countries. The EDPS notes that the right to erasure, also termed the right to be forgotten, is the most frequently exercised data subject right, though it is not absolute and permits exceptions for legal compliance, public interest and other grounds.

Wojciech Wiewiórowski, EDPS, stated that the action demonstrates commitment to coherent application of EU data protection law and protection of personal data across the EU/EEA. The EDPS notes that the right to erasure empowers individuals to reclaim control over their identity in the digital world, though its application is complex, requiring case-by-case analysis by controllers balancing data protection rights against collective memory considerations.

Following the fact-finding exercise, the EDPS will summarise findings, identify best and worst practices, and highlight areas for improvement. The EDPB will publish a coordinated report on the enforcement action's outcome. This is the fourth such coordinated action by the EDPS since 2022, with prior actions addressing data protection officers, cloud services use in the public sector, and the right of access to personal data.

reciMc80s8qnVO7Ht.orig_title: Coordinated Enforcement Action: EDPS findings highlight challenges on right of access to personal data
reciMc80s8qnVO7Ht.gen_title: EDPS publishes findings on right of access to personal data in EU institutions
reciMc80s8qnVO7Ht.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) has released findings on how EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies (EUIs) implement individuals' right of access to personal data under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. The findings form part of a broader Coordinated Enforcement Action initiated by the European Data Protection Board in February 2024, which aggregated results from participating Data Protection Authorities across the EU.

The EDPS conducted a detailed survey amongst EUIs and analysed complaints related to access requests. Wojciech Wiewiórowski, EDPS, stated that the right of access constitutes a core element of data protection and serves as a vehicle for transparency and accountability regarding how personal data is processed by EUIs.

The EDPS identifies five key findings from the enforcement action. First, most EUIs receive a limited volume of access requests annually, with 58 out of 63 respondents reporting between 0 and 25 requests in 2023, partly attributable to self-service tools enabling individuals to download their personal data. Second, many EUIs lack centralised systems for managing access requests, creating potential inconsistencies and compliance demonstration difficulties during audits or litigation. Third, EUIs encounter obstacles in distinguishing access requests from other request types, such as public access to documents or complaints. Fourth, identity verification processes sometimes result in excessive or unnecessary processing of personal data, including sensitive data. Fifth, EUIs face practical challenges in interpreting obligations when providing copies of personal data whilst protecting the rights and freedoms of others.

The EDPS states that these findings will shape future supervisory and enforcement actions aimed at improving how EUIs address the right of access. The Coordinated Enforcement Action represents part of the EDPB's broader Coordinated Enforcement Framework, which previously examined cloud services use by the public sector in 2022 and data protection officers' roles and responsibilities in 2023. The framework's 2025 focus will examine implementation of the right to erasure.

reckfsMWRfpP9VKnE.orig_title: EDPS reprimands Frontex for non-compliance with Regulation (EU) 2019/1896
reckfsMWRfpP9VKnE.gen_title: EDPS reprimands Frontex for systematic sharing of suspect data with Europol without necessity assessment
reckfsMWRfpP9VKnE.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has issued a reprimand to Frontex, the European Border and Coast Guard Agency, for breaching Regulation (EU) 2019/1896 by systematically sharing personal data of suspects of cross-border crimes with Europol without assessing whether such sharing was strictly necessary for Europol to perform its mandate, as required by Article 90(2)(a) of the Frontex Regulation.

On 20 April 2026, the EDPS published its determination following an audit initiated in October 2022. The EDPS found that Frontex collected information on suspects of cross-border crime during debriefing interviews of individuals intercepted at EU external borders and then transmitted this information to Europol proactively without performing any assessment of necessity. The EDPS determined that Frontex infringed the Frontex Regulation by failing to establish whether the sharing of suspect information was strictly necessary for Europol to fulfil its operational mandate. Wojciech Wiewiórowski, the EDPS, stated that Frontex bore responsibility for complying with specific safeguards to prevent individuals who may not be of interest to Europol from being entered into their systems, noting that processing data in an EU law enforcement database can have serious consequences for those involved, including wrongful association with criminal activity across the EU.

The EDPS determined that this constituted a severe breach of the Frontex Regulation. However, the EDPS decided to issue only a reprimand rather than exercise broader enforcement powers, taking into account that five days after adoption of the EDPS audit report in May 2023, Frontex halted its systematic sharing with Europol. Since then, Frontex has shared suspect data with Europol on only one occasion, following an individual, precise and specific assessment of necessity. The EDPS found that Frontex has since engaged in discussions with Europol to define criteria for assessing whether collected information is strictly necessary for Europol's mandate and to establish detailed rules governing future information exchanges.

rec29H0EMQLlQZtHC.orig_title: Annual Report 2024: acting for the future of data protection
rec29H0EMQLlQZtHC.gen_title: EDPS publishes 2024 Annual Report marking two decades of data protection oversight
rec29H0EMQLlQZtHC.summary:
On 23 April 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) released its Annual Report 2024, concluding its 2020–2024 mandate and documenting 20 years of privacy and personal data protection for EU institutions and bodies.

The EDPS reports that it established an Artificial Intelligence Unit in 2024 and unveiled an AI Strategy founded on Governance, Risk Management and Supervision. The EDPS states that it has assumed a dual regulatory role: competent market surveillance authority for supervision of AI systems, and notified body for assessing conformity of certain high-risk AI systems under EU law. The EDPS determines that this dual mandate is necessary to ensure that EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies uphold the highest EU data protection standards when developing and using AI tools.

The EDPS documents that its foresight projects in 2024 focused on monitoring and understanding AI-led technologies, examining both their benefits and limitations for data protection. From a policy and consultation perspective, the EDPS reports issuing 97 legislative consultations—a record number—advising EU co-legislators on data protection aspects of forthcoming EU regulations covering the Digital Rulebook, Health, Justice and Home Affairs, and Digital Wallets. The EDPS states these legislative interventions reflect direct impact on EU citizens' fundamental rights to privacy.

The EDPS notes that supervision and enforcement activities were intensified during 2024, providing tools to EU institutions through Supervisory Opinions, verification and authorisation of international transfers, training sessions, and data protection officer networking forums to ensure compliance with EU data protection laws (Regulation (EU) 2018/1725). The EDPS reports that it strengthened collaboration with the European Data Protection Board, of which it is a member and Secretariat provider, to ensure consistent application of EU data protection rules across member states. The EDPS states it participated in and led G7 data protection and privacy authority engagement and other international cooperation platforms to elevate EU data protection standards globally.

rec1vNV0KRMDOj1ZK.orig_title: Opinion 1/2026 on the Proposal for a Regulation amending Regulation (EU) No 904/2010 as regards access of the EPPO and OLAF to VAT information
rec1vNV0KRMDOj1ZK.gen_title: EDPS issues Opinion 1/2026 on VAT data access for EPPO and OLAF
rec1vNV0KRMDOj1ZK.summary:
On 7 January 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor issued Opinion 1/2026 on a European Commission proposal to amend Regulation (EU) No 904/2010 to grant the European Public Prosecutor's Office and the European Anti-Fraud Office direct access to value added tax information at Union level, pursuant to Article 42(1) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

The Commission issued the proposal on 14 November 2025 to address cross-border VAT fraud, including Missing Trader Intra-Community fraud. The EDPS recognised that EPPO's and OLAF's direct access to VAT information is necessary to achieve effective combating of cross-border VAT fraud. The EDPS assessed this position considering the specific characteristics of MTIC fraud, the targeted nature of the access, and the specific categories of information concerned, which relate primarily to VAT numbers and business-to-business transactions.

The EDPS emphasised that personal data processing for administrative purposes and for criminal law enforcement purposes are distinct from each other, and that this separation must be carefully reflected in the proposal. The EDPS noted that the proposal concerns the boundaries between administrative VAT cooperation and criminal enforcement.

The EDPS recommended that the proposal should clearly explain and underline the exceptional nature of providing direct, albeit limited, access to administrative databases for law enforcement purposes. The EDPS considers that further clarification is warranted to avoid the proposal being misconstrued as a precedent for more general application. The supervisor also recommended that the proposal ensure implementation of appropriate legal, technical and organisational safeguards tailored to the targeted nature of the measure. The EDPS stated that whilst technical and organisational measures can be further specified by implementing act, certain parameters should be defined at the level of the basic act. The EDPS recommended including additional safeguards to help ensure compliance with the principles of purpose limitation, data minimisation and accountability.

recJIhNGKrBoU9Lhz.orig_title: Prior consultations, zero surprises: early oversight in data processing in the Area of Freedom, Security and Justice
recJIhNGKrBoU9Lhz.gen_title: EDPS completes ten prior consultations with Europol and Eurojust within legal deadlines
recJIhNGKrBoU9Lhz.summary: The European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) has concluded ten prior consultations submitted by Europol and Eurojust within the applicable legal timeframes in 2025, demonstrating effective early oversight of high-risk data processing in law enforcement and judicial cooperation. Prior consultations are required under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 when planned data processing is likely to result in high risk to individuals' rights and freedoms, even after mitigation measures have been identified. The EDPS states that the mechanism operates on a logic of accountability and prevention rather than reaction, creating structured dialogue between organisations and supervisory authorities before problems arise. All ten consultations were assessed and concluded within the prescribed legal deadlines: six weeks under the Europol Regulation (extendable by one additional month) and two months under the Eurojust Regulation. The EDPS notes that prior consultations are particularly important in the Area of Freedom, Security and Justice, where law enforcement and judicial agencies often handle sensitive data, deploy advanced analytical tools, and engage in cross-border cooperation with far-reaching consequences for individuals. Under the Law Enforcement Directive (Directive (EU) 2016/680) and Chapter IX of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725, prior consultation is required either where a Data Protection Impact Assessment indicates high risk or where processing involves new technologies, mechanisms or procedures that per se carry high risk to individuals' rights and freedoms. The EDPS states that in practice it conducts detailed examination of proposed operations and issues written opinions recommending modifications or additional safeguards. The legal framework recognises operational urgency in law enforcement: Europol may initiate processing after launching prior consultation but before the standard deadline expires if necessary to prevent or combat immediate threats or protect vital interests, provided the EDPS is notified and its subsequent advice is taken into account. The EDPS finds that timely decision-making is essential to legal certainty, as delayed opinions can stall operational projects or create compliance uncertainty.

recBGqhfVfrZv85vo.orig_title: Prior consultations, zero surprises: early oversight in data processing in the Area of Freedom, Security and Justice
recBGqhfVfrZv85vo.gen_title: EDPS completes ten prior consultation assessments for Europol and Eurojust within legal deadlines
recBGqhfVfrZv85vo.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has concluded ten prior consultation assessments submitted by Europol and Eurojust within applicable legal deadlines, demonstrating effective early oversight of high-risk data processing operations in law enforcement and judicial cooperation.

On 20 April 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor published guidance on prior consultation mechanisms under EU data protection law, illustrating the framework's application to agencies operating in the Area of Freedom, Security and Justice. The EDPS notes that prior consultations are required when planned data processing is likely to result in high risk to individuals' rights and freedoms, even after mitigation measures have been identified (Art. 36 GDPR; Art. 63(5) Regulation (EU) 2018/1725).

The EDPS identifies prior consultations as grounded in accountability and prevention principles. The mechanism establishes structured dialogue between organisations and supervisory authorities before implementation of high-risk processing. For law enforcement and judicial cooperation, the Law Enforcement Directive (Directive (EU) 2016/680) and Chapter IX of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 broaden the requirement to apply where Data Protection Impact Assessments indicate high risk or where processing per se involves high risk through new technologies, mechanisms or procedures. The EDPS states that prior consultation provides organisations with legal certainty, enables proactive risk mitigation, facilitates consistent interpretation of data protection principles, and allows innovation within clear legal frameworks.

The EDPS notes that in 2025, ten prior consultations from Europol and Eurojust were assessed and concluded within applicable deadlines: six weeks under the Europol Regulation, extendable by one month, and two months under the Eurojust Regulation. The EDPS indicates that meeting these deadlines in complex cases required strong internal coordination, careful legal and technical analysis, and constructive dialogue with consulting agencies. The EDPS states that timeliness itself is essential to legal certainty, as delayed decisions risk stalling operational projects or creating compliance uncertainty. The EDPS emphasises that the Europol Regulation includes an urgency procedure permitting processing initiation before standard consultation deadlines where operations are urgent and necessary to prevent crime or protect vital interests, provided the EDPS is notified and advice is subsequently considered.

recXmuenB445OnOqP.orig_title: EDPS Supervisory Guidance on the Role of the Data Protection Officers in EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies
recXmuenB445OnOqP.gen_title: EDPS issues supervisory guidance on data protection officer role in EU institutions
recXmuenB445OnOqP.summary:
On 18 December 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) published supervisory guidance interpreting the role, position, and tasks of data protection officers (DPOs) in Union institutions, bodies, offices and agencies under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

The EDPS states that the Guidance aims to support EU institutions in ensuring the effective, independent, and consistent application of Union data protection law. The EDPS identifies the DPO as a key internal safeguard for the protection of personal data within the EU institutional framework.

The Guidance addresses the statutory obligations placed on DPOs operating within the EU's institutional structure, which differs from the private and public sector framework under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). The EDPS notes that DPOs in EU institutions operate under a distinct regulatory regime that requires tailored interpretation to reflect the institutional context and governance structures specific to the Union.

The EDPS has also adopted a separate Decision on Rules governing the application of prior consent requirements for the dismissal of DPOs. That Decision is scheduled for publication in the Official Journal of the European Union at the beginning of 2026 and will enter into force on the twentieth day following its publication. The EDPS states that this procedural safeguard reinforces the independence of DPOs by requiring prior consent from the EDPS before DPO dismissal, reflecting the significance of the DPO function as an independent monitoring mechanism within institutional data protection governance.

The Guidance document, available in English, provides practical interpretation for EU institutions seeking to operationalise their data protection officer arrangements in compliance with Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 requirements. The publication of the accompanying Decision on dismissal procedures will complete the EDPS's regulatory framework for DPO independence and accountability within the Union institutional context.

recqxJOrNOPwkoj0V.gen_title: Spain's Supreme Court upholds Tax Agency's use of third-party personal data in tax fraud investigations
recqxJOrNOPwkoj0V.summary:
On 20 April 2026, the EU Agency for Fundamental Rights published case law reference STS 4096/2025 from Spain's Supreme Court, which dismissed an appeal challenging the Spanish Tax Administration Agency's inclusion of a third party's personal data in official tax liquidation documents issued to a separate taxpayer.

The Supreme Court held that the Tax Agency's processing of X's personal data (name, familial relation, and administrator role) to substantiate tax liability and reject expense deductions did not constitute an unlawful disclosure under the Organic Law 3/2018 (LOPDGDD) or a violation of Article 8 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU. The Court determined that the inclusion of third-party data in tax proceedings constituted lawful personal data processing rather than a prohibited transfer, confirming the inadmissibility decision previously issued by the Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD).

The Supreme Court found that processing third-party personal data in tax liquidations is legitimate when three conditions are satisfied: first, it fulfils a legal obligation applicable to the Tax Agency under Article 102.2.c of the General Tax Law; second, it pursues a public interest in combating tax fraud and ensuring effective tax administration; and third, the data inclusion is strictly necessary and proportionate. The Court concluded that the personal data were essential to establish the familial and professional links between X and the taxpayer, thereby justifying the rejection of contested expense deductions.

The Court interpreted Article 6.1(e) of the GDPR (processing necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest) as providing a valid legal basis for the Tax Agency's processing. The decision reflects the Court's view that balancing the fundamental right to data protection under Article 8 of the Charter against the State's exercise of public powers in combating tax fraud permits such processing when adequate, appropriate, pertinent and necessary to determine facts and motivate tax decisions. The ruling addresses the scope of permissible data processing by tax authorities when investigating alleged fraudulent tax arrangements involving multiple parties.

recSHn5TA41GfEWVp.orig_title: Guidance for Risk Management of Artificial Intelligence systems
recSHn5TA41GfEWVp.gen_title: EDPS publishes guidance on risk management for artificial intelligence systems
recSHn5TA41GfEWVp.summary:
On 11 November 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) published guidance addressing the identification and mitigation of technical risks associated with artificial intelligence systems in the context of personal data protection.

The EDPS guidance provides practical recommendations to help organisations recognise common technical risks inherent in AI systems. The document aims to support data protection compliance by offering insights into how technical vulnerabilities in AI can affect the protection of personal data. The guidance addresses the intersection of AI system design and data protection obligations, particularly relevant given the regulatory framework established under the AI Act and the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

The EDPS notes that AI systems present distinct technical challenges that require systematic risk identification and mitigation strategies. The guidance recognises that many organisations implementing AI technologies may lack comprehensive understanding of how technical failures or design flaws in these systems can create data protection risks. By providing practical recommendations, the EDPS seeks to enable organisations to embed risk management processes into AI system development and deployment.

The publication reflects the EDPS's role as an independent data protection authority for EU institutions and bodies, and its capacity to issue guidance on emerging data protection issues. The timing of this guidance aligns with increasing deployment of AI systems across public and private sector organisations, where personal data processing through AI is subject to GDPR requirements including data protection by design and default (Art. 25 GDPR). The EDPS guidance complements the AI Act's requirements for risk assessment and documentation, creating a more comprehensive framework for organisations to address both technical AI risks and data protection obligations simultaneously.

The guidance document is available in English and has been published in portable document format. The EDPS continues to monitor developments in AI technology and data protection compliance, with this publication representing part of its ongoing work to support compliance with data protection law as AI systems become more prevalent in processing personal data across the EU.

recgb0g60ngN5tVsb.orig_title: First EDPS Orientations for EUIs using Generative AI
recgb0g60ngN5tVsb.gen_title: EDPS publishes first orientations on generative AI use by EU institutions
recgb0g60ngN5tVsb.summary:
On 3 June 2024, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) published its Orientations on generative Artificial Intelligence and personal data protection, providing practical guidance to EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies on complying with data protection requirements when processing personal data through generative AI systems.

The EDPS orientations address the application of the data protection legal framework to generative AI use across the European Union's institutional apparatus. The guidance is directed at EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies, recognising that these entities operate under specific data protection obligations when deploying artificial intelligence technologies that process personal data.

The orientations provide practical advice and instructions on the processing of personal data when using generative AI systems. The EDPS structured the guidance to facilitate compliance with applicable data protection requirements, acknowledging the technical and operational complexities that arise when deploying generative AI in institutional contexts. The document covers the intersection of generative AI deployment and personal data protection obligations, offering EU institutions specific direction on how to implement lawful processing practices when utilising such systems.

The EDPS made the orientations available in English, with the full document published as a PDF file of 5.17 megabytes. The guidance represents the EDPS's first formal orientations specifically addressing generative AI and personal data protection, marking an initial attempt to provide clarity on how existing data protection frameworks apply to emerging AI technologies within the EU institutional framework. The publication reflects the EDPS's role in providing guidance to EU institutions on data protection compliance matters and the growing necessity for clear instruction as generative AI systems become integrated into institutional operations. The orientations address practical implementation questions that arise when EU bodies seek to deploy generative AI whilst maintaining compliance with the data protection legal framework.

recYQSEkAzzHevZ6j.orig_title: EDPB-EDPS Joint Opinion 3/2026 on the Proposal for a Regulation on establishing a framework of measures for strengthening Union’s biotechnology and biomanufacturing sectors particularly in the area of health (European Biotech Act).
recYQSEkAzzHevZ6j.gen_title: EDPB and EDPS issue joint opinion on European Biotech Act proposal
recYQSEkAzzHevZ6j.summary:
On 10 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board and European Data Protection Supervisor adopted Joint Opinion 3/2026 on the European Commission's proposal for a Regulation establishing a framework of measures for strengthening the Union's biotechnology and biomanufacturing sectors, particularly in health (European Biotech Act).

The EDPB and EDPS expressed support for the proposal's general objective to foster EU competitiveness in biotechnology and biomanufacturing sectors and to address challenges related to consistent application of the Clinical Trials Regulation (EU) 536/2014. The authorities found these goals align with the EDPB's Helsinki Statement on data protection in health contexts.

The joint opinion addresses amendments to the Clinical Trials Regulation, including modifications to Article 93 concerning data protection. The authorities examined the legal basis for processing personal data under Articles 6 and 9(2)(i) and (j) GDPR, the sharing of personal data with competent authorities and the Commission, and the qualification of sponsors and investigators as controllers under Article 4(7) GDPR. The EDPB and EDPS also reviewed provisions concerning retention periods for personal data including genetic data and health data, further processing for other clinical trials or scientific research, appropriate technical and organisational measures under Article 32 GDPR, and transition periods for compliance.

The authorities further examined other Clinical Trials Regulation amendments including assessment report requirements under Article 7, collection of informed consent via electronic means under Articles 2(21) and 29(1), regulatory sandboxes for clinical trials under Article 27d, and the use of artificial intelligence in clinical trials under new Article 27e. The opinion also addressed Articles 31 to 40 of the proposal concerning artificial intelligence and data as biotechnology enablers, including guidance on advanced technologies deployment, biotechnology testing environments, data quality accelerators, and regulatory sandboxes for novel health biotechnology products. The authorities analysed verification of legitimate need requirements under Article 44 and amendments to the Food Law Regulation (EC) 178/2002 and the Substances of Human Origin Regulation (EU) 2024/1938.

recPEWU7k2s1Ahgzs.orig_title: EDPB-EDPS Joint Opinion on the Proposal for a Cybersecurity Act 2 and the Proposal on amendments to the NIS 2 Directive
recPEWU7k2s1Ahgzs.gen_title: EDPB and EDPS joint opinion on Cybersecurity Act 2 and NIS 2 amendments issued
recPEWU7k2s1Ahgzs.summary:
On 18 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board and European Data Protection Supervisor jointly issued an opinion on the European Commission's Proposal for a Cybersecurity Act 2 and amendments to the NIS 2 Directive, submitted on 20 and 21 January 2026 respectively.

The EDPB and EDPS recognise that cybersecurity and data protection have a dual relationship: cybersecurity protects personal data by limiting risks of unauthorised access or modification, yet some cybersecurity measures can interfere with privacy and data protection rights. The authorities emphasise that whilst effectiveness is important, necessity and proportionality must be considered in all measures.

The EDPB and EDPS support the proposals' general objectives to strengthen ENISA's role and facilitate uptake of cybersecurity certification, subject to specific recommendations in the joint opinion. They welcome mechanisms to help organisations comply with cybersecurity requirements more coherently and effectively, and endorse efforts to address risks to ICT supply chains including non-technical risks. The authorities strongly support establishing a single-entry point for personal data breach notification, finding this would reduce administrative burden for organisations without diminishing data subject protection.

The EDPB and EDPS welcome clarification of ENISA's support mechanisms to different stakeholders and recommend adding the EDPS as a potential requestor of ENISA advice. They recommend that such advice follow a formal request to ensure clear coordination and division of responsibilities. The authorities also welcome synergies from ENISA cooperation with other Union entities and recommend adding an explicit reference to the EDPS as a Union body for cooperation purposes.

The EDPB and EDPS state that if ENISA's future tasks as an information hub require substantial processing of personal data, this must be explicitly spelled out in the governing provisions, including essential processing elements and appropriate safeguards. Conversely, if the aim is for ENISA to collect and process mostly aggregated non-personal data, this should be clarified. The authorities recommend that prior consultation with the EDPS occur before adoption of rules governing such processing under Article 66 of the Cybersecurity Act 2 Proposal.

recRNti8Jq2ONHTHp.orig_title: EDPB-EDPS Joint Opinion on Digital Omnibus
recRNti8Jq2ONHTHp.gen_title: EDPB and EDPS issue joint opinion on Digital Omnibus proposal
recRNti8Jq2ONHTHp.summary:
On 10 February 2026, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor adopted a joint opinion on the proposed Digital Omnibus Regulation, which aims to simplify the digital legislative framework across the European Union.

The EDPB-EDPS opinion addresses multiple amendments to the GDPR and the ePrivacy Directive. The authorities examine proposed changes to the definition of personal data, including provisions for implementing acts to clarify whether data resulting from pseudonymisation constitutes personal data for certain entities. The opinion further considers definitional and operational amendments relating to scientific research, including assessments of purpose limitation and transparency requirements applicable to that processing category.

The joint opinion addresses proposed exemptions permitting the processing of biometric data and special categories of personal data in artificial intelligence development and operation contexts. The EDPB and EDPS examine the use of legitimate interest as a lawful basis in AI model development and assess a proposed exemption for incidental and residual processing of special categories of data during AI system operation.

Regarding data subject rights, the authorities analyse proposed limitations to access requests and an exemption to transparency obligations where personal data are collected from the data subject directly. The opinion also addresses changes to automated individual decision-making rules. The EDPB-EDPS assess amendments to data breach notification requirements, including provisions for a common template and single-entry point framework.

On Data Protection Impact Assessments, the authorities review proposals for common EDPB lists and methodologies. The opinion further considers ePrivacy amendments concerning terminal equipment protection and security of processing, including automated and machine-readable indications of data subject choices, and the repeal of Article 4 of the ePrivacy Directive.

The joint opinion addresses changes to the Data Acquis framework, including provisions enabling data requests during public emergencies and modifications to data intermediation services and data altruism organisations. The EDPB-EDPS examine amendments shifting data intermediation registration from mandatory prior notification to voluntary registration and permitting functional rather than legal separation of intermediation services from other operations.

recBmYDPqbmcRWOM4.orig_title: EDPB-EDPS Joint Opinion 01/2025 on the Proposal for a Regulation on simplification measures for SMEs and SMCs
recBmYDPqbmcRWOM4.gen_title: EDPB and EDPS joint opinion addresses derogation from record-keeping obligation for SMEs and SMCs under GDPR Article 30(5)
recBmYDPqbmcRWOM4.summary:
On 8 July 2025, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor adopted a joint opinion on the European Commission's proposal to simplify data protection obligations for small and medium-sized enterprises and small mid-cap enterprises.

The EDPB and EDPS assessed the Commission's proposal issued on 21 May 2025, which would modify Article 30(5) GDPR to provide that the record-keeping obligation would not apply to an enterprise or organisation employing fewer than 750 persons unless the processing it carries out is likely to result in a high risk to data subjects' rights and freedoms within the meaning of Article 35 GDPR. The proposal also introduces a definition of SMEs and SMCs in Article 4 GDPR and extends the scope of Articles 40(1) and 42(1) GDPR to SMCs.

The EDPB and EDPS stated that they support the general objective of reducing administrative burden for SMEs and SMCs, provided this does not result in lowering protection of fundamental rights, particularly the right to protection of personal data. The authorities welcomed the proposal's targeted modifications to simplify and clarify the record-keeping obligation, noting these do not affect core GDPR principles and obligations. The EDPB and EDPS highlighted that the proposal lacked an assessment of consequences on fundamental rights and emphasised that simplification must be proportionate, balanced and based on necessity under Article 52 of the Charter.

Regarding Article 30(5) GDPR derogation, the EDPB and EDPS proposed that co-legislators clarify in the recitals that records would only be mandatory for processing activities likely to result in high risk. The authorities stressed that processing of personal data covered under Articles 9 and 10 GDPR is important when assessing whether processing is likely to result in high risk. The EDPB and EDPS suggested rephrasing Recital 10 to clarify that processing under Article 9(2)(b) GDPR would not require a record unless assessment indicates high-risk processing. The EDPB and EDPS noted that records of processing activities support compliance demonstration and constitute useful means to support compliance with several GDPR requirements.

reco91GFjT0w0Wg2E.orig_title: Towards trustworthy AI in the EU public administration: The EDPS Compass for its new role under the AI Act
reco91GFjT0w0Wg2E.gen_title: EDPS designates itself as market surveillance authority for EU institutions' AI systems under AI Act
reco91GFjT0w0Wg2E.summary:
The European Data Protection Supervisor has published a strategic compass defining its supervisory role over artificial intelligence systems deployed by European Union institutions, bodies, offices and agencies under Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (the AI Act).

On 17 March 2026, the EDPS published its Compass document outlining how it will exercise its newly expanded mandate as a market surveillance authority (MSA) and notified body for conformity assessments under the AI Act. The EDPS states that the document defines how it will supervise EU institutions and enforce the AI Act to ensure safe, compliant and human-centric development and use of AI systems.

The EDPS notes that it has undertaken organisational and procedural preparations to operationalise its powers under the AI Act. The authority announced its AI Preparedness Strategy in May 2024 and established an Artificial Intelligence Unit in October 2024. The Compass document presents an overview of the EDPS's expanded mandate, the operational context for exercising supervisory powers, and four strategic pillars guiding its action as MSA and notified body. The EDPS identifies these pillars as: supervision of EU institutions' AI systems through ex ante conformity assessment and ex post market surveillance; contribution to AI Act governance and regulatory coordination through participation in the AI Board; institutional empowerment for trustworthy AI through interinstitutional knowledge exchange and a proposed Trustworthy AI Centre of Excellence; and international engagement and exchange of best practices.

The EDPS states that it is grounded in commitments to impartiality, integrity, transparency, pragmatism and responsible innovation, and intends to reinforce institutional capacity to fulfil its role under the AI Act. The authority identifies priority actions to be pursued over the 2026–2027 period, noting that this timeframe reflects the evolving nature of the AI Act and anticipated developments from harmonised standards, European Commission guidelines and the 2025 Digital Omnibus legislative proposal.

recDxnDG2kH6cc1fu.gen_title: EDPS adopts transparency measures requiring meetings with registered interest representatives
recDxnDG2kH6cc1fu.summary:
On 24 April 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) adopted a decision establishing transparency measures requiring the Supervisor, the Head of the EDPS Secretariat, and staff holding managerial responsibilities to meet exclusively with interest representatives registered in the EU Transparency Register, in accordance with the Interinstitutional Agreement of 20 May 2021 on a mandatory transparency register.

The EDPS decision, adopted pursuant to Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 on data protection by EU institutions and bodies, establishes that meetings between EDPS senior officials and interest representatives must be scheduled bilateral encounters organised to discuss issues related to the formulation or implementation of policy or legislation falling within EDPS competencies. The EDPS defines an interest representative as any natural or legal person, or formal or informal group, association or network that engages in activities carried out with the objective of influencing the formulation or implementation of policy or legislation, or the decision-making processes of the Union institutions, bodies, agencies and offices.

The EDPS determines that information concerning meetings held by the Supervisor, Head of EDPS Secretariat, and staff with managerial responsibilities will be published in the EDPS Agenda, including details of participants and subjects addressed. The authority states that this commitment to openness is intended to provide citizens with transparent insights into EDPS engagements and to foster public understanding and confidence in the EDPS's work and democratic oversight.

To support public access and awareness, the EDPS has established the EDPS Public Access to Documents Register, a platform allowing the public to consult EDPS documents for which access has been granted. The EDPS also provides detailed information on the process for public access to documents to assist citizens in exercising their rights. Through these measures, the EDPS reaffirms its commitment to transparency as a fundamental pillar of democratic governance within the European Union.

recMKa1XCsZXz5Abr.gen_title: Mecklenburg-Vorpommern LfDI warns of fraud scheme targeting paediatric medical data
recMKa1XCsZXz5Abr.summary:
The State Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information of Mecklenburg-Vorpommern (LfDI MV) has issued a warning regarding unknown individuals posing as researchers to obtain medical information of children from medical practices, particularly paediatric surgeries.

On 17 April 2026, the LfDI MV received notification of a pattern where an association contacts medical practices via email under the guise of a research project and requests the transmission of children's medical data. The LfDI MV states that initial investigations indicate this is not an isolated incident. Sebastian Schmidt, the LfDI MV, noted that the authority aims to alert institutions to examine such requests carefully and transmit patient data only under clear legal and ethical conditions.

According to the notification received, doctors have been requested via email to participate in the alleged study. The fraudsters specifically request photographs, for example from treatment documentation, sometimes combined with additional information such as age, diagnosis or treatment date. The submitted research documentation materials appear deceptively authentic. Upon closer examination, the LfDI MV identifies references to websites containing pornographic content and vague statements regarding data processing.

The LfDI MV establishes that release of patient data, particularly photographic material of minors, is only permissible under strict legal and ethical conditions. Medical confidentiality, data protection and the protection needs of children hold the highest priority. Legitimate medical research is typically recognisable by the presence of a verifiable opinion from the competent ethics commission with file reference and date. Genuine projects also maintain transparent study documentation, a clearly named research institution with officially verifiable contact details, and a comprehensible data protection and consent concept, particularly for minors.

The LfDI MV identifies indicators of fraudulent requests as including unclear or changing information about the institution or study, non-verifiable contact methods, submission via free email providers or similarly resembling domains, and requests for uploads to unknown platforms. The authority notes disproportionate data requests, such as requests for "all photographs" including metadata or diagnoses, and abbreviated references to "anonymisation" as suspicious.

Medical practices that have inadvertently responded to such requests and transmitted data must report this immediately as a data breach to the LfDI MV or the responsible data protection supervisory authority in the relevant state and file a report with police.

recQwDicLJXZKB0nF.gen_title: Belgian Data Protection Authority issues opinion on draft ordinance on public infrastructure information exchange
recQwDicLJXZKB0nF.summary:
On 12 March 2026, the Belgian Data Protection Authority (Autorité de protection des données) issued Opinion No. 44/2026 on a draft ordinance concerning the exchange of information on installations in public roads and regional domain, submitted by the Minister of Mobility, Public Works and Road Safety on 4 December 2025.

The Authority examined the draft ordinance, which replaces previous regulations from 2013 and 2014 governing access to and exchange of information on underground cables and pipelines. The draft introduces a new legal framework for exchanging information on underground infrastructure, mandating exclusive use of the CICC system (Central Contact Point for Cable and Pipeline Information), establishing strict deadlines for data transmission with more precise localisation, and creating a system of administrative sanctions. The ordinance also establishes a single point of contact for information sharing with operators regarding public roads and regional domain, in compliance with EU Regulation 2024/1309 on measures to reduce the cost of deploying gigabit-capable electronic communications networks.

The Authority identified several concerns requiring the legislator's attention. First, the Authority finds that certain definitions in the draft differ from those in EU Regulation 2024/1309, despite the explanatory memorandum stating alignment with that Regulation's definitions. The Authority specifically notes discrepancies regarding "public sector body" (Art. 2, para. 14) and "civil works" (Art. 2, para. 21). Additionally, the Authority determines that the text and explanatory memorandum contain errors that impair readability and comprehensibility, emphasising the importance of clear and error-free legislative language.

Second, concerning legality and foreseeability principles, the Authority identifies three categories of personal data processing under the draft: processing within the CICC system (Arts. 4 et seq.); processing within two cadastres managed by Paradigm (Art. 10); and processing for investigating breaches and imposing administrative sanctions (Arts. 13-27). The Authority notes that regarding the CICC system, the requesting minister did not adequately address questions on the scope of personal data processing, with the explanatory memorandum merely referring to federal authority designation of KLIM-CICC as the operator.

recFWG2HDf1eTB2WQ.orig_title: Parere su uno schema di decreto concernente la disciplina della piattaforma telematica istituita presso il Centro elaborazione dati della Direzione generale per la motorizzazione, ai fini della definizione delle tempistiche e delle modalità di presentazione dell’istanza da parte delle agenzie telematiche per l’iscrizione al Registro elettronico delle Agenzie Telematiche - 17 aprile 2026 [10241964]
recFWG2HDf1eTB2WQ.gen_title: Italian data protection authority issues opinion on electronic registry platform for vehicle consultation agencies
recFWG2HDf1eTB2WQ.summary:
On 17 April 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali (Italian Data Protection Authority) issued an opinion on a draft decree by the Directorate General for Motor Vehicles concerning the electronic platform and registry for telematic agencies providing vehicle consultation services.

The opinion addresses the regulatory framework governing registration in the Registro elettronico delle Agenzie Telematiche (ReAT), which was established under Law n. 177/2024 on road safety. The Garante reviewed the draft decree against the GDPR (Regulation (EU) 2016/679) and the Italian Data Protection Code (Legislative Decree n. 196/2003).

The Garante noted that registration in the ReAT is a necessary condition for telematic agencies to access and operate within the automated information systems of the Directorate General for Motor Vehicles. Under the regulatory framework, agencies must demonstrate they hold provincial authorisation and are not subject to suspension or revocation orders to be eligible for registration. The Garante recognised that applicants submit registration requests through a dedicated web application, accessing it via SPID level 2 or CIE (electronic identity card), with the Directorate General for Motor Vehicles responsible for processing registrations.

The Garante examined the decree's provisions regarding suspension, revocation and cancellation of ReAT registration, finding that these measures result in agencies losing access to the automated information systems. The authority also addressed provisions concerning the professional responsibility of agency owners and managers, as established under Law n. 264/1991 governing vehicle consultation activities.

The opinion was delivered following submissions from the Ministry of Infrastructure and Transport dated 14 November 2025, 19 February 2026 and 20 March 2026, seeking the required data protection assessment of the proposed decree regulating application procedures and timelines for agency registration.
recFWG2HDf1eTB2WQ.body: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

recfqJ2dHL2byHx2g.orig_title: Provvedimento del 17 aprile 2026 - Linee Guida in materia di utilizzo di tracking pixel nelle comunicazioni di posta elettronica [10241943]
recfqJ2dHL2byHx2g.gen_title: Italian Data Protection Authority issues guidelines on tracking pixels in email communications
recfqJ2dHL2byHx2g.summary:
On 17 April 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali (Italian Data Protection Authority) adopted guidelines addressing the use of tracking pixels in email communications, published as Provvedimento n. 284/2026.

The Garante recognises that tracking pixels represent a category of tracking tools with particular characteristics that may operate as concealed instruments not readily identifiable by data subjects, thereby constituting a more invasive form of processing. The authority notes that tracking pixels are small, often transparent images of a single pixel embedded in email messages that enable senders to determine whether recipients have opened emails and to gather information about user behaviour and service usage patterns. The Garante observes an increase in the deployment of such tracking tools by platform operators, service providers and intermediaries, and a corresponding rise in complaints from individuals concerning their use.

The guidelines establish that the Garante considers tracking pixels in email communications to fall within the scope of the ePrivacy Directive (Directive 2002/58/EC) and the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). The authority bases its guidance on the EDPB Guidelines 2/2023 concerning the technical scope of Article 5(3) of the ePrivacy Directive, alongside the Garante's existing frameworks on cookie use and online profiling established in prior decisions (Provvedimenti n. 229/2014, n. 161/2015 and n. 231/2021).

The Garante finds that establishing user trust in the digital ecosystem depends fundamentally on individuals exercising control over their personal information, particularly in digital contexts. The authority states that specific protective measures are required that account for the different characteristics and invasiveness of various tracking tools. The guidelines address the concealed nature of tracking pixels and the need for data controllers to provide transparent information to data subjects and obtain appropriate consent prior to deploying such technologies in email communications, consistent with the legal bases and transparency obligations under GDPR (Arts. 5, 6, 13, 14 GDPR) and the ePrivacy Directive.
