The UODO determined that neither BEST Towarzystwo Funduszy Inwestycyjnych S.A. nor BEST III NSFIZ possessed a lawful basis under Polish data protection law to process the applicant's personal data for purposes of enforcing a claim against a deceased person. The authority found that death of a debtor does not automatically render heirs liable for the deceased's financial obligations, nor does it entitle creditors to obtain heir data for debt collection purposes.

The UODO noted that creditors are not entitled to independently determine the circle of potential heirs. The authority stated that only common courts possess jurisdiction to examine inheritance matters through formal proceedings to determine acquisition of the inheritance. The UODO emphasised that mere death of a debtor does not establish liability of descendants for the debtor's debts. Only a formal legal determination of inheritance acquisition, provided by law, would establish who acquired the inheritance and to what extent.

The decision addresses a recurring pattern identified by the UODO: creditors directing repayment demands to debtors' heirs without lawful basis for processing their personal data. The authority found such conduct unlawful. The UODO observed that creditors sometimes direct demands to heirs who have explicitly rejected the inheritance, further demonstrating absence of any legal foundation for such data processing activities.

recE0FO9CKrIB3W91.orig_title: Accompagnement des professionnels : le programme de travail de la CNIL pour 2026
recE0FO9CKrIB3W91.gen_title: CNIL sets 2026 work programme on GDPR compliance and AI governance
recE0FO9CKrIB3W91.summary:
On 7 April 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes (CNIL) published its work programme for 2026, outlining guidance and resources to support public and private sector organisations in complying with the GDPR and certain provisions of the AI Act.

The CNIL states it will continue regular dialogue with professional representatives, economic actors and civil society to identify key compliance challenges and promote best practices. The authority finds that a structured approach to stakeholder engagement enables organisations to anticipate regulatory developments and prepare for future consultations.

In the field of marketing and online commerce, the CNIL will launch work on cross-domain consent mechanisms in 2026, following its earlier recommendations on multi-terminal consent collection. The authority aims to establish a framework that permits collection of a single consent across multiple sites or media within the same group, whilst protecting user privacy and freedom of choice, particularly relevant for media groups and multi-brand environments.

On artificial intelligence governance, the CNIL will finalise practical guidance on AI deployment in workplace and healthcare sectors, addressing algorithmic bias risks and safeguards for employee and patient rights. The authority is conducting a public consultation with the HAS (Haute Autorité de Santé) until 16 April 2026 to define best practices for AI use in clinical care settings. The CNIL will also clarify conditions for implementing automatic voice transcription and analysis tools in call centres and videoconferencing software through use-case documentation.

The CNIL plans to develop operational resources for data protection officers (DPOs) on governance, compliance roadmaps and documentation management in cooperation with DPO associations. The authority will clarify how GDPR applies to non-anonymised AI models and will define the responsibilities of actors across the AI value chain, including model designers and deployers, to ensure balanced governance.

Regarding the AI Act's progressive implementation since 2024, the CNIL anticipates designation as market surveillance authority under the European AI Regulation. The authority identifies the primary challenge as implementing this regulation coherently with existing frameworks to balance innovation support with maintaining public trust in AI uses.

In other sectors, following 2025 consultations, the CNIL will issue guidance for social housing actors on data protection principles and sector-specific data processing practices.
recE0FO9CKrIB3W91.body: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rec6DvJsdkDuzLJqb.orig_title: Annual report 2025 from the actions of CERT Polska. The Polish security landscape
rec6DvJsdkDuzLJqb.gen_title: CERT Polska publishes 2025 annual report on Polish cybersecurity incidents and threat landscape
rec6DvJsdkDuzLJqb.summary:
On 3 April 2026, CERT Polska, the national computer emergency response team operating within the National Research Institute NASK, published its annual report covering cybersecurity activities, incidents and threats across Poland throughout 2025.

CERT Polska acts as CSIRT NASK under the Act of 5 July 2018 on the National Cybersecurity System (Art. 26). The authority is responsible for monitoring cybersecurity threats and incidents at national level, responding to reported incidents, coordinating incident handling, performing advanced analyses of malware and vulnerabilities, developing detection and response tools, and conducting awareness-raising activities. CERT Polska coordinates incidents reported by public finance sector units, government administration subordinates, research institutes, the Office of Technical Inspection, the Polish Centre for Accreditation, environmental protection funds, commercial companies performing public service tasks, and digital service providers.

The 2025 report documents CERT Polska's response to widespread fraud campaigns, advanced persistent threat group monitoring, and threat detection breakthroughs. The report covers security testing, vulnerability disclosure, malware analysis, and ransomware attacks. CERT Polska identifies specific operational areas including the Warning List, SMS fraud detection, coordinated vulnerability disclosure, the #BezpiecznyPrzemysł initiative targeting industrial security, web application audits, and security analysis of public sector mobile applications.

The report details CERT Polska's participation in international exercises including Locked Shields 2025 and the European Cyber Security Challenge 2025. The authority notes its role during Poland's Presidency of the Council of the European Union and its organisation of the SECURE International Summit. CERT Polska documents educational initiatives and awareness-building programmes targeting Polish citizens and professionals.

CERT Polska describes development of multiple tools and platforms including Moje.cert.pl, Artemis, Snitch, AIPITCH, PERUN, FETTA, and DNS4EU, alongside maintenance of the MWDB malware database and n6 incident reporting platform. The report includes incident statistics, MWDB submissions data, and usage metrics for the authority's secure mail and reporting systems. CERT Polska marks its 30th anniversary of operations since 1996 as Poland's first computer emergency response team.

rec1abFyfgXG2Htzw.orig_title: Gestion des ressources humaines : la CNIL publie un référentiel de durées de conservation
rec1abFyfgXG2Htzw.gen_title: CNIL publishes retention periods framework for human resources data processing
rec1abFyfgXG2Htzw.summary:
On 2 April 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL) published a referential framework to guide organisations in identifying and determining appropriate retention periods for human resources data processing activities, covering both public and private sector employers subject to French labour law.

The CNIL developed the framework in collaboration with professional networks and associations across multiple sectors to address operational needs of data controllers managing personnel information. The referential serves as a practical tool for data protection officers, GDPR practitioners, human resources personnel, and information systems management, though compliance is not mandatory where the framework constitutes "soft law". However, the CNIL notes that certain retention periods specified within the framework reflect mandatory legal or regulatory requirements that controllers cannot override.

The framework covers ten processing activities within human resources management: recruitment; administrative personnel management; remuneration management; security of assets and persons; management of professional vehicles; listening to and recording workplace telephone conversations; management of collective labour relations; occupational accident management; litigation and pre-litigation management; and data subject access requests. The CNIL emphasises that retention periods prescribed by legislative or regulatory texts, including those under the French labour code and social security code, remain binding regardless of the framework's guidance. The referential addresses only French national law; organisations operating across multiple jurisdictions must verify applicable legislation in other Member States or territories.

The framework presents retention periods in months to align with legislative formulations, though controllers may convert these to days within applicable software systems. The CNIL advises that controllers must systematically consult the referenced legal texts or regulatory foundations to ensure compliance with their specific circumstances, as multiple retention periods may apply depending on the precise provisions governing particular data categories. The referential supplements existing CNIL guidance on recruitment, human resources management generally, and work-related data processing, and does not replace sector-specific legal requirements under tax, road safety, or internal security legislation.
rec1abFyfgXG2Htzw.body: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rec7qR9gzAXPiThxx.gen_title: CERT-EU documents cyberattacks, sanctions, and supply-chain compromises in March 2026
rec7qR9gzAXPiThxx.summary:
On 1 April 2026, the EU Cybersecurity Agency (CERT-EU) published its monthly Cyber Brief analysing 343 open-source threat reports from March 2026, documenting enforcement actions, espionage campaigns, supply-chain attacks, and data breaches across European institutions and critical infrastructure.

On 16 March, the Council of the European Union sanctioned one Iranian company and two Chinese companies, plus two individuals, for cyberattacks against EU member states and partners. CERT-EU reports the Chinese firms were listed for compromising devices and providing hacking services, whilst the Iranian entity faced sanctions for breaching a French subscriber database, advertising disinformation on billboards during the 2024 Paris Olympic Games, and compromising a Swedish SMS service.

On 11 March, Europol and Eurojust, working with international partners, disrupted the SocksEscort proxy service, which CERT-EU notes had compromised more than 369,000 routers and Internet-of-Things devices worldwide. Authorities seized 34 domains and 23 servers, froze 3.5 million US dollars in cryptocurrency, and disconnected infected devices used to support cybercrime including ransomware attacks, distributed denial-of-service attacks, and child sexual abuse material distribution.

CERT-EU documents significant supply-chain attacks during the period. The agency reports that cybercrime actor TeamPCP compromised several widely used development tools in multi-stage supply-chain attacks, including the Trivy open-source vulnerability scanner and the LiteLLM Python package on PyPI. Additionally, CERT-EU identifies a bot named hackerbot-claw targeting continuous integration and continuous deployment workflows in major open-source GitHub repositories. On the espionage front, CERT-EU notes that a leak of Russia-linked APT FancyBear exposed its cyberespionage infrastructure, tools, and data, whilst a Russia-linked threat actor deployed an iOS full-chain exploit affecting multiple targets across Turkey, Ukraine, Saudi Arabia, and Malaysia.

CERT-EU reports data breaches affecting European government institutions, including Swedish government data stolen from its E-Gov platform by cybercrime group ByteToBreach, a breach of Dutch Ministry of Finance systems in an unspecified cyberattack, and a breach of Dutch national police systems following a phishing attack.

recl4dZaJciURfase.orig_title: Déclaration en ligne des systèmes de vidéosurveillance et vidéoprotection : l’IPDCP délivre les premiers récépissés et pictogrammes - IPDCP
recl4dZaJciURfase.gen_title: IPDCP issues first video surveillance declaration receipts and pictograms in Togo
recl4dZaJciURfase.summary:
The Instance de Protection des Données à Caractère Personnel (IPDCP) of Togo has issued the first receipts and regulatory pictograms for video surveillance and video protection system declarations following the launch of its online declaration platform on 1 April 2026.

On 1 April 2026, the IPDCP opened its online declaration service through the national public services portal following the official launch of the platform on 27 March 2026. The IPDCP registered three initial declarations submitted by one company and two individuals for private-use installations. The authority processed these first applications through to completion within an optimised timeframe, demonstrating the full operational capability of the system and the IPDCP's capacity to deliver efficient, secure processing compliant with legal requirements.

Following delivery of the receipts and pictograms, the President of the IPDCP, Colonel BELEI Bédiani, formally presented the first declaration receipts and regulatory pictograms to the applicants. The IPDCP states that this symbolic moment marks the completion of the dematerialised process and the entry into force of a strengthened compliance mechanism. The pictogram, which the IPDCP identifies as the central element of the system, constitutes an essential requirement for informing data subjects, guaranteeing transparency of processing and compliance with fundamental principles of personal data protection, notably the right to information and respect for privacy.

The IPDCP invites all data controllers, including public administrations, companies, non-governmental organisations, associations, commercial establishments such as shops, bars, hotels, motels and restaurants, and individuals, to comply with legal provisions by declaring their video surveillance devices via the dedicated platform accessible through the national public services portal at https://service-public.gouv.tg. The IPDCP characterises this process as simple and secure, and states that it contributes to promotion of responsible use of surveillance technologies within a framework respectful of fundamental rights and freedoms.
recl4dZaJciURfase.body: Instance de Protection des Données à Caractère Personnel

rec06OTOwauQdf2ms.orig_title: Municipales 2026 : le bilan de l’observatoire des élections de la CNIL
rec06OTOwauQdf2ms.gen_title: CNIL records 739 reports during 2026 municipal elections, initiates four investigations
rec06OTOwauQdf2ms.summary:
On 8 April 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL) published its assessment of the 2026 municipal elections held on 15 and 22 March, finding that it had received 739 reports and processed 81 complaints, with four investigations and one simplified procedure initiated, in the first electoral cycle subject to the transparency and targeting of political advertising regulation that entered into force on 10 October 2025.

The CNIL reactivates its elections observatory at each electoral deadline to monitor political communication practices, engage with parties and candidates, and inform voters of their rights. The 2026 municipal elections represented a particular focus because they were the first elections subject to the regulation on the transparency and targeting of political advertising (RPP). To support stakeholders' understanding of this new framework, the CNIL implemented an action plan comprising six practical guides and seven reflexes for responsible campaigning directed at professionals, together with a fact sheet on voters' rights and a frequently-asked-questions document for the electorate. The authority also made available to voters a reporting form to flag practices deemed prohibited.

Between the launch of the reporting platform on 30 January 2026 and the second round of voting, the CNIL observed a substantial decrease in the number of reports and complaints compared with the previous 2020 municipal elections. Of the 739 reports received, the CNIL finds that 405 concerned SMS prospection (63 per cent), 98 postal mail (16 per cent), 85 emails (13 per cent), 45 telephone calls (7 per cent) and 9 social media prospection (1 per cent). The CNIL processed 81 complaints, predominantly arising from emails (41), SMS messages (25) and social media outreach (2), with the primary complaint ground concerning data origin and, in certain cases involving incumbent candidates, suspected purpose diversion under Article 5(1)(b) GDPR. The authority notes that four investigations and one simplified procedure have been initiated, targeting a candidate who allegedly failed to respond to a rights exercise request.
rec06OTOwauQdf2ms.body: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rec1mFihIsMe6SPIN.orig_title: New Episode: The IDPC Podcast – GDPR and data processing - IDPC
rec1mFihIsMe6SPIN.gen_title: IDPC publishes podcast episode on GDPR data processing fundamentals
rec1mFihIsMe6SPIN.summary:
On 1 April 2026, the Information and Data Protection Commissioner (IDPC) of Malta released a new episode of its podcast series addressing core aspects of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), specifically focusing on data processing obligations and principles.

Hosted by Franco Aloisio, Head of Communications at the IDPC, the episode features discussion with Ingrid Camilleri, Head Legal at the IDPC. The podcast explores the foundational elements of GDPR compliance, including the true purpose and scope of the regulation, the definition of personal data and its boundaries, and whether the GDPR applies to data concerning deceased persons.

The IDPC discusses what constitutes "processing" of data in practical terms and outlines the key principles that organisations must follow when handling personal data. The episode also addresses the scope of the GDPR, including its limitations and exemptions, to provide clarity on where the regulation applies and where exceptions may exist.

The IDPC presents the GDPR as a framework intended to establish trust and enable business operations in data-driven environments, rather than viewing it solely as a regulatory compliance burden. The podcast is available on YouTube and Spotify platforms, making the content accessible to both legal professionals and organisations seeking foundational understanding of GDPR obligations.

This podcast episode forms part of the IDPC's broader communications and public education programme on data protection matters within Malta's jurisdiction.

recQKmsLTIgaqeqEZ.orig_title: WATENGENEZA MAUDHUI MWANZA WAKUMBUSHWA UMUHIMU WA KULINDA TAARIFA BINAFSI MTANDAONI
recQKmsLTIgaqeqEZ.gen_title: PDPC urges Mwanza content creators to prioritise personal data protection compliance
recQKmsLTIgaqeqEZ.summary:
On 1 April 2026, the Personal Data Protection Commission (PDPC) of Tanzania issued a public reminder to content creators in Mwanza and the Lake Region regarding their obligations under the Personal Data Protection Act, Chapter 44, emphasising that data protection forms a core component of their accountability in the digital age.

Innocent Mungy, Head of Public Relations and Communications at PDPC, delivered the message during a session in Mwanza addressing the importance of personal data protection for content creators. PDPC states that content creators bear responsibility not only for entertainment but also for protecting the personal information of individuals featured in their content. Mungy stressed that content creators are directly involved in the collection, processing and distribution of personal data through their daily activities and are required to obtain consent from data subjects and use their information only for the purposes specified under the Personal Data Protection Act, Chapter 44.

PDPC identifies personal data to include names, telephone numbers, photographs, locations of individuals and financial information. The authority finds that failure to protect such data can result in significant harm, including loss of follower trust, reputational damage and exposure to legal action. PDPC notes that the reminder was issued to encourage content creators to register with the Commission before the voluntary registration period closes on 8 April 2026, in order to avoid consequences arising from non-compliance with legal requirements.

The outreach activity reflects PDPC's broader compliance enforcement strategy, following previous deadlines and registration drives across multiple sectors and regions in Tanzania. The Commission has previously set and extended registration deadlines, with the April 2026 deadline representing a critical checkpoint for entities seeking to formalise their data protection compliance status.

recEyJewcsL0JEDxc.orig_title: L’IPDCP lance la déclaration en ligne des dispositifs de vidéosurveillance au Togo - IPDCP
recEyJewcsL0JEDxc.gen_title: Togo's IPDCP launches online declaration platform for video surveillance devices
recEyJewcsL0JEDxc.summary:
On 27 March 2026, the Instance de Protection des Données à Caractère Personnel (IPDCP), Togo's data protection authority, officially launched an online platform for the declaration of video surveillance and video protection devices. The platform was presented at a ceremony in Yamoussoukro, with IPDCP President Colonel Bédiani Belei and government officials including Secrétaire Général M. Kassime Tidjani attending on behalf of the Minister of Public Service Efficiency and Digital Transformation.

The IPDCP states that the digitalised declaration system aims to modernise administrative procedures, streamline user processes, and strengthen traceability in file handling. The authority determines that the platform ensures responsible and compliant use of video surveillance devices in accordance with data protection principles governing personal data protection. Colonel Bédiani Belei emphasised the strategic importance of the reform in his remarks, noting that the digital platform modernises administrative procedures whilst guaranteeing adherence to data protection principles.

At the launch ceremony, IPDCP teams demonstrated the platform's functionality to participants, detailing the process stages of online declaration. The IPDCP highlights that key innovations include simplified procedures, significantly reduced processing timeframes, and enhanced traceability of operations. The demonstration included presentation of a declaration receipt and regulatory pictogram, which the IPDCP identifies as essential elements for compliant and regulated operation of video surveillance devices.

The authority notes that the platform represents a decisive advance in modernising public administration by combining technological innovation, administrative efficiency, and data protection requirements. The IPDCP states the platform reflects an administration committed to digital transformation as a vector for trust, transparency, and the guarantee of citizens' fundamental rights and freedoms. Interactive discussions during the launch provided opportunity to address participant concerns and ensure effective adoption of the digital tools.
recEyJewcsL0JEDxc.body: Instance de Protection des Données à Caractère Personnel

recwLYHHyovMQ8RUE.orig_title: NIST Workshop on AI Incident Management
recwLYHHyovMQ8RUE.gen_title: NIST convenes workshop on AI incident management and response frameworks
recwLYHHyovMQ8RUE.summary:
On 13 and 14 May 2026, the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), within the U.S. Department of Commerce, will host a workshop on AI Incident Management at its Gaithersburg facility. The workshop brings together stakeholders from government, industry, and academia to develop coordinated approaches to managing incidents involving AI systems across critical infrastructure and national security contexts.

NIST states that as AI systems become increasingly embedded in critical operations, a new class of incidents is emerging in which AI systems function both as targets and sources of risk. The workshop will present a high-level NIST roadmap to advance AI incident response standards and practices, engage stakeholders to understand current approaches including existing playbooks and frameworks, and explore definitions, lifecycles, and taxonomy of AI-related incidents. NIST notes that participants will identify gaps in current cybersecurity and AI risk management guidance and examine emerging AI incident types beyond traditional cybersecurity, including misuse scenarios.

NIST indicates that outcomes from the workshop will inform future efforts by its Information Technology Laboratory (ITL) and Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) to implement America's AI Action Plan. The authority states that results will support development of new recommendations and updates to existing guidelines, with the aim of strengthening ecosystem readiness and supporting national and global alignment on AI incident management.

The workshop targets AI developers, service providers, incident responders, critical infrastructure partners, academics, cybersecurity professionals, and government stakeholders. The event runs from 8:30 am to 5:30 pm EDT on both days. NIST has arranged group accommodation at the Gaithersburg Marriott Washingtonian Center at $276 USD per night, with booking available until 26 April 2026. Non-US citizens must present a valid passport, US permanent residents must present a valid green card, and US citizens must present REAL ID-compliant identification for campus access.
recwLYHHyovMQ8RUE.body: National Institute of Standards and Technology

rec6w8RalmSU253iG.orig_title: NIST Workshop on AI Incident Management
rec6w8RalmSU253iG.gen_title: NIST convenes workshop on AI incident management and response standards
rec6w8RalmSU253iG.summary:
On 13–14 May 2026, the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), within the U.S. Department of Commerce, will host a workshop on artificial intelligence incident management at its Gaithersburg facility. The workshop aims to establish coordinated approaches to managing incidents in which AI systems act as both targets and sources of risk, building on collaborative efforts across government, industry, and academia.

NIST states that as AI systems become integral to critical infrastructure and national security, a new class of incidents is emerging that requires scalable, mission-aligned response approaches. The workshop will present a high-level NIST roadmap to advance AI incident response standards and practices, engage stakeholders to understand current approaches including existing playbooks and frameworks, and explore definitions, lifecycles, and taxonomy of AI-related incidents. NIST also notes that the workshop will identify gaps in existing cybersecurity and AI risk management guidance and highlight emerging AI incident types beyond cybersecurity, including misuse scenarios.

The workshop is designed to convene leaders from the AI and cybersecurity stakeholder communities. Invited participants include AI developers, service providers, incident responders, critical infrastructure partners, academics, cybersecurity professionals, and government stakeholders. NIST states that outcomes from the workshop will inform future guidelines and recommendations developed by its Information Technology Laboratory and Center for AI Standards and Innovation, supporting implementation of America's AI Action Plan. The findings are intended to strengthen ecosystem readiness and support national and global alignment on AI incident management practices.

NIST will use stakeholder input to inform updates to existing guidelines and the development of new recommendations addressing the evolving landscape of AI-related incidents.
rec6w8RalmSU253iG.body: US Center for AI Standards and Innovation

rec4h7BGGWpmpeMjb.gen_title: Ecuador's data protection authority issues general regulation on biometric data processing
rec4h7BGGWpmpeMjb.summary:
On 31 March 2026, the Superintendencia de Protección de Datos Personales (SPDP) of Ecuador issued Resolution N° SPDP-SPD-2026-00[**]-R establishing a general regulation for the processing of biometric data.

The SPDP acted pursuant to its statutory authority under the Ley Orgánica de Protección de Datos Personales (LOPDP) to emit normative acts necessary for guaranteeing the right to personal data protection. The SPDP recognises that biometric data, defined under Article 4 of the LOPDP as unique personal data relating to physical, physiological or behavioural characteristics permitting or confirming unique identification, constitute sensitive data categories under Article 25(a) of the LOPDP. The authority notes that such data possess information with special weight, and their improper processing may give rise to discrimination and may threaten fundamental rights and individual freedoms.

The SPDP determines that the growing use of biometric technologies in public and private services generates specific risks to fundamental rights and individual freedoms, including algorithmic discrimination, improper identification and data reuse for incompatible purposes. The authority finds it necessary to establish specific criteria for biometric data processing, attending to their special nature and associated utilisation risks.

The regulation was developed by the Intendencia General de Regulación de Protección de Datos Personales (IRD), the SPDP's regulatory division, in accordance with its mandate under the organisational resolution N° SPDP-SPDP-2024-0001-R (as amended by resolution N° SPDP-IRD-2025-0028-R of 30 July 2025) to propose and elaborate normative acts necessary for the SPDP's exercise of its competencies and attribution of powers.

The resolution grounds itself in Ecuador's constitutional protections for personal data (Art. 66(19) and Art. 92 of the Constitución de la República del Ecuador), which guarantee citizens the right to know of the existence of their personal data in public or private files and to request free access, updating, rectification, deletion or annulment, particularly concerning sensitive data, with adoption of necessary security measures.
rec4h7BGGWpmpeMjb.body: Superintendencia de Proteccion de Datos Personales

recE9ZPyphOeQhzPL.orig_title: 关于对《网络安全标准实践指南——OpenClaw类智能体部署使用安全指引（征求意见稿）》公开征求意见的通知
recE9ZPyphOeQhzPL.gen_title: China's TC 260 opens public consultation on OpenClaw-class intelligent agent deployment security guidance
recE9ZPyphOeQhzPL.summary:
On 31 March 2026, the National Technical Committee 260 on Information Security Standardisation (TC 260) opened a public consultation period on a draft cybersecurity standard practice guide addressing the safe deployment and use of OpenClaw-class intelligent agents.

TC 260's secretariat issued the consultation notice (document reference: 网安秘 字[2026]33号) to solicit feedback on the draft "Cybersecurity Standard Practice Guide: Security Guidance for OpenClaw-class Intelligent Agent Deployment and Use" (征求意见稿). The secretariat states that the guidance has been prepared to mitigate security risks associated with deploying and using OpenClaw-class intelligent agents and to enhance management capabilities for their deployment and use.

The consultation period runs until 15 April 2026. TC 260's secretariat is inviting all relevant organisations and stakeholders to submit comments and recommendations to inform the final version of the guidance. Feedback should be directed to the secretariat contact, Wang Hansheng, via telephone (010-64102730) or email (wanghs@cesi.cn).

The draft guidance document has been made publicly available for review. The preparation and consultation process follows the procedures set out in the "File Management Methods for Cybersecurity Standard Practice Guides" established by the National Technical Committee 260 on Information Security Standardisation. This procedural framework requires that draft practice guides undergo formal public consultation before finalisation. The secretariat is based at the China Electronics Technology Standardisation Research Institute (中国电子技术标准化研究院) in Beijing.
recE9ZPyphOeQhzPL.body: National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization

rec91WAvSc4I5XHaX.gen_title: ICO updates automated decision-making guidance following Data (Use and Access) Act 2025
rec91WAvSc4I5XHaX.summary:
On 31 March 2026, the Information Commissioner's Office published updated draft guidance on automated decision-making, including profiling, to reflect amendments to the UK GDPR introduced by the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 (DUAA). The ICO states that the guidance addresses article 22A provisions relating to solely automated decisions with significant effects (Art. 22A UK GDPR).

The ICO has clarified the scope of automated decision-making as processing that is "based solely on automated processing", including profiling with no meaningful human involvement, and produces a "legal or similarly significant effect" on a person. The authority has added content to help organisations determine whether their processing falls within the scope of these provisions. The guidance specifically defines "ADM provisions" as articles 22A–22D of the UK GDPR (Arts. 22A–22D UK GDPR).

The ICO notes that it has clarified when organisations' ability to undertake automated decision-making faces certain restrictions and what conditions must be satisfied in these cases. The authority has also created a new section outlining the safeguards organisations must put in place, as well as the rights individuals have regarding automated decision-making that affects them. The ICO states that this detailed guidance aims to help organisations understand and meet their obligations when carrying out automated decision-making and explains the relevant provisions, the ICO's interpretation, and recommendations for compliance.

The ICO confirms the guidance is currently in draft form and is consulting on the updates. The authority will withdraw the previous version of the automated decision-making guidance once the updated guidance is finalised following the consultation. The ICO notes that this guidance will inform the statutory code of practice on artificial intelligence and automated decision-making that it will develop. The guidance is aimed at data protection officers, compliance professionals, and technical leads overseeing organisations' use or procurement of automated decision-making systems, whether in-house-developed or offered by external vendors.

recOqgHnsJTHb1JXA.orig_title: BEREC Provides Early Assessment of the Digital Networks Act, Welcoming Ambition While Highlighting Areas for Improvement
recOqgHnsJTHb1JXA.gen_title: BEREC assesses Digital Networks Act proposal, identifying concerns on centralisation and market contestability
recOqgHnsJTHb1JXA.summary:
On 30 March 2026, the Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications (BEREC) published its early assessment of the European Commission's proposal for a Digital Networks Act (DNA). BEREC welcomes the overarching ambition to modernise the regulatory framework for Europe's connectivity sector and to support resilient, sustainable and high quality digital networks. At the same time, BEREC identifies important areas where further refinement is needed to ensure that the DNA strengthens, rather than complicates, Europe's internal market for electronic communications.

BEREC welcomes the updated objectives introduced by the DNA, including resilience and sustainability alongside existing objectives from the European Electronic Communications Code. However, BEREC finds that the proposal introduces elements of greater centralisation that require further clarification to ensure they contribute effectively to a well functioning internal market. BEREC recognises the Commission's intention to streamline the General Authorisation procedures but notes that the proposed Single Passport mechanism raises concerns from several perspectives. The authority considers that by concentrating significant enforcement responsibilities in the Member State of first notification, the system risks forum shopping, uneven supervisory burdens and slower enforcement. BEREC determines that the proposal could introduce greater operational complexity, administrative burden and legal uncertainty without offering clear benefits over the current straightforward authorisation regime.

BEREC also notes that the DNA proposes major changes to spectrum management, including treating spectrum as an EU level resource and introducing common rules for fees and long duration rights. The authority warns that the proposed single market procedure, which includes a Commission veto, signifies a notable trend towards centralisation. BEREC cautions that some proposals, particularly the introduction of unlimited licence duration and automatic renewals, risk favouring existing rights holders and reducing market contestability. On numbering resources, BEREC finds that treating these as a common Union asset introduces a more centralised approach, though the authority sees potential benefits in greater harmonisation. BEREC also finds that key concepts require further clarification and that additional analysis of demand, use cases and operational impacts would help ensure alignment with the needs of end users, operators and regulators.

Regarding competition and market regulation, BEREC welcomes the preservation of the Significant Market Power regime and continued availability of symmetric access tools. However, the authority determines that several DNA proposals, including new sequencing rules for remedies and expanded Commission veto powers, could reduce the ability of national regulatory authorities to address national market needs. BEREC also welcomes the DNA's proposal to clarify and harmonise the core tasks of national regulatory authorities, highlighting the importance of safeguarding their independence and that of BEREC itself, stressing that proposed institutional changes must not dilute existing technical expertise and autonomy.
recOqgHnsJTHb1JXA.body: Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications

recLhewkEvhAkeLDp.gen_title: Mecklenburg-Vorpommern LfDI and LKA warn of extortion targeting BDH Klinik Greifswald cyberattack victims
recLhewkEvhAkeLDp.summary:
On 26 March 2026, the Landesdatenschutzbeauftragten für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit Mecklenburg-Vorpommern (LfDI MV) and the Landeskriminalamt Mecklenburg-Vorpommern issued a joint statement warning former patients of BDH Klinik Greifswald of ongoing extortion attempts following a cyberattack in February 2026.

The LfDI MV and LKA MV report that former patients are being contacted unlawfully by email by perpetrators using patient data stolen during the February 2026 cyberattack. The authorities state that the perpetrators are using the compromised patient records to exert pressure on those affected through extortion.

The LfDI MV and LKA MV advise affected individuals to exercise caution and observe specific precautions. The authorities recommend that recipients remain sceptical and refrain from responding to communications from perpetrators via email, telephone, messaging applications or other channels, as doing so would confirm contact details and make individuals further accessible to extortionists. The authorities warn against making any payments to perpetrators in response to financial demands, noting that many patient records have already been published and that payment to criminals does not guarantee deletion of unlawfully published data. The authorities further note that payments typically result in additional demands from the same or other criminal actors who may have received the data.

The authorities advise affected individuals not to open email attachments or click links in email messages, as these are common attack vectors for executing or downloading malware. The LfDI MV and LKA MV direct those who receive extortion emails to file a report through the online reporting portal of Landespolizei Mecklenburg-Vorpommern at https://portal.onlinewache.polizei.de/, attaching the perpetrator's email message where possible, or to report the matter at any local police station.

The authorities state that further information regarding the criminal investigation cannot be released on grounds of investigative tactics, and direct inquiries to the press office of the Staatsanwaltschaft Rostock.

recmXpn5oneDndWmh.orig_title: Cyber Europe 2026: Διήμερη άσκηση τον Ιούνιο για την ενίσχυση της κυβερνοανθεκτικότητας υποδομών των μεταφορών
recmXpn5oneDndWmh.gen_title: Greece to participate in Cyber Europe 2026 exercise focused on transport infrastructure resilience
recmXpn5oneDndWmh.summary:
On 27 March 2026, the National Cyber Security Authority Greece announced that it will participate in Cyber Europe 2026, a two-day exercise scheduled for June 2026 designed to strengthen the collective capacity of European Union member states to prevent, detect and respond to complex cyber threats. The exercise is organised under the auspices of the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA), in collaboration with national cyber security authorities of member states and competent European bodies.

The Cyber Europe 2026 exercise focuses on cyber crises affecting the transport sector, with particular emphasis on critical infrastructure including ports and railway networks. The National Cyber Security Authority Greece states that the exercise constitutes an important cooperation platform where participants will have the opportunity to strengthen their capabilities and prepare effectively by addressing challenge scenarios in the continuously evolving field of cyber security.

The National Cyber Security Authority Greece confirms its active participation in the planning and implementation of the exercise at national level, demonstrating its commitment to strengthening European cyber resilience. Through its participation, the authority contributes to the development and implementation of strategies that ensure the protection of critical infrastructure, such as transport, and enhance the overall security of Greece and the European Union.

reckM1SuRU4s7gSlY.orig_title: SIOPE+ Gruppo di Lavoro "Regole Tecniche OPI"
reckM1SuRU4s7gSlY.gen_title: Agenzia per l'Italia Digitale publishes technical rules roadmap for OPI standard modifications
reckM1SuRU4s7gSlY.summary:
On 30 March 2026, the Agenzia per l'Italia Digitale (AgID) published a planning summary for modifications to the OPI (Ordinativi di Pagamento e Incasso) standard, detailing scheduled changes to XSD schemas and technical rules across ten implementation lots spanning from March 2018 to November 2026.

The roadmap, titled "Sintesi della Pianificazione degli interventi di modifica dello Standard OPI", sets out 55 numbered modifications grouped into batches with designated testing and implementation dates. AgID specifies that the first lot, comprising urgent modifications to XSD schemas version 1.2.4a, includes improvements to invoice handling, cash journal pagination, and treatment of invoices following ANAC determinations, with testing scheduled for 26 March 2018 and implementation for 1 April 2018.

Subsequent lots progress through schema versions 1.3.1, 1.4.1, 1.5.1, 1.6.0, 1.6.1, 1.6.2, 1.7.1, and 1.8.0 through 1.8.1. Modifications include enhancements to invoice handling, cash journal management, payment code formats, multiple beneficiary operations, liquidity reporting, and regulatory alignment. AgID notes that lot 4, containing only cards 20, 21 and 22, applies schema version 1.4.1 to those specific modifications. Lot 8, implemented from 1 October 2024 to 31 December 2024, addresses rationalisations to regularisation procedures, credit account valuation for inter-entity transfers under unified treasury regimes, F24EP payments, mandates expiring at financial year-end under article 219 TUEL, suspended debt clearances with interest refunds, liquidity availability management, and balance structure repeatability.

The most recent modifications, grouped in lots 9 and 10, encompass instant payments implementation (testing from 7 July 2025 to 9 October 2025), stamp duty treatment on provincial treasury accreditation operations, aggregation of amounts under identical SIOPE management codes, mandatory field population requirements, and beneficiary verification procedures for ordinary and instant transfers (testing from 1 October 2026 to 30 November 2026). AgID structures each modification with distinct testing and exercise phases, allowing public administrations phased adoption of revised technical requirements.

recaVgqXLpU0tijhE.orig_title: Andmekaitse Inspektsioon: oleme teadlikud veebilehest ettevaatust.ee
recaVgqXLpU0tijhE.gen_title: Estonia's Data Protection Inspectorate opens own-motion investigation into ettevaatust.ee website
recaVgqXLpU0tijhE.summary:
The Data Protection Inspectorate of Estonia (Andmekaitse Inspektsioon) has initiated an own-motion supervisory procedure into the website ettevaatust.ee, which publishes personal data alongside references to court decisions.

On 27 March 2026, the Andmekaitse Inspektsioon announced it is aware of the website ettevaatust.ee and has commenced a self-initiated investigation. The inspectorate stated that it will determine all further action holistically with respect to the entire website and will not open separate supervisory proceedings based on individual complaints submitted by data subjects.

The inspectorate clarified that individuals whose personal data have been made public through the site retain their rights. Data subjects may submit objections or other requests directly to the website operator or pursue remedies in court. The authority noted that complaints regarding the disclosure of personal data must first be directed to the data controller, namely the operator of the website, rather than to the regulatory authority itself.

The inspectorate emphasised that data controllers bear the obligation and responsibility to explain the lawful basis for processing personal data, to detail the conditions under which data protection safeguards are applied, and to ensure that individuals' rights are respected and exercisable. The inspectorate stated that data controllers are required to respond to requests without undue delay and in any event within one month. The inspectorate directed individuals to its website for information on rights including access to personal data, rectification of personal data, erasure of personal data, and the right to object to processing. The authority has not announced a timeline for completion of its investigation into the website or indicated what enforcement actions may result from the supervisory procedure.

rec3KwPIxJPMiYDzJ.orig_title: Andmekaitse Inspektsioon avaldas 2025. aasta aastaraamatu: rekordarv kaebuseid ja kasvavad ootused digiajastul
rec3KwPIxJPMiYDzJ.gen_title: Estonia's Data Protection Inspectorate publishes 2025 annual report recording highest complaints intake
rec3KwPIxJPMiYDzJ.summary:
Estonia's Andmekaitse Inspektsioon (Data Protection Inspectorate) has published its 2025 annual report documenting record numbers of complaints and evolving data protection demands in the digital age.

On 31 March 2026, the Andmekaitse Inspektsioon released the annual report, which Chief Director Pille Lehis indicates reflects the authority's operation within an increasingly complex legal environment shaped by new European Union digital regulations that expand the inspectorate's responsibilities and accountability.

The inspectorate records that it received the highest number of complaints in its history during 2025, a figure the authority finds reflects growing public awareness and heightened expectations that the state will respond to data protection breaches more promptly and consistently. Alongside complaint processing, the Andmekaitse Inspektsioon expanded preventative work, organising regional training sessions, developing data protection networks, and focusing on data protection practices in education and artificial intelligence use in schools.

The annual report highlights key supervisory themes identified by the authority, including employee monitoring, legal frameworks for alcohol testing, and municipal document registers where the authority has identified continued unlawful disclosure of sensitive personal data. The inspectorate notes the Apotheka client data breach as a significant case, which the authority finds resulted in an out-of-court settlement concluded in 2025 with a record penalty and revealed serious shortcomings in security measure implementation.

The authority emphasises that data protection extends beyond technical compliance requirements, constituting instead a matter of trust and transparency. The Andmekaitse Inspektsioon states that its strategic focus for the coming period centres on risk-based approaches and strengthened preventative action to better protect individual rights in the rapidly evolving digital environment.

recVoYFm9mmP0i2BQ.gen_title: Italian DPA finds unlawful marketing communications and access rights breach by Fix It s.r.l.s.
recVoYFm9mmP0i2BQ.summary:
On 26 March 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali (Italian Data Protection Authority) found that Fix It s.r.l.s., operator of the platform www.megamta.com and based in Borgo Veneto, Padua, violated the GDPR and Italian data protection law by sending unauthorised marketing communications and failing to respond to a data subject access request.

The authority initiated enforcement proceedings on 26 January 2026 following a complaint filed on 27 September 2025. The complainant reported receiving an unsolicited marketing email and stated that the company failed to respond to a data access request submitted on 30 July 2025 under Article 15 GDPR. The authority found violations of Article 5(1)(a) and 5(2) GDPR, Article 6 GDPR, Articles 12 and 15 GDPR, and Article 130 of the Italian Data Protection Code (d.lgs. 30 giugno 2003, n. 196).

In its defence, Fix It claimed it had not received the access request email and therefore could not respond. The company stated that data subjects could access their personal data 24/7 through their account on the MegaMta.com portal. The company also noted the complainant unsubscribed on 21 September 2025, after which no further marketing communications were sent. The authority found these arguments insufficient. The Garante determined that Fix It failed to provide a specific legal basis for the marketing treatment, merely asserting generically that the platform sends communications only to recipients who accepted receipt. The authority found this declaration inadequate to demonstrate valid consent or any lawful basis for processing. The Garante states that without specific evidence of a lawful ground, the data processing must be considered unlawful. The authority also found the company breached Articles 5(1)(a) and 5(2) GDPR, which require processing to be lawful and fair, with controllers able to demonstrate compliance with applicable lawfulness conditions.
recVoYFm9mmP0i2BQ.body: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

recVtLdEQR8DSE7Gp.orig_title: More attacks, more reporting: Belgium’s cyber reality in 2025
recVtLdEQR8DSE7Gp.gen_title: Belgium records 635 cyber incidents in 2025, up 70% year-on-year
recVtLdEQR8DSE7Gp.summary:
The Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB) recorded 635 incident notifications in 2025, representing a 70% increase compared to 2024, with 556 classified as cyber-related incidents marking a 58% rise year-on-year. The CCB attributes the rise to stricter reporting requirements under NIS2 and DORA alongside improved detection capabilities, though the figures also reflect sustained and growing pressure on organisations across all sectors.

On 26 March 2026, the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium published its cyber threat landscape analysis for 2025. The CCB finds that account compromise remains the most frequently reported incident type, with 144 cases largely driven by credential theft and abuse. The Safeonweb "BePhish" platform processed nearly 10 million suspicious emails in 2025, and the CCB observes that attackers are evolving techniques by combining email with messaging platforms, deploying CEO fraud scenarios, and introducing "hands-on-keyboard" tactics such as ClickFix and FileFix to trick users into executing malicious commands.

Ransomware incidents remained relatively stable at 105 cases in 2025 compared to 109 in 2024, but the CCB determines that their impact has intensified as attackers increasingly combine data encryption, data exfiltration, and escalating extortion tactics. The authority identifies Qilin, Akira, and Clop among the most active groups targeting Belgian organisations following the takedown of LockBit. Additionally, the CCB notes that Belgium was among European countries most frequently targeted by pro-Russian hacktivist groups through Distributed Denial-of-Service attacks, with Group NoName057(16) launching five coordinated campaigns in 2025. Despite the frequency of these attacks, the CCB finds that their real-world impact remained limited thanks to the CCB's "Red Button" coordination procedure enabling rapid response coordination between victims, Internet Service Providers, hosting providers, and authorities.

The CCB records a significant acceleration in attack speed, finding that the average time-to-exploit window between vulnerability disclosure and active exploitation dropped to five days in 2025, with nearly one-third of vulnerabilities exploited within 24 hours. The authority also identifies supply chain incidents and credential theft as systemic risks, noting that malware trends show credential theft is increasingly central to intrusions. In response, the CCB conducted 103 emergency response operations, issued 32,005 targeted "spear warnings" representing a 42% increase, and expanded awareness initiatives including 15 Connect & Share programme sessions reaching over 13,000 participants.

recUmPpgH0ShgBo2N.orig_title: Judgment of the Court (Third Chamber) of 26 March 2026. — Proceedings brought by FP and LD. — Request for a preliminary ruling from the Cour d'appel de Bruxelles. — Reference for a preliminary ruling – Area of freedom, security and justice – Judicial
recUmPpgH0ShgBo2N.gen_title: Court of Justice rules Directive 2012/29/EU inapplicable to crimes against humanity prosecuted under extraterritorial jurisdiction
recUmPpgH0ShgBo2N.summary:
On 26 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union (Third Chamber) ruled on the scope of Directive 2012/29/EU (the Victims' Rights Directive) in relation to extraterritorial criminal jurisdiction, determining that the directive does not apply to proceedings where Member States exercise extraterritorial jurisdiction over crimes allegedly committed outside Union territory.

The case arose from proceedings initiated in Belgium by FP and LD against leaders of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia for crimes against humanity allegedly committed against their sister, SX, a Saudi national and women's rights activist. The Cour d'appel de Bruxelles referred the question of whether Article 2(1)(a) of Directive 2012/29/EU applied to victims of crimes prosecuted under Belgium's extraterritorial jurisdiction.

The Court found that Directive 2012/29/EU, adopted under Article 82(2) TFEU to establish minimum standards on victims' rights in criminal matters with a cross-border dimension, is limited in its territorial scope by its own terms. The Court determined that Recital 13 of the directive explicitly states: 'This Directive applies in relation to criminal offences committed in the Union and to criminal proceedings that take place in the Union. It confers rights on victims of extra-territorial offences only in relation to criminal proceedings that take place in the Union.' The Court concluded that this language restricts the directive's application to proceedings concerning crimes committed within the Union's territory, or alternatively, establishes that victim protections under the directive apply only where both the crime occurs within the Union and proceedings take place within the Union.

The Court noted that Recital 50 further provides that the directive's obligations do not affect Member States' competence to institute proceedings under their own jurisdiction rules, and remain without prejudice to conflict-of-laws provisions governing the exercise of jurisdiction. The Court held that the directive's scope rationale reflects the legislative purpose to facilitate mutual recognition of judgments in cross-border criminal matters within the Union, a purpose not engaged by extraterritorial prosecutions of crimes committed outside Union territory.
recUmPpgH0ShgBo2N.body: Court of Justice of the European Union

recoVtIpgnnOZsAgu.orig_title: NEWSLETTER del 26 marzo 2026 - Telemarketing, il Garante privacy sanziona Enel Energia per oltre 500mila euro - Annunci online senza consenso, il Garante sanziona Bakeca - Recupero crediti, Garante privacy: illecita la comunicazione ai familiari del debitore - Troppi rischi su siti e app per i minori: l’indagine del GPEN
recoVtIpgnnOZsAgu.gen_title: Italian DPA fines Enel Energia €563,052 for unlawful telemarketing and Bakeca €5,000 for unpermitted data publication
recoVtIpgnnOZsAgu.summary:
On 26 March 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali (Italian Data Protection Authority) issued multiple enforcement actions addressing unlawful personal data processing across telemarketing, online advertising and debt recovery sectors.

The Garante imposed a fine of €563,052 (approximately £480,000) on Enel Energia for processing customer data for telemarketing and teleselling purposes without a lawful basis. The Authority found that Enel Energia, including through third-party entities, submitted additional commercial offers to customers during purely administrative contacts in the absence of appropriate legal grounds. The Garante determined that proposals were made even when customers had expressly refused consent for marketing purposes (GDPR Art. 6). The Authority also identified deficiencies in technical and organisational measures adopted to prevent unlawful processing during user recontact procedures. The Garante stressed that controllers must adopt consent acquisition methods capable of verifying the identity of data subjects and ensuring the lawful provenance of personal data, including through double opt-in processes requiring active user confirmation to receive promotional communications. The Authority ordered Enel Energia to implement adequate measures to ensure personal data processing complies with GDPR requirements throughout the entire processing chain.

The Garante also fined Bakeca srl €5,000 for unlawfully processing a woman's telephone number and work location, which had been inserted without her knowledge into advertisements in sensitive categories including "massages-wellness" and "dating-encounters", with one advertisement containing explicit intimate expressions. The woman discovered the advertisements only after receiving unsolicited calls from strangers. The Garante found the processing lacked a valid legal basis and was non-compliant with principles of fairness and security. The Authority identified inadequate technical and organisational measures preventing misuse of data, noting the portal permitted publication of information relating to third parties without verifying ownership or legitimate use. The Garante ordered Bakeca srl to adopt appropriate technical and organisational measures to verify that users inserting contact information in advertisements are legitimately entitled to do so.

The Garante also determined unlawful the disclosure by debt recovery company Sagitter spa of information regarding a debtor's existence, amount and payment modalities to family members, mortgage co-owners, colleagues and neighbours, reaffirming that communications by credit recovery entities to persons unrelated to the credit relationship lack appropriate legal basis.
recoVtIpgnnOZsAgu.body: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

recHeqfjhNL9HoxBf.gen_title: Italian DPA finds Intesa Sanpaolo understated scope of employee data breach affecting 3,572 customers
recHeqfjhNL9HoxBf.summary:
On 26 March 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali issued a decision following investigation into a personal data breach notification submitted by Intesa Sanpaolo S.p.A. on 17 July 2024. The DPA determined that the bank's initial notification significantly underestimated the breach's extent, which resulted from unauthorised access by a bank employee to customer financial data without professional justification between 21 February 2022 and 24 April 2024.

Intesa Sanpaolo's original notification to the DPA indicated that nine individuals were affected by the breach, comprising customers, employees and former employees of the bank. However, the bank stated it would not assess the breach as presenting elevated risks to data subjects' rights and freedoms. The bank subsequently submitted a supplementary notification on 30 August 2024 disclosing that it had dismissed the employee for gross misconduct on 7 August 2024.

Following press reports in early October 2024 describing unauthorised access to banking information for a significant number of customers, the DPA initiated a detailed investigation. This investigation revealed that the actual scope of the breach was substantially wider than reported in the original and supplementary notifications. The DPA issued an information request on 10 October 2024 under article 157 of the National Data Protection Code. In its response of 17 October 2024, Intesa Sanpaolo confirmed that the breach involved loss of confidentiality due solely to unjustified access by the employee acting outside his professional duties. The bank further disclosed that it first detected the anomalous access on 9 October 2023 through an alert generated by monitoring systems implemented in compliance with the DPA's May 2011 Decision No. 192 on information circulation and traceability in banking operations.

The DPA's decision applies articles 33 and 34 of the GDPR, concerning breach notification to authorities and affected individuals respectively, and references the EDPB Guidelines 9/2022 on breach notification and Guidelines 01/2021 on breach notification examples.
recHeqfjhNL9HoxBf.body: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rec7PTAuAYQwqejCV.orig_title: Parere su uno schema di decreto legislativo di recepimento della direttiva (UE) 2024/1640 (VI Direttiva Antiriciclaggio – AMLD6), in materia di accesso alle informazioni sulla titolarità effettiva - 26 marzo 2026 [10241495]
rec7PTAuAYQwqejCV.gen_title: Italian Data Protection Authority issues opinion on legislative decree transposing EU Anti-Money Laundering Directive 6 on beneficial ownership access
rec7PTAuAYQwqejCV.summary:
On 26 March 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali (Italian Data Protection Authority) issued an opinion on a draft legislative decree to transpose Directive (EU) 2024/1640 (the Sixth Anti-Money Laundering Directive, AMLD6) concerning access to beneficial ownership information.

The Italian Presidency of the Council of Ministers requested the opinion under Article 36(4) GDPR and Article 154(5) of Italy's Personal Data Protection Code (Legislative Decree 196/2003). The draft decree implements delegating legislation in Law 91/2025 of 3 June 2025 and seeks to balance anti-money laundering requirements with individual rights, including data protection.

The Authority notes that the draft decree aims to conform to principles established by the Court of Justice of the European Union in its judgment of 22 November 2022 (Cases C-37/20 and C-601/20). The Court found indiscriminate public access to beneficial ownership data incompatible with the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union, determining it a serious and disproportionate interference with rights to private life and personal data protection. The Court required Member States to adopt selective access models based on qualified and verifiable interest.

The draft decree substantively amends Legislative Decree 231/2007, redesigning the access regime for beneficial ownership information in the Business Register. Article 21-bis establishes a reserved regime for competent authorities, granting them immediate, unfiltered, direct and free access through dedicated electronic systems without prior notice to beneficial owners. The Authority finds the list of authorised authorities exhaustive, including the Ministry of Economy and Finance, sectoral supervisory authorities (Bank of Italy, IVASS, CONSOB), the Financial Intelligence Unit (UIF), the Directorate for Anti-Mafia Investigations (DIA), the Financial Police, the National Anti-Mafia Prosecutor's Office, judicial authorities and tax authorities. Article 21-ter regulates access for obligated subjects, including banks and professionals, through prior electronic accreditation with the Chamber of Commerce. The Authority acknowledges pending preliminary references before the Court of Justice (Cases C-684/24 and C-685/24) concerning compatibility with Articles 7 and 8 of the Charter and Article 8 ECHR regarding the undefined notion of "legitimate interest" for beneficial ownership access.
rec7PTAuAYQwqejCV.body: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rec6EIcGX7z7h0gj3.gen_title: Italian Data Protection Authority closes investigation into GP's refusal of deletion request after disciplinary proceedings archived
rec6EIcGX7z7h0gj3.summary:
On 26 March 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali (Italian Data Protection Authority) issued Decision No. 205, finding that a general practitioner's refusal to delete a patient's personal data was justified under the legal basis permitting retention for potential legal disputes, whilst requiring correction of incomplete information notices.

The investigation arose from a patient complaint alleging the GP had failed to comply with a deletion request submitted on an unspecified date, claiming retention was necessary pending expiry of the patient's prescription periods and potential litigation. The patient also objected that the privacy notice provided under Art. 13 GDPR lacked required information elements.

The Garante finds that regarding the deletion refusal, the GP's stated intention to retain data for management of potential disputes fell within Art. 17(2)(e) GDPR and Art. 9(2)(f) GDPR, providing lawful grounds for maintaining records. This assessment was made particularly in light of a parallel disciplinary procedure initiated by ULSS 8 Berica (the local health authority) following the patient's complaint to the health provider's administrative office. The health authority subsequently archived that disciplinary proceeding under Art. 25 of the Collective Agreement for General Practitioners.

However, the Garante determined the GP's privacy notice contained material deficiencies. The authority identified gaps in: the identity or categories of data recipients (Art. 13(1)(e) GDPR); data retention periods (Art. 13(2)(a) GDPR); and data subject rights including access, rectification, erasure, processing restriction, objection and portability (Art. 13(2)(b) GDPR). The notice also failed to specify the right to lodge complaints with the Garante or pursue judicial remedies against the authority or controller.

Following the Garante's information requests under Art. 157 of the Italian Data Protection Code (d.lgs. 30 June 2003, No. 196), the GP submitted an updated privacy notice. The revised version added required details on data recipients at section 3, data retention periods at section 4, and enumerated data subject rights at section 5, including complaint and judicial remedy provisions.

The Garante issued formal notice of violation under Art. 166(5) of the Code, inviting the GP to submit defensive arguments or supporting documentation or request a hearing, as required by Art. 166(6)-(7) of the Code and Art. 18(1) of Law No. 689/1981.
rec6EIcGX7z7h0gj3.body: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

recjScVyl6eBjIuBp.gen_title: Italian DPA opens enforcement proceedings against Il Gazzettino over journalist's naming of doctor in published article
recjScVyl6eBjIuBp.summary:
On 26 March 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali (Italian DPA) initiated enforcement proceedings against Il Gazzettino S.p.A. following a complaint by a general practitioner regarding the publication of an article naming her in connection with disputes with patients.

On 26 March 2026, the Italian DPA, in a meeting chaired by President Pasquale Stanzione, issued decision no. 216/2026 (doc. web n. 10241926) opening proceedings under article 58(2) of the GDPR against the publisher Il Gazzettino S.p.A. The DPA finds that the publisher failed to respond to a request for exercise of data subject rights submitted by the complainant on 29 July 2025, in breach of articles 5(2), 12(2) and (3), and 24(1) and (2) of the GDPR.

The complainant, a general practitioner, lodged a complaint under article 77 of the GDPR regarding a newspaper article published by Il Gazzettino in both print and digital format. The article, relating to disputes between the doctor and her patients, mentioned the complainant's full name twice. The complainant stated she had sent a preliminary request to the publisher on 29 July 2025 seeking deletion of her personal data and de-indexing of the article but received no response. She subsequently filed a formal complaint asserting that the publication breached article 137(3) of the Italian Data Protection Code and the principles of lawfulness, fairness and data minimisation, and contravened journalistic deontological rules by including information not essential to the news value.

The DPA notes that by 31 October 2025, the publisher had removed the complainant's name from the archived digital version and withdrawn the article from online. The publisher attributed the delayed response to a combination of factors including use of certified email rather than the dedicated privacy contact address, timing during summer holiday period, and circumstances relating to the journalist's illness and death. The DPA states that whilst acknowledging the content's removal, it recalled the publisher's obligation to respect journalistic principles, particularly the principle of information essentiality, in exercising the right to report news under journalistic activity rules.
recjScVyl6eBjIuBp.body: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

recGGVMDl6EFYLHfu.gen_title: Italian Data Protection Authority orders municipal council to cease unlawful processing of health data
recGGVMDl6EFYLHfu.summary:
On 26 March 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali (Italian Data Protection Authority) adopted a decision against a municipal council for processing a general secretary's electronic health certificate without lawful basis, in violation of the GDPR (Arts. 5(1)(a) and (c), Art. 6, Art. 9) and the Italian Data Protection Code (Art. 2-ter).

The municipal council transmitted a full copy of the complainant's electronic sick leave certificate, obtained from the INPS database, to the Prefectura and to the municipal council's substitute general secretary, without proper authorisation. The authority found that the council failed to comply with the principles of lawfulness, fairness and transparency, and data minimisation (Arts. 5(1)(a) and (c) GDPR). The council's processing also lacked a valid legal basis under Article 6 GDPR and constituted unlawful processing of health data under Article 9 GDPR and Article 2-ter of the Italian Data Protection Code.

The Garante finds that while transmission to the Prefectura was justified on the ground that it manages the register of municipal secretaries and holds responsibility for employment relationships and personnel records, the transmission to the substitute general secretary's personal email address lacked proper authorisation. The Garante further notes that internal access to the certificate by unauthorised personnel, including a protocol officer and the secretariat office, violated data protection principles. Although the council argued the certificate contained only a generic attestation of absence without health-related information, the Garante determined that the document's classification as a health certificate and its attachment to correspondence concerning sick leave constituted processing of health data requiring appropriate legal safeguards.

The council contended the inclusion of the certificate resulted from an error committed by a temporarily reassigned employee responding to urgent administrative needs. The authority rejected this defence, finding that operational convenience does not justify processing personal data outside established procedures. The Garante initiated enforcement proceedings under Article 166(5) of the Italian Data Protection Code, affording the council opportunity to submit written defences and documentation in accordance with Article 166(6)-(7) of the Code and Article 18 of Law 689/1981.
recGGVMDl6EFYLHfu.body: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

reclN2vku2NOvaOVR.gen_title: Italian DPA finds violations of GDPR principles in Facebook publication of judicial decree
reclN2vku2NOvaOVR.summary:
On 26 March 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali (Italian Data Protection Authority) issued a decision following a complaint concerning the publication on Facebook of a judicial document relating to a criminal proceeding. The authority found violations of Articles 5(1)(a) and 5(1)(c) of Regulation (EU) 2016/679, Article 137 of Legislative Decree 30 June 2003 n. 196 (Italian Data Protection Code), and Article 6 of the Deontological Rules for Journalistic Data Processing.

On 30 July 2025, a complainant filed a formal complaint under Article 77 of the GDPR against Nicolò Cristaldi, who had published on his Facebook profile a judicial communication concerning a criminal decree of conviction issued by a court against the complainant for the offence of serious threat under Article 612(2) of the Italian Criminal Code. The complainant alleged the publication constituted unlawful processing of judicial data without a legal basis and was potentially damaging to his reputation.

The authority requested the data controller to provide observations on 9 September 2025 pursuant to Article 157 of the Data Protection Code. The authority found that the data controller failed to respond to this request, which constituted an autonomous administrative violation under Article 157 of the Code. A procedural notice was served on 22 January 2026, with assistance from the Guardia di Finanza's specialised privacy unit, contesting violations of the lawfulness, fairness and data minimisation principles under Articles 5(1)(a) and 5(1)(c) of the GDPR, as well as the principle of informational essentiality.

The data controller submitted defensive observations on 12 February 2026, claiming the initial information request email had been sent to his sole proprietorship company at an address managed by an external accountant who had deleted it without identifying the sender. The controller argued that his individual certified email address could have been easily identified through the INI-PEC register using his tax identification number. The authority's findings regarding violations of GDPR principles and the Italian Data Protection Code were recorded in decision n. 215 of 26 March 2026.
reclN2vku2NOvaOVR.body: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rec1DrTSzpRLMDXFg.orig_title: ZIMESALIA SIKU CHACHE KUFIKIA UKOMO WA TAASISI KUJISAJILI PDPC
rec1DrTSzpRLMDXFg.gen_title: Tanzania PDPC sets 8 April 2026 deadline for mandatory organisation registration under Personal Data Protection Act
rec1DrTSzpRLMDXFg.summary:
On 26 March 2026, the Personal Data Protection Commission (PDPC) of Tanzania announced a final deadline of 8 April 2026 for all public and private organisations to register under the Personal Data Protection Act, Chapter 44 of the laws of Tanzania, with enforcement and penalties to commence on 9 April 2026.

On 26 March 2026, the PDPC issued a final notice to all organisations collecting and processing personal data in Tanzania. The PDPC's Chief Executive, Dr Emmanuel Lameck Mkilia, stated that the government has provided sufficient time for registration since the Commission's launch in April 2024, with the deadline extended twice, most recently by the Minister of Communications and Information Technology to 8 April 2026.

The PDPC determines that from 9 April 2026, formal enforcement of the Personal Data Protection Act, Chapter 44 will commence. Any organisation found not to have registered with the PDPC system will be subject to legal liability. The PDPC states that penalties for individuals are fines up to Tanzanian Shilling 20 million or imprisonment up to ten years. For organisations and companies, the PDPC notes that penalties range from Tanzanian Shilling 1 million to Tanzanian Shilling 5 billion, depending on the nature of the breach. The Act applies across all critical sectors, the PDPC confirms, including government ministries and departments, banks, insurance companies, hospitals, schools, universities, telecommunications companies, industrial operations, tourism, hotels, transport companies, construction firms, political parties, trade unions, religious organisations, non-governmental organisations and other institutions.

Dr Mkilia emphasised that the Act protects citizens as data subjects by safeguarding the fundamental right to privacy. The PDPC considers the Act essential for building trust between service providers and customers, which it identifies as critical for developing Tanzania's digital economy and attracting investment.

recNb2QwuO5nI7KwK.orig_title: 关于组建人工智能安全标准工作组（WG9）和网络安全国际标准研究组（SG1）的通知
recNb2QwuO5nI7KwK.gen_title: China establishes AI safety standards working group and international cybersecurity standards research group
recNb2QwuO5nI7KwK.summary:
China's National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization has established a Working Group 9 (WG9) for artificial intelligence safety standards and Study Group 1 (SG1) for international cybersecurity standards, following approval by the committee's first directorial office meeting in 2026.

On 27 March 2026, the National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization announced the formation of the two groups to advance standardisation efforts in emerging technology risks and align domestic standards with international developments.

The AI Safety Standards Working Group (WG9) is assigned responsibility for investigating the current state and development trends of AI safety standards, researching and proposing an AI safety standards framework, and conducting research and formulation of AI safety standards. The group is chaired by Zhou Bowen, director of the Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory.
