Vice-chairs include Zhang Zhen (deputy director, National Computer Network Emergency Response Technical Team and Coordination Centre), Wei Kai (director, AI Research Institute at China Academy of Information and Communications Technology), Sheng Xiaobao (deputy director, Data Security Technology Research and Development Centre, Public Security Bureau Third Research Institute), and Hou Yuanwei (deputy director, China Information Security Assessment Centre).

The International Cybersecurity Standards Research Group (SG1) is tasked with tracking foreign cybersecurity standardisation work progress, conducting domestic and international cybersecurity standards comparative analysis, and organising research and formulation of China's international cybersecurity standards proposals. Lin Ning, a researcher at the China Electronics Technology Standardisation Research Institute, chairs the group. Vice-chairs include Shangguan Xiaoli (director, International Cooperation Department, China Electronics Technology Standardisation Research Institute), Hu Ying (deputy director, Cybersecurity Research Centre, China Electronics Technology Standardisation Research Institute), Chen Kai (chief cybersecurity standards officer, Huawei Technologies), and Li Tong (associate professor, Beijing University of Technology).

The committee noted that detailed member lists are contained in supporting documentation.
recNb2QwuO5nI7KwK.body: National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization

recB8HWPKQPQm3aYm.orig_title: SCADA 'in the cloud': new guidance for OT organisations
recB8HWPKQPQm3aYm.gen_title: NCSC publishes guidance on cloud-hosted SCADA for operational technology organisations
recB8HWPKQPQm3aYm.summary:
On 24 March 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) published new guidance on cloud-hosted Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) systems for operational technology organisations.

The NCSC notes that industry engagement has revealed a marked shift in attitudes towards cloud deployment for industrial applications. Where cloud solutions were previously dismissed due to perceived risks, operational technology organisations are increasingly considering cloud-based approaches. The NCSC states that this guidance is intended to support risk-informed decision-making on SCADA cloud migration, with cyber security as a central consideration.

The NCSC clarifies that the guidance does not prescribe whether cloud deployment is appropriate or inappropriate for any given organisation. Rather, the NCSC finds that cloud migration decisions must be grounded in each organisation's unique risk profile and specific technical requirements. The authority recognises that although cloud-hosted SCADA presents particular risks, securing cloud platforms represents a shared responsibility between operational technology and information technology functions. Consequently, the NCSC states that its new guidance should be used alongside existing cloud security guidance.

The NCSC identifies three critical assessment areas that organisations must evaluate before proceeding with SCADA cloud migration: understanding business drivers and cloud opportunities; organisational readiness; and technology and cloud solutions suitability. The authority notes that operational technology organisations must also address challenges including reliance on legacy equipment, on-premises infrastructure, and monolithic software packages, as these factors affect the feasibility and security of migration.

The NCSC states that organisations implementing cloud-hosted SCADA are required to reference the NCSC's guidance on using cloud services securely to ensure their cloud services are configured to meet their security requirements. The authority notes that the guidance was developed with input from industry bodies, critical national infrastructure operators, and SCADA vendors.

recnEIwp5V3KnFlxG.gen_title: German data protection authorities issue unified guidance on landlord tenant screening
recnEIwp5V3KnFlxG.summary:
On 24 March 2026, the Mecklenburg-Vorpommern state data protection authority (LfDI MV) and all German federal and state data protection authorities published coordinated guidance establishing uniform rules governing what personal information landlords may request from prospective tenants. The guidance, developed under the leadership of the North Rhine-Westphalia data protection authority (LDI NRW), creates nationwide legal certainty for both landlords and prospective tenants regarding permissible data collection during the rental process.

The ten-page guidance distinguishes between different stages of the rental process and specifies which data may be collected at each stage: during property viewings, during the pre-contractual phase, and after selection of a tenant. Sebastian Schmidt, the LfDI MV, emphasised that the guidance provides practical support for self-disclosure requests and establishes uniform implementation procedures across all German data protection authorities. The guidance applies the core test of whether the landlord's interest in the data is legitimate or whether collection is necessary for executing the tenancy agreement, balanced against the tenant's right to data protection (Art. 6 GDPR).

The authorities find that as the rental process progresses, more information may be requested. During property viewings, landlords are generally not permitted to request information about financial circumstances. When a prospective tenant applies to rent the property, they may be required to answer questions about insolvency proceedings. After selection, landlords may request evidence of creditworthiness, but only documentation specifically necessary for concluding the tenancy agreement. The guidance prohibits landlords from requesting comprehensive self-disclosures obtained from credit agencies, which frequently contain additional information beyond what is necessary. The authorities also clarify when collected data must be deleted.

Schmidt presented the guidance to industry representatives at the annual Regional Housing Industry Conference of the Northern German Housing Association on 25 March 2026 in Rostock, noting that the unified procedure clarifies data protection boundaries for implementing self-disclosure requests in practice.

recBr7lwJDCA3onRe.gen_title: Advocate General proposes Member States may exclude high-risk telecommunications equipment on national security grounds, subject to proportionality review
recBr7lwJDCA3onRe.summary:
On 19 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union published the Opinion of Advocate General Tamara Ćapeta in Case C-354/24, Elisa Eesti, concerning the exclusion of telecommunications equipment from 2G-4G and 5G networks on national security grounds.

The case arose following Estonia's 2022 decision to refuse authorisation to Elisa Eesti AS, an Estonian telecommunications provider, to use hardware and software from Chinese manufacturer Huawei in its telecommunications networks. The Estonian authorities determined that the equipment posed a risk to national security. The Administrative Court, Tallinn, referred preliminary ruling questions to the Court of Justice.

Advocate General Ćapeta proposes that Member States may, in principle, exclude hardware and software from telecommunications infrastructure where the manufacturer poses a risk to national security. However, the Advocate General finds that such measures must remain subject to full judicial review, including proportionality assessment. The Advocate General notes that whilst risk assessments may differ between third country equipment manufacturers and EU manufacturers, exclusion decisions cannot rest on generalised suspicion. Instead, they must involve specific assessment of the intended equipment and associated risks.

The Advocate General further observes that the European Electronic Communications Code (Directive (EU) 2018/1972) establishes security requirements for national telecommunications networks and services. The Advocate General finds that EU and Member State security interests converge under these provisions, permitting national authorities to draw on risk assessments undertaken by EU institutions and other national and EU bodies when making exclusion decisions.

Regarding property rights, the Advocate General determines that restrictions on use of equipment on security grounds constitute a limitation on property use under Article 17(1) of the Charter of Fundamental Rights, not a deprivation. The Advocate General finds that companies are not entitled to compensation unless the national court determines that the restriction burden is disproportionately heavy, even where the restriction is necessary.

The Advocate General's Opinion is non-binding. The Judges of the Court of Justice are now deliberating. Judgment will be delivered at a later date.
recBr7lwJDCA3onRe.body: Court of Justice of the European Union

recDDv7R5Y5tShjFy.orig_title: Notificación de brechas de datos personales a la Autoridad de Control | AEPD
recDDv7R5Y5tShjFy.gen_title: AEPD sets out mandatory notification requirements for personal data breaches
recDDv7R5Y5tShjFy.summary:
The Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD) has issued guidance establishing when organisations must notify the authority of personal data breaches affecting individuals in Spain, with notification required within 72 hours of discovery where a breach is likely to present a risk to rights and freedoms.

On 24 March 2026, the AEPD published notification requirements for personal data breaches under Article 33 of the General Data Protection Regulation. The AEPD defines a personal data breach as a security incident involving accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, or unauthorised communication or access to personal data processed by a controller. The authority notes that such breaches may cause considerable adverse effects on individuals, including physical, material or immaterial harm to rights and freedoms.

Controllers are required to assess the risk level of any breach and notify the AEPD when a breach is likely to present a risk to rights and freedoms. The AEPD states that notification must occur within 72 hours of the controller becoming aware of the breach (Art. 33 GDPR). Where the risk is high, controllers must also communicate the breach to affected individuals (Art. 34 GDPR). The AEPD finds that notification constitutes evidence of organisational diligence, whilst failure to comply with notification obligations is categorised as an infraction.

Notification requirements vary by entity type. In the private sector, controllers with their sole establishment in Spain, or with a main establishment in Spain among multiple EU establishments, must notify the AEPD. Controllers without an EU establishment need only notify if the breach affects individuals in Spain. Public sector organisations must generally notify the AEPD unless they fall within specific autonomous communities' jurisdiction. The AEPD requires all notifications to be submitted electronically via its electronic headquarters portal using the designated breach notification form to ensure compliance with Article 33.3 GDPR.

Where controllers consider no risk to rights and freedoms exists, the AEPD requires them to document all facts relating to the breach, its effects and corrective measures taken. This documentation enables the authority to verify compliance with Article 33 requirements. The AEPD offers risk assessment guidance through its ASESORA BRECHA tool and a notification guide to assist controllers in meeting their obligations.
recDDv7R5Y5tShjFy.body: Agencia Espanola de Proteccion de Datos

recXj9lypURHWOo17.gen_title: Court of Justice rules first data access request may be refused as abusive if made solely to generate compensation claims
recXj9lypURHWOo17.summary:
On 19 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union delivered judgment in Case C-526/24, Brillen Rottler, determining that a data subject's first request for access to personal data under Article 15 GDPR may be regarded as excessive and therefore abusive in certain circumstances.

The case concerned an Austrian individual who subscribed to a newsletter from Brillen Rottler, a German optician company, and submitted a data access request thirteen days later. The company refused the request, arguing it was abusive. The individual subsequently claimed compensation of at least €1,000 for alleged non-material damage. The Local Court, Arnsberg, sought clarification from the Court of Justice on whether a first access request could be excessive and whether compensation could be awarded for refusal of such a request.

The Court determined that a first request for access may be regarded as excessive within the meaning of GDPR where the controller demonstrates that, despite formal compliance with GDPR conditions, the request was made not to verify lawfulness of processing but with abusive intention to artificially create conditions for obtaining compensation. The Court found that evidence of a data subject making numerous access requests to various controllers, followed by compensation claims, may be considered when establishing such abusive intention.

Regarding compensation, the Court confirmed that persons suffering material or non-material damage as a result of a GDPR infringement, including infringement of the right of access, have the right to receive compensation from the controller under Article 82 GDPR. However, the Court found that a data subject must demonstrate actual damage has been suffered. Critically, the Court stated that no compensation is available under the GDPR if the data subject's own conduct is the determining cause of the damage.

The Court noted that controllers may, pursuant to Article 12(5) GDPR, either charge a reasonable fee for excessive requests or refuse to act on them. The Local Court, Arnsberg was directed to resolve the dispute applying the Court's findings.
recXj9lypURHWOo17.body: Court of Justice of the European Union

recjw2XXixQW3IZub.gen_title: Court of Justice Advocate General opines on unlicenced sports betting operator's liability to refund player stakes
recjw2XXixQW3IZub.summary:
On 19 March 2026, Advocate General Nicholas Emiliou issued an opinion in Case C-530/24 concerning whether a sports betting operator offering services without a required national licence may be obliged to refund stakes collected from players. The case concerns Tipico, a Maltese-licensed operator, against which a German consumer sought recovery of lost wagers placed on Tipico's German website between 2013 and 9 October 2020, when Tipico held no German licence as required by German law.

Advocate General Emiliou opined that Member States may require a licence for provision of certain services on their territory, and that such requirements are compatible with the freedom to provide services under EU law. The Advocate General found that national authorities, including civil courts, are entitled to enforce licence requirements against operators providing services without authorisation and to apply civil-law consequences, such as rendering contracts null and void or imposing restitution obligations. This remains so even where an operator claims inability to obtain a licence owing to deficiencies in the licensing procedure. The Advocate General determined that the operator's protection is sufficiently ensured by the possibility of challenging the licensing procedure before a court or tribunal.

However, the Advocate General identified an exception: civil-law consequences should not be imposed where doing so would be disproportionate. The Advocate General opined this exception applies where authorised and reliable national authorities provided the operator with precise, unconditional and consistent assurances that the licensing requirement would not be enforced and that services could be offered without a licence. The Advocate General found that nullity of gambling contracts, potentially requiring repayment of stakes to players, is proportionate to consumer-protection objectives and discourages operators from circumventing licensing systems. The Advocate General stated that if Tipico received such assurances from German authorities, German courts should apply available mechanisms under German private law to exempt Tipico from civil-law consequences, with any resulting consumer harm attributable to the public authorities providing the assurances. The Advocate General's opinion is not binding; the Court of Justice will issue its judgment at a later date.
recjw2XXixQW3IZub.body: Court of Justice of the European Union

recY1MAOlgBGcpiP6.orig_title: Judgment of the Court (Fourth Chamber) of 19 March 2026. — Brillen Rottler GmbH & Co. KG v TC. — Request for a preliminary ruling from the Amtsgericht Arnsberg. — Reference for a preliminary ruling – Protection of natural persons with regard to the p
recY1MAOlgBGcpiP6.gen_title: Court of Justice clarifies data subject's right of access and controller liability under GDPR Articles 12, 15 and 82
recY1MAOlgBGcpiP6.summary:
On 19 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union (Fourth Chamber) delivered judgment in Case C‑526/24, Brillen Rottler GmbH & Co. KG v TC, clarifying the scope of a data subject's right of access under Article 15(1) GDPR, the circumstances in which a controller may refuse a request, and the availability of compensation for non-material damage arising from infringement of access rights.

The case arose from a reference for a preliminary ruling by the Amtsgericht Arnsberg (Local Court, Arnsberg, Germany) concerning a dispute between Brillen Rottler GmbH & Co. KG, an optician company, and TC, an individual, over the company's refusal to grant access to personal data. The Court examined the interpretation of Article 4(2), Article 12(5) and Article 82(1) GDPR.

The Court considered Recital 63 of the GDPR, which recognises that data subjects possess a right of access to personal data collected concerning them, exercisable easily and at reasonable intervals, to verify the lawfulness of processing. The Court noted that Article 12(5) GDPR permits controllers to refuse manifestly unfounded or excessive requests, but such refusals must be proportionate and cannot operate as a blanket exemption from the access right itself.

On the question of liability and compensation, the Court examined Recital 85 and Article 82(1) GDPR. The Court determined that infringement of the right of access may result in non-material damage to data subjects, including loss of control over personal data. The Regulation contemplates broad interpretation of damage, reflecting its protective objectives. The Court found that a data subject may claim compensation under Article 82(1) GDPR for non-material harm arising from a controller's unjustified refusal to provide access, without requiring proof of quantifiable financial loss.

The judgment establishes that controllers cannot rely on general excessiveness arguments to deny first requests for access from data subjects and must apply Article 12(5) GDPR narrowly and case-specifically. The Court confirmed that the right to an effective remedy, recognised in Article 47 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights, encompasses access to judicial review and compensation for breaches of access rights.
recY1MAOlgBGcpiP6.body: Court of Justice of the European Union

reciNIQYkau1LjXIK.orig_title: EDPB and EDPS support strengthening EU’s cybersecurity and easing compliance while protecting individuals’ personal data
reciNIQYkau1LjXIK.gen_title: EDPB and EDPS issue joint opinion supporting Cybersecurity Act 2 proposal with data protection safeguards
reciNIQYkau1LjXIK.summary:
On 19 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) and the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) adopted a joint opinion on the European Commission's proposal for a Cybersecurity Act 2 (CSA2) and targeted amendments to the Network and Information Security 2 (NIS2) Directive, following the Commission's cybersecurity package proposal published on 20 January 2026.

The EDPB and EDPS support the general objectives of the CSA2 proposal, including strengthening the role of the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) and facilitating uptake of cybersecurity certification. The authorities find that the proposal to provide further clarification on ENISA's support to different stakeholders is well received. The EDPB and EDPS specifically welcome that ENISA's advice would be issued upon prior request from the EDPB, ensuring clear coordination and division of responsibilities. However, the authorities recommend adding the EDPS as a possible requestor of advice from ENISA. The EDPB and EDPS recall that where ENISA's Management Board decides to adopt additional measures necessary for application of EU Data Protection Regulation, such decisions must be limited to very technical details related to personal data processing, with prior consultation of the EDPS required before adoption.

Regarding cybersecurity certification, the EDPB and EDPS find that whilst the objective of facilitating uptake is welcome, the scope of the European Cybersecurity Certification Framework and its relationship with GDPR certification requires further clarification. The authorities state that ENISA must consult with the EDPB before adopting certification schemes relating to security of personal data processing. The EDPB and EDPS also recommend that the European Cybersecurity Skills Framework extend beyond cybersecurity professionals to include general workforce profiles.

On breach notification, the EDPB and EDPS express support for establishing a single-entry point for personal data breach notifications, finding this would reduce administrative burden for notifying organisations without affecting protection levels for individuals. The authorities also welcome the designation of European Digital Identity Wallets and European Business Wallets providers as essential entities under the proposed NIS2 amendments.

rec8hRBYOxHEzsLe7.orig_title: L’ANSSI lance un appel à commentaires sur les architectures de référence de sa nouvelle doctrine de supervision de sécurité
rec8hRBYOxHEzsLe7.gen_title: ANSSI launches public consultation on security supervision reference architectures
rec8hRBYOxHEzsLe7.summary:
On 19 March 2026, the Agence nationale de la sécurité des systèmes d'information (ANSSI) opened a public consultation on reference architectures and technical components for its emerging security supervision doctrine. The consultation period runs until 7 May 2026 (inclusive).

The ANSSI defines security supervision as the combination of means and activities enabling the rapid detection, qualification and appropriate response to security incidents across a supervised perimeter. The ANSSI states that supervision encompasses human, organisational, technical and financial resources, extending beyond incident detection to encompass qualification of incidents and selection of appropriate responses.

The consultation targets a broad ecosystem: beneficiaries of ANSSI services including operators of vital importance (OIV), essential service operators (OSE), government ministries and regulated entities; security supervision service providers including security incident detection providers (PDIS) and managed security service providers (MSSP); software and hardware solution vendors developing or seeking qualification for security supervision tools; and other relevant stakeholders. The ANSSI finds that the consultation will inform ongoing development of two technical-level guides: "Architectures and Deployment" and "Technologies and Implementation". These form part of a broader "Security Supervision" guidance collection, which already includes two published guides from 2025 covering strategic and operational decision-making levels.

The ANSSI notes that the consultation does not challenge existing regulation or the current PDIS v2.0 qualification framework, which remain in force. The ANSSI states that the new guidance aims to present a wider variety of secure supervision architectures reflecting current challenges including cloud hosting, service delegation and technological product diversity. The guides will serve as the foundation for a new PDIS qualification framework.

Respondents may participate through a PDF document presenting architecture components and reference architectures, and an online questionnaire accessible via the ANSSI's digital consultation platform.

recxksZvbuqADkekc.orig_title: CEF 2026 : Le CEPD lance une action coordonnée concernant les obligations de transparence et d'information
recxksZvbuqADkekc.gen_title: EDPB launches 2026 coordinated enforcement action on GDPR transparency and information obligations
recxksZvbuqADkekc.summary:
On 19 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board announced a coordinated enforcement action for 2026 focusing on transparency and information obligations under the GDPR (Arts. 12, 13, 14 GDPR), with the French data protection authority, the CNIL, coordinating the initiative across 25 European data protection authorities.

The EDPB states that the action will examine how organisations across different sectors respect their obligations to inform data subjects of the existence and conditions of processing their personal data. The participating authorities will contact controllers throughout Europe via questionnaires and investigative enquiries. The EDPB notes that authorities may decide to conduct additional follow-up actions following initial investigations.

The EDPB explains that the right to information is an essential element of the transparency principle and enables data subjects to exercise greater control over their data. During the second half of 2026, the participating authorities will exchange and examine their findings to synthesise results from national actions and develop a shared understanding of compliance with transparency requirements. The EDPB will then prepare a consolidated report for adoption by the Board, enabling targeted follow-up at both national and EU level.

This action forms part of the EDPB's Coordinated Enforcement Framework, identified as a key action in the Board's strategic plan for 2024-2027, which aims to rationalise the application of data protection legislation and strengthen cooperation between data protection authorities. The EDPB states this follows previous coordinated actions: in 2023 on the use of cloud-based services in the public sector; in 2024 on the appointment and role of data protection officers; in 2025 on implementation of the right of access; and in 2025 on the right to erasure.
recxksZvbuqADkekc.body: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rec2t37h2xw1j1Q3g.orig_title: CEF 2026: EDPB launches coordinated enforcement action on transparency and information obligations under the GDPR
rec2t37h2xw1j1Q3g.gen_title: EDPB launches 2026 coordinated enforcement action on GDPR transparency and information obligations
rec2t37h2xw1j1Q3g.summary:
On 19 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) launched its Coordinated Enforcement Framework (CEF) action for 2026, focusing on compliance with transparency and information obligations under the General Data Protection Regulation. The EDPB determined that transparency obligations under Articles 12, 13 and 14 GDPR are core elements ensuring individuals are informed when their data is being processed and have control over their personal data.

Twenty-five Data Protection Authorities across Europe will participate in the 2026 initiative. The EDPB states that participating DPAs will assess controller compliance with transparency obligations through enforcement actions or fact-finding exercises. Controllers from different sectors across Europe will be contacted during 2026. The EDPB notes that participating DPAs may undertake additional follow-up actions if needed following initial fact-finding exercises.

During the second half of 2026, participating DPAs will share and discuss their findings to aggregate results of national actions and generate insight into the topic. The EDPB will then draft a consolidated report for adoption by the Board, allowing for targeted follow-ups at both national and EU levels. The CEF operates under the EDPB's 2024-2027 Strategy, aimed at streamlining enforcement and cooperation among DPAs. This follows previous coordinated actions: in 2023 on use of cloud-based services by the public sector, in 2024 on designation and position of Data Protection Officers, in 2025 on the right of access, and in 2026 on the right to erasure under Article 17 GDPR. The Board selected transparency and information obligations as the 2026 CEF topic during its October 2025 plenary session.
rec2t37h2xw1j1Q3g.body: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

reclUeGvKN5lMwiFJ.orig_title: Niedersachsen beteiligt sich an europaweiter Datenschutzprüfung zu Transparenz- und Informationspflichten | Der Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz Niedersachsen
reclUeGvKN5lMwiFJ.gen_title: Niedersachsen joins pan-European data protection enforcement action on transparency obligations
reclUeGvKN5lMwiFJ.summary:
The Niedersächsischer Landesbeauftragte für Datenschutz (Niedersachsen Data Protection Authority) is participating in a coordinated enforcement action launched by the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) under the Coordinated Enforcement Framework (CEF) for 2026, focused on compliance with transparency and information requirements under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

On 19 March 2026, the EDPB commenced the 2026 CEF enforcement action. The Niedersachsen authority states that the focus of this year's coordinated inspections centres on compliance with Articles 13 and 14 GDPR, which impose obligations on controllers to inform data subjects comprehensively when personal data are collected or processed. The authority notes that transparency towards citizens is a central element of the GDPR and strengthens the control of data subjects over their data.

Denis Lehmkemper, head of the Niedersachsen authority, states that transparency is a central element for effective data protection, noting that citizens can only exercise their data subject rights when they know which data about them are processed and how they are processed. A total of 25 data protection supervisory authorities across Europe are participating in this year's CEF action. From Germany, the authorities from Niedersachsen and Brandenburg are taking part. The Niedersachsen authority indicates that the objective of the action is to examine compliance with transparency obligations across various sectors and to obtain findings that are comparable across Europe. The enforcement action follows the previous year's coordinated examination of the right to erasure under the CEF framework.

recx1jOZWhws60h5R.orig_title: Welche Angaben dürfen Vermieter von potenziellen Mietern verlangen? Datenschutzbeauftragte legen einheitliche Regeln fest | Der Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz Niedersachsen
recx1jOZWhws60h5R.gen_title: German data protection authorities issue unified guidance on rental applicant information requests
recx1jOZWhws60h5R.summary:
On 19 March 2026, the Niedersachsen data protection authority (LfD Niedersachsen) and the joint conference of German federal and state data protection authorities published unified guidance on the permissible scope of personal information that landlords, letting agents and property managers may request from prospective tenants during the rental process.

The guidance addresses a recurring compliance gap identified in the sector. Denis Lehmkemper, Landesbeauftragter für den Datenschutz Niedersachsen, stated that the LfD Niedersachsen's on-site inspections of property sector companies in 2024 identified multiple data protection deficiencies, including unlawful retention of identity document copies and impermissible questions in tenant self-disclosure forms. The new guidance provides all participants with support for complying with data protection obligations and protecting prospective tenant rights.

The ten-page orientation guidance differentiates between three sequential stages of the rental process: the property viewing, the preliminary phase when the prospective tenant confirms intention to rent, and the final selection stage. The authorities determine that permissible information requests vary by stage. At the viewing stage, landlords are generally prohibited from requesting information about economic circumstances. In the preliminary phase, prospective tenants must answer questions about open insolvency proceedings. Once a tenant is selected, landlords may request solvency evidence specifically necessary for contract conclusion, but comprehensive self-disclosures obtained from credit reporting agencies are not permitted.

The guidance applies a three-part assessment framework: whether the landlord holds a legitimate interest in the requested data, whether collection is necessary for contract performance, and whether the prospective tenant's right to informational self-determination outweighs these interests. Impermissible questions need not be answered.

Bettina Gayk, Landesdatenschutzbeauftragte in Nordrhein-Westfalen, whose authority led the project, noted that the guidance demonstrates coordination among data protection authorities on significant economic policy matters. The LfD Niedersachsen emphasises that clear rules and orientation guidance for handling tenant data are essential for sector compliance.

recI6ACYIoTn57idj.orig_title: Singapore Signs Memorandum of Cooperation with Japan on Mutual Recognition of Internet-of-Things Cybersecurity Schemes
recI6ACYIoTn57idj.gen_title: Singapore and Japan establish mutual recognition arrangement for IoT cybersecurity labelling schemes
recI6ACYIoTn57idj.summary:
On 18 March 2026, the Cyber Security Agency of Singapore (CSA) and Japan's Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry (METI) signed a Memorandum of Cooperation to mutually recognise IoT cybersecurity labelling schemes, with the arrangement taking effect on 1 June 2026.

The CSA and METI have agreed to mutually recognise the labelling scheme for smart devices certified under either jurisdiction. Under this arrangement, smart devices that have obtained cybersecurity labels under Japan's JC-STAR scheme and Singapore's Cybersecurity Labelling Scheme (CLS) will be mutually recognised. This permits manufacturers to apply for the other country's labelling scheme through a streamlined process. The arrangement applies to smart devices including smart home assistants, home automation and alarm systems, IoT gateways and hubs that connect multiple devices.

The CSA notes that Japan is the fifth nation to enter into such a mutual recognition arrangement with Singapore. Prior arrangements have been established with Finland, Germany, the Republic of Korea, and the United Kingdom. The CSA states that this cooperation enhances cybersecurity standards for IoT devices across both nations, provides market access for manufacturers and gives consumers greater confidence in the security of their smart devices. The CLS is a voluntary scheme, with the exception of Wi-Fi routers, for which obtaining CLS Level 1 certification is mandatory. Under the scheme, smart devices are rated according to their levels of cybersecurity provisions, with ratings displayed on products as asterisks ranging from one to four stars, where each additional asterisk indicates that the product has completed progressively more rigorous testing and security assessments.

Since the CSA launched the CLS in 2020, it has received applications for over 1,000 products, ranging from routers to smart lighting to smart cameras.

rec6tu78mXcwI1FJa.gen_title: Court of Justice rules biometric data collection in criminal investigations requires case-by-case necessity assessment
rec6tu78mXcwI1FJa.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union has determined that police authorities may collect biometric data such as fingerprints and photographs in criminal investigations only where such collection is strictly necessary on a case-by-case basis, and that systematic collection without individual assessment is contrary to EU law.

On 19 March 2026, the Court of Justice delivered judgment in Case C-371/24, a preliminary ruling reference from the Court of Appeal, Paris, concerning the lawfulness of French legislation permitting systematic collection of biometric data from persons suspected of criminal offences.

The Court clarifies that biometric data constitute sensitive personal data within the meaning of EU law requiring enhanced protection under Article 10 of Directive 2016/680. The Court finds that the mere existence of reasonable grounds for suspicion of an offence does not suffice as justification for biometric data collection. Every decision to gather identification data must contain a clear statement of reasons, even if succinct, enabling the data subject to understand the measure and exercise their right to a remedy. The Court determines that this obligation to state reasons does not impose an excessive burden on authorities, since collection cannot be systematic.

The Court specifies that national legislation rendering data gathering systematic, without competent police authorities being able to assess necessity in individual cases, would violate EU law by permitting indiscriminate and generalised collection of biometric data. National law must therefore specify concrete purposes for collection.

Regarding penalties for refusing to consent to biometric data gathering, the Court holds that such penalties' lawfulness depends on compliance with the strictly necessary condition under Directive 2016/680. Where collection satisfies this condition, a penalty is not incompatible with EU law provided it observes the principle of proportionality established by the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union. The Court finds that a person cannot lawfully be prosecuted for refusing consent to collection undertaken without proper justification, even if ultimately not prosecuted for the underlying suspected offence.
rec6tu78mXcwI1FJa.body: Court of Justice of the European Union

recNcCzNGVZqBHOii.orig_title: Governo do Brasil regulamenta ECA Digital e estabelece novo marco de proteção à infância no ambiente online
recNcCzNGVZqBHOii.gen_title: Brazil's Secretaria de Governo Digital enacts ECA Digital regulation establishing online child protection framework
recNcCzNGVZqBHOii.summary:
On 18 March 2026, Brazil's Secretaria de Governo Digital formalised the regulatory implementation of Law no. 15,211/2025, the Digital Statute for Children and Adolescents (ECA Digital), through three presidential decrees signed at the Palácio do Planalto. The decrees regulate the ECA Digital itself, establish the National Centre for Protection of Children and Adolescents within the Federal Police, and strengthen the National Data Protection Authority (ANPD) to enforce the new framework.

The Secretaria de Governo Digital states that the ECA Digital modernises online protections by extending guarantees from the 1990 Child and Adolescent Statute into digital environments. The legislation imposes mandatory obligations on applications, games, social media platforms and digital services operating in Brazil. The authority finds that platforms must replace self-declaration with reliable age-verification systems, remove illegal content expeditiously, and apply default security configurations to minor accounts. The regulation addresses harmful practices including manipulative design that encourages compulsive platform use and "loot boxes" associated with gambling mechanics. The Secretaria de Governo Digital notes that the framework requires judicial authorisation for commercial content by child influencers and strengthens parental supervision tools.

The regulatory structure establishes shared responsibility across the State, families, society and digital platforms. The newly created National Centre, housed within the Federal Police, centralises complaints and expands the State's capacity to respond to digital crimes including sexual exploitation, grooming and cyberbullying. The Secretaria de Governo Digital confirms that the ANPD receives enhanced regulatory and supervisory capacity, with authority to establish technical parameters for law implementation and oversee personal data protection. The government announced R$100 million in funding via Finep to support development of technological solutions for protecting children and adolescents online, in coordination with state technology enterprises Serpro and Dataprev.

recABSpCcdWoZcGcK.orig_title: Towards trustworthy AI in the EU public administration: The EDPS Compass for its new role under the AI Act
recABSpCcdWoZcGcK.gen_title: EDPS designates itself market surveillance authority for EU institutions' AI systems under AI Act
recABSpCcdWoZcGcK.summary:
On 17 March 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor published a strategic compass defining its supervisory role over artificial intelligence systems used by European Union institutions, bodies, offices and agencies under Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (the AI Act).

The EDPS states that it has been designated as a market surveillance authority for AI systems deployed by EUIs and as a notified body for conformity assessments of certain high-risk AI systems. The supervisor notes that it established an Artificial Intelligence Unit in October 2024 to operationalise these powers. The compass document outlines the EDPS's expanded mandate, which includes supervision of EUIs' AI systems through both ex ante and ex post mechanisms, contribution to AI Act governance, institutional empowerment for trustworthy AI development, and international engagement on best practices.

The EDPS identifies four strategic pillars to guide its action under the AI Act over 2026–2027. These pillars encompass supervision of EUIs' AI systems, comprising ex ante supervision through notified body conformity assessment functions and ex post supervision as market surveillance authority; contribution to AI Act governance and regulatory coordination through its role in the AI Board; institutional empowerment through interinstitutional knowledge exchange and a proposed Trustworthy AI Centre of Excellence; and international engagement and exchange of best practices.

The supervisor grounds its approach in commitments to impartiality, integrity, transparency, pragmatism and responsible innovation. The EDPS states it intends to reinforce institutional capacity to fulfil its new role under the AI Act, enhance legal and procedural clarity through a cooperative approach with EUIs, and discharge its renewed mission with authority and foresight. The two-year timeframe reflects the evolving nature of the AI Act, anticipated harmonised standards and European Commission guidelines, and possible amendments envisaged in the 2025 Digital Omnibus legislative proposal.

recLO8lF3WrBydpZk.orig_title: News release: World Anti-Doping Agency agrees to limit uses of athletes’ sensitive personal information
recLO8lF3WrBydpZk.gen_title: World Anti-Doping Agency commits to restrict use of athletes' sensitive personal data
recLO8lF3WrBydpZk.summary:
On 17 March 2026, the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada concluded a compliance agreement with the World Anti-Doping Agency (WADA) requiring the organisation to implement measures limiting the use of sensitive personal information collected from athletes to anti-doping purposes only.

The Privacy Commissioner Philippe Dufresne opened an investigation following a complaint that WADA had disclosed personal information to international sporting federations for uses beyond anti-doping, specifically to assess athletes' sex-based eligibility without their knowledge or consent. The complainant alleged that sensitive personal data under WADA's control was being repurposed without authorisation. The compliance agreement commits WADA to remedial measures to prevent such unauthorised disclosure and use by recipient organisations.

WADA, headquartered in Montreal, Quebec, became subject to the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA) in 2015 following international pressure to ensure its substantial holdings of sensitive athlete information received proper regulatory oversight. The organisation maintains databases containing highly sensitive personal information from thousands of athletes worldwide. The Privacy Commissioner stated that the commitment reflects the need to ensure such information is used only for the purposes for which it was collected.

The agreement requires WADA to implement safeguards to prevent international sporting federations and other anti-doping organisations receiving athlete data from WADA from using it for purposes unrelated to anti-doping activities. The Privacy Commissioner's investigation determined that existing controls were insufficient to prevent such secondary uses. The compliance agreement represents the resolution of the complaint without formal enforcement action, with WADA agreeing voluntarily to strengthen its data governance practices and contractual controls over recipient organisations. The agreement does not specify financial penalties or corrective payments to affected athletes. WADA's commitment addresses the gap between its collection of athlete information for legitimate anti-doping purposes and the use of that information by third parties for unrelated eligibility determinations.
recLO8lF3WrBydpZk.body: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada

recZz1PnEy74LE1kU.orig_title: Entwurf eines Gesetzes zur Umsetzung der Richtlinie (EU) 2022/2557 und zur Stärkung der Resilienz kritischer Anlagen
recZz1PnEy74LE1kU.gen_title: Germany enacts critical infrastructure resilience law transposing EU Directive 2022/2557
recZz1PnEy74LE1kU.summary:
On 16 March 2026, the Federal Ministry of the Interior announced that Germany has enacted legislation to transpose Directive (EU) 2022/2557 on the resilience of critical entities (the CER Directive) into national law. The law, formally proclaimed on 16 March 2026, establishes uniform federal rules for the physical protection of critical infrastructure to strengthen economic resilience and public supply security.

The legislation introduces four principal regulatory frameworks. First, it establishes requirements for identifying operators of critical facilities and critical entities of particular significance to Europe. Second, it mandates the conduct of national risk analyses and risk assessments for critical services. Third, it sets minimum requirements for resilience-enhancing measures that operators of critical facilities must implement. Fourth, it introduces an incident reporting system for operators to notify relevant authorities of disruptive events affecting critical infrastructure.

The legislative process began on 1 September 2025 with publication of a referral draft. The government subsequently produced a cabinet draft before parliamentary consideration. The Federal Ministry of the Interior coordinated consultation with numerous stakeholder organisations across critical sectors, including energy and water utilities, banking, transportation, logistics, healthcare, and emergency services. Industry bodies including the Federation of German Industries, the Association of Energy and Water Industries, the Association of Security Businesses, and the Association of Free Petrol Stations provided formal submissions during the consultation period.

The law reflects Germany's compliance obligations under the CER Directive, which harmonises resilience standards across the European Union. By establishing uniform national rules applicable to all operators of critical infrastructure, the legislation aims to eliminate regulatory fragmentation across German states (Länder) and ensure consistent implementation of EU resilience requirements. The incident reporting system creates a structured mechanism for identifying threats to critical infrastructure and enabling coordinated response by competent authorities. The risk assessment framework requires operators and authorities to systematically evaluate vulnerabilities in critical services including energy supply, water systems, healthcare, transportation, and communications.

recGyya6zaBjzSz55.gen_title: Egypt publishes National AI Governance Framework with 'State as Orchestrator' model
recGyya6zaBjzSz55.summary:
On 15 March 2026, the Egyptian Ministry of Communications and Information Technology, through the National Council for Artificial Intelligence, Quantum Computing, and Emerging Technologies, authorised and published the Guide to Egypt's National AI Governance Framework (version 2.0), establishing a comprehensive governance structure for artificial intelligence development and deployment across public and private sectors.

The Egyptian Center for Responsible AI (ECRAI), operating under the auspices of the National Council, prepared the Framework as a foundational blueprint for responsible AI governance. The document underwent iterative development beginning in August 2025, incorporating feedback from government, private sector, and international organisations during public consultation phases conducted in January and February 2026. Minister Raafat Hindy, Chairman of the National Council, authorised the final release on 15 March 2026.

The Framework adopts a governance philosophy termed 'State as Orchestrator' combined with a 'Strategic Hybrid' approach. The NCAI characterises this model as simultaneously enabling AI adoption through infrastructure, data, and capacity-building initiatives whilst establishing regulatory protections for high-risk systems. The Framework positions AI as a strategic national priority capable of transforming public services and driving economic growth, whilst maintaining safeguards rooted in Egypt's constitutional values and aligned with global best practices.

The governance architecture comprises a hierarchical suite of instruments: the National Strategy; this Guide, which defines what is governed through Risk Tiers and 'Dual-Check' compliance logic; the National Guidelines for Trustworthy and Responsible AI; and future sectoral regulations. The document establishes institutional architecture and regulatory certainty across sectors, designating the Framework as the comprehensive umbrella term for all governance instruments. The NCAI describes the approach as positioning Egypt as a regional anchor in responsible AI, serving as a conduit between the Arab region and Africa and the global digital economy. The Framework emphasises that technological advancement must not compromise national sovereignty, societal values, or citizens' constitutional rights.
recGyya6zaBjzSz55.body: Ministry of Communications and Information Technology

reclnMjYkQuKMw0t9.orig_title: KI-Verordnung nimmt Anbieter in die Pflicht | HmbBfDI
reclnMjYkQuKMw0t9.gen_title: Hamburg data protection authority outlines new transparency obligations for general-purpose AI providers from August 2025
reclnMjYkQuKMw0t9.summary:
On 31 July 2025, the Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) published guidance on new transparency requirements for providers of general-purpose AI (GPAI) models under the EU AI Regulation, which take effect from 2 August 2025.

The HmbBfDI notes that providers of GPAI models placed on the European market after 2 August 2025 face six key obligations. These include creating documentation and declaring compliance with EU copyright law. Providers must publish a detailed summary of training data used and provide information about how their models function. The authority states that providers must test high-impact models carrying systemic risk for vulnerabilities and risks, and report serious security incidents to regulators.

The HmbBfDI emphasises that these obligations build on earlier provisions of the AI Regulation. Bans on certain AI practices came into force in February 2025, and competency requirements were established simultaneously. The new transparency rules represent the first concrete operational obligations for GPAI providers since the regulation's entry into force.

The authority identifies significant advantages for organisations deploying GPAI models. The HmbBfDI finds that companies and public authorities integrating GPAI models into their own AI systems can now actively request information from model providers necessary for their compliance obligations. The HmbBfDI states this empowers deployers to better assess GPAI models in use, minimise risks, and fulfil data protection due diligence requirements.

Regarding implementation timelines, the HmbBfDI notes that providers of GPAI models already available before 2 August 2025 benefit from a transition period extending to 2 August 2027. The authority clarifies that the Commission's AI Office may launch investigations and demand corrective measures from August 2025 onwards. However, the HmbBfDI notes that the Commission's authority to impose fines and other sanctions does not commence until 2 August 2026, one year after the initial obligations take effect. The authority notes the EU Commission has published implementation guidelines to support compliance with these requirements.
reclnMjYkQuKMw0t9.body: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

recO0Gh9q3L6Ny7aa.gen_title: German data protection authorities propose GDPR amendments to strengthen rights and reduce SME compliance burden
recO0Gh9q3L6Ny7aa.summary:
On 17 March 2026, the Datenschutzkonferenz (DSK), the conference of independent federal and state data protection authorities in Germany, issued recommendations for targeted amendments to the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) in response to the European Commission's Digital Fitness Check of data and digital legislation.

The DSK determined that whilst GDPR provisions have proven effective during increasing digitalisation of many areas of life, the regulation requires specific modifications to strengthen protection of fundamental rights to informational self-determination, particularly for children, and to reduce compliance burden on small and medium-sized enterprises. The DSK proposes amendment of Article 37(7) GDPR to eliminate the obligation for data protection officers to notify supervisory authorities of their contact details, finding this change would reduce administrative burden on businesses. The authority recommends further clarification of the definition of "biometric data" under Article 9 GDPR and modifications to the right of access to balance the rights and freedoms of third parties in justified individual cases.

The DSK identified need for manufacturers and providers of hardware and software to assume greater data protection responsibility. The authority notes that Article 25 GDPR establishes data protection by design and by default principles intended for manufacturers, importers and providers, yet currently only users of hardware and software bear data protection obligations. The DSK finds that responsibility concepts must be aligned with other digital legal instruments, including the Cyber Resilience Act and the AI Regulation, to achieve meaningful SME relief.

Regarding child protection, the DSK proposes improved safeguards for children's data, including prohibition of personalised advertising and child-appropriate default settings in social networks. The authority additionally recommends establishing specific legal bases for development, training and operation of AI models and systems, and strengthening information and disclosure rights for individuals concerning AI processing of personal data. The DSK emphasised that core provisions of Articles 5 and 6 GDPR should remain unaltered, and that supervisory authorities require additional discretion in complaint handling given significantly increased complaint volumes across Europe.

rec3X7Ddi1mF5HYSE.orig_title: Declaración conjunta de autoridades de protección de datos personales sobre privacidad e imágenes generadas por Inteligencia Artificial
rec3X7Ddi1mF5HYSE.gen_title: Argentine data protection authority joins 60+ regulators on AI-generated imagery principles
rec3X7Ddi1mF5HYSE.summary:
On 12 March 2026, the Agencia de Acceso a la Información Pública (AAIP), Argentina's data protection authority, joined more than 60 data protection regulators worldwide in issuing a joint declaration addressing privacy risks posed by artificial intelligence systems that generate realistic images and videos depicting identifiable individuals without their knowledge or consent.

The AAIP and fellow regulators, coordinating through the Global Privacy Assembly's International Enforcement Cooperation Working Group, established foundational principles to guide organisations and institutions developing and deploying AI-powered audiovisual content generation systems. The declaration identifies expectations for responsible development and deployment of such technologies, with particular emphasis on safeguarding vulnerable populations.

The regulators determined that organisations must implement robust security measures to prevent misuse of personal information and the generation of non-consensual intimate imagery and other harmful material, particularly where depictions involve children and adolescents. The AAIP and co-signatories expect organisations to ensure transparency regarding system capabilities, security safeguards, acceptable uses and consequences of misuse. Organisations must provide effective and accessible mechanisms enabling individuals to request deletion of content involving their personal information and respond promptly to such requests. The authorities identified specific risks affecting children and adolescents, requiring organisations to furnish clear, age-appropriate information for young people, parents, guardians and educators regarding the capabilities and risks of AI imagery generation systems.

Signatory jurisdictions include Albania, Australia, Belgium, Brazil, Canada, Colombia, Croatia, Ecuador, France, Germany, Ghana, Iceland, Israel, Italy, Kenya, Malta, Mexico, the Netherlands, New Zealand, Nigeria, Norway, Peru, Portugal, Singapore, South Korea, Spain, Switzerland, the United Kingdom and Uruguay, among others. The declaration was issued under the auspices of the Global Privacy Assembly, the international forum for data protection authorities, reflecting coordinated international regulatory consensus on AI governance in the context of personal data protection.
rec3X7Ddi1mF5HYSE.body: Agencia de Acceso a la Informacion Publica

recghrzsaJsSJCOsj.orig_title: Zwischenbilanz 2025: HmbBfDI verhängt Bußgelder von insgesamt 775.000 Euro | HmbBfDI
recghrzsaJsSJCOsj.gen_title: Hamburg data protection authority imposes €775,000 in GDPR fines in 2025 interim review
recghrzsaJsSJCOsj.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) has imposed aggregate fines totalling approximately €775,000 (£656,000) for breaches of the General Data Protection Regulation in 2025, with a single financial services company accounting for €492,000 of that sum.

On 30 September 2025, the Hamburg data protection authority announced its interim enforcement report. The largest fine of €492,000 (£414,000) was levied against a financial services company for violations concerning automated decision-making in individual cases. The HmbBfDI determined that the company had rejected multiple credit card applications from customers with good creditworthiness using automated decisions made on the basis of algorithms without human intervention. When affected customers requested reasons for the rejections, the HmbBfDI found the company failed to fulfil its statutory information and disclosure obligations adequately. The authority notes that automated decisions carry particular risks for the rights and freedoms of data subjects and are permitted under GDPR only under strict conditions. Data controllers are required to provide affected persons with meaningful information about the logic involved in automated decision-making (Art. 22 GDPR). The HmbBfDI applied significant mitigation because the company made substantial efforts to improve its processes for fulfilling data subject rights and cooperated comprehensively with the authority.

By the end of September 2025, the HmbBfDI had concluded 15 administrative penalty proceedings. The authority identified unlawful marketing and individual employee violations as enforcement priorities. In three cases, companies sent marketing emails to customers without their consent, resulting in fines in the low five-figure range. The HmbBfDI imposed six fines against police officers and other Hamburg public employees who conducted unauthorised queries about private individuals in official databases. One hospital employee was fined for accessing a colleague's patient records without involvement in treatment. Additionally, the HmbBfDI imposed a €195,000 (£164,000) fine against a retail company that engaged service providers to send postal marketing. The authority determined the company failed to fulfil data subject rights requests from recipients in multiple cases and over an extended period within the required timeframes.
recghrzsaJsSJCOsj.body: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

recdFbfosTjiv5suw.orig_title: Geltungsbeginn des Data Acts – neue Aufgaben für den HmbBfDI | HmbBfDI
recdFbfosTjiv5suw.gen_title: Hamburg data protection authority takes on Data Act supervision for personal data from 12 September 2025
recdFbfosTjiv5suw.summary:
On 12 September 2025, the Data Act comes into force in the European Union, granting consumers and business users new rights to access sensor data from connected devices such as smart toothbrushes and wind turbines. The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI), Hamburg's data protection authority, confirms it will supervise compliance with Data Act obligations where personal data is involved.

The HmbBfDI states that Article 37(3) of the Data Act designates data protection authorities as the supervisory body for compliance with Data Act provisions relating to personal data. The authority will support rights-holders in exercising their entitlements under the Data Act insofar as personal data is concerned, including rights to access personal data held by manufacturers, to switch data processing service providers (cloud-switching), to receive technical and organisational protections of confidentiality at the receiving organisation, and to benefit from transparency obligations. The HmbBfDI notes it may enforce these rights through orders, and violations may be subject to fines. Individuals and organisations may alternatively pursue claims through civil proceedings.

The HmbBfDI states that any natural or legal person may lodge a complaint if they have reason to believe a Hamburg-based company has violated their Data Act rights concerning personal data. The authority finds that complaints may be submitted informally to dataact@datenschutz.hamburg.de, by post, or using the general complaint form. The HmbBfDI determines that it will investigate complaints in the department responsible for data protection supervision of the relevant controller, ensuring unified assessment of data uses under both the General Data Protection Regulation and the Data Act. The authority notes its jurisdiction as Data Act supervisor is limited to cases involving personal data. For non-personal data scenarios, the HmbBfDI states the German federal legislator has not yet designated a supervisory authority, meaning claims concerning non-personal data must currently be pursued independently through civil proceedings.
recdFbfosTjiv5suw.body: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

recTBSgY4KheMqqrU.orig_title: News release: Privacy Commissioner of Canada tables in Parliament Special Report on ArriveCAN app investigation
recTBSgY4KheMqqrU.gen_title: Privacy Commissioner of Canada tables Special Report on ArriveCAN app investigation
recTBSgY4KheMqqrU.summary:
On 12 March 2026, the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (OPC) tabled a Special Report in Parliament following its investigation into the ArriveCAN application and the Canada Border Services Agency's (CBSA) contracting practices during the app's development.

The investigation was initiated following a complaint regarding CBSA's contracting practices related to the ArriveCAN app, which was launched during the COVID-19 pandemic to digitalise the collection of traveller information and expedite border processing. The OPC found no evidence to suggest that personal information collected through the ArriveCAN app was used or disclosed in contravention of the Privacy Act, which governs the personal information handling practices of federal institutions. The OPC determined that all ArriveCAN-related contracts permitting access to personal information contained appropriate clauses describing security requirements and outlining specific safeguards for implementation.

Whilst no contraventions of the Act were identified, the OPC identified certain shortcomings in CBSA's contracting framework. The Commissioner expects the CBSA to implement measures including: rigorously and accurately assessing security requirements within reasonable timeframes prior to contract award; ensuring task descriptions in authorisations clearly define projects or work to be performed so that privacy and security requirements specific to those tasks are accurately identified and assessed; proactively managing security clearances and renewal processes with rigour and strong oversight; and restricting permissions and access to personal information to what is strictly necessary.

The CBSA accepted the Commissioner's recommendations and agreed with the overall objective of strengthening privacy and security practices within its contracting framework. The agency indicated its commitment to ensuring that its policies and procedures uphold the highest standards of accountability, transparency and protection of personal information. Privacy Commissioner Philippe Dufresne stated that the investigation highlights the importance of privacy as a core consideration when developing outsourcing contracts, and noted that the findings present an opportunity to raise awareness among all federal institutions regarding best practices in contracting to ensure strong privacy protections for Canadians.
recTBSgY4KheMqqrU.body: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada

recVhxABNc6HPo9Ae.orig_title: EDPB and EDPS support harmonisation of clinical trials under European Biotech Act, but call for specific safeguards for sensitive health data - IDPC
recVhxABNc6HPo9Ae.gen_title: EDPB and EDPS call for safeguards for health data in European Biotech Act clinical trials framework
recVhxABNc6HPo9Ae.summary:
On 12 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor adopted a joint opinion on the European Commission's proposal for a European Biotech Act, supporting harmonisation of clinical trials regulation while recommending specific protections for sensitive health and genetic data.

The EDPB and EDPS stated that they support the proposed Act's objective of strengthening Europe's biotechnology and biomanufacturing sectors and addressing fragmentation in the application of the Clinical Trials Regulation. The authorities noted that the proposal aims to establish a single legal basis for the processing of personal data by sponsors and investigators across the EU, which the EDPB and EDPS recognise will improve legal clarity and remove inconsistencies in how clinical trial data is handled across member states.

However, the EDPB and EDPS underlined that the sensitivity of health and genetic data processed in clinical trials requires a high standard of protection. The authorities stated that the proposed simplifications must not lower the level of protection for clinical trial participants. The joint opinion provides several recommendations to ensure that streamlining the regulatory framework does not compromise data protection safeguards. The EDPB and EDPS emphasised that whilst harmonisation of clinical trial rules is beneficial for competitiveness and legal certainty, the processing of sensitive personal data in this context demands robust protective measures.

The joint opinion reflects the data protection authorities' balance between supporting regulatory modernisation in the biotech sector and maintaining rigorous standards for the processing of health and genetic information. The EDPB and EDPS position indicates that any changes to the Clinical Trials Regulation framework must preserve the high level of protection afforded to participants, particularly given the inherently sensitive nature of health data involved in clinical research.

rec9HPcCegiVHYmv1.orig_title: EDPB and EDPS support harmonisation of clinical trials under European Biotech Act, but call for specific safeguards for sensitive health data
rec9HPcCegiVHYmv1.gen_title: EDPB and EDPS adopt joint opinion supporting European Biotech Act with clinical trial data safeguards
rec9HPcCegiVHYmv1.summary:
On 12 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor adopted a joint opinion on the European Commission's Proposal for a European Biotech Act, supporting harmonisation of clinical trial regulation whilst recommending specific protections for sensitive health data.

The EDPB and EDPS state that they support the Proposal's objective of fostering EU competitiveness and addressing fragmentation in the application of the Clinical Trials Regulation. The authorities welcome the aim to establish a single legal basis for the processing of personal data by sponsors and investigators, which the EDPB and EDPS find will significantly improve legal clarity across Europe. However, the EDPB and EDPS underline that the sensitivity of health and genetic data processed in clinical trials requires a high standard of protection, and recommend that proposed simplifications do not lower the level of protection for clinical trial participants.

The EDPB and EDPS recommend clarifying whether actors in funding and conducting clinical trials act as sole or joint data controllers to ensure clear allocation of responsibilities. The authorities recommend that the mandatory 25-year minimum retention period apply expressly only to the clinical trial master file, rather than to all personal data processed during a trial. The EDPB and EDPS recommend that where the Proposal aims to provide a legal basis for further processing of trial data by the same controller, the Biotech Act should clearly define purposes and provide specific safeguards. The authorities recommend that the Biotech Act ensure obligations for sponsors complement requirements under the AI Act to create a consistent regulatory environment.

Additionally, the EDPB and EDPS recommend that the Clinical Trials Regulation explicitly require pseudonymisation whenever processing directly identifiable personal data is unnecessary. Where regulatory sandboxes are implemented, the EDPB and EDPS recommend implementing acts provide a legal basis for personal data processing and derogations under Article 9(2) GDPR for sensitive data processing; for other sandboxes, personal data processing must be based on a legal basis under the GDPR.

recJch4y5ltJ0rEqQ.orig_title: Bozza di Linee Guida per lo sviluppo di sistemi di Intelligenza Artificiale nella pubblica amministrazione
recJch4y5ltJ0rEqQ.gen_title: Italian digital agency publishes draft guidance for artificial intelligence system development in public administration
recJch4y5ltJ0rEqQ.summary:
On 11 March 2026, the Agenzia per l'Italia Digitale (Italian Digital Agency) published a draft version of guidelines for the development of artificial intelligence systems within Italian public administration, pursuant to the three-year information technology plan for public administration 2024-2026.

The guidelines, released for public consultation, establish the scope of application across the subject matter and entities covered. The document sets out seven core principles for the development and procurement of AI systems in public administration under Law 132/2025. These principles comprise: protection of fundamental rights, transparency, proportionality and non-discrimination; data and process quality, correctness, reliability and transparency; human-centredness, explainability and harm prevention; protection of democratic and institutional methods and sovereignty; consistency with the European regulatory framework, specifically the AI Act; cybersecurity and resilience across the system lifecycle; and universal accessibility and inclusion.

The draft guidance categorises AI systems into six families of technologies. The AGID provides an architectural reference model applicable to public administration, structured across multiple layers spanning from energy requirements through infrastructure, AI models, and application levels. The guidance identifies heterogeneous data sources, including cloud, on-premises and hybrid environments, as essential components of the reference architecture model. An orchestration component is specified as central to coordinating these elements within the system framework.

The document addresses the role of data throughout the AI system development lifecycle, distinguishing between training data and operational data stages. The guidance establishes a logical architecture framework encompassing tools at the application level and data sources across multiple deployment models. The AGID publication represents the implementation of standards set out in the three-year digital plan covering the 2024-2026 period, with the guidelines issued for public stakeholder consultation prior to finalisation.

recvrZHA4A9KKkisK.orig_title: Frist zur Benennung von KI-Aufsicht abgelaufen: Fuchs fordert Bundesregierung zum Handeln auf | HmbBfDI
recvrZHA4A9KKkisK.gen_title: Hamburg Data Protection Authority calls on German government to name AI market surveillance authorities after deadline missed
recvrZHA4A9KKkisK.summary:
On 31 July 2025, the Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) issued a statement that Germany failed to meet the 2 August 2025 deadline set by EU law to designate market surveillance authorities responsible for overseeing the AI Regulation. The HmbBfDI notes that EU member states are obliged under the AI Regulation to appoint competent market surveillance authorities by this date, yet Germany lacks both the requisite implementation legislation and the formal designation of responsible bodies.

Thomas Fuchs, the Hamburg Data Protection Commissioner, states that the delay leaves businesses and public authorities without a clearly defined contact point for enquiries regarding the AI Regulation, creating uncertainty for Germany as an AI innovation location. The HmbBfDI finds that although member states must designate these authorities, Germany has not yet adopted an AI Regulation implementation act, leaving the governance structure unclear. The authority notes that the AI Regulation imposes stringent requirements on designated bodies: they must operate independently and be equipped with adequate technical, financial and human resources, including sufficient staff with specialist knowledge in AI, data protection law and product safety law.

The HmbBfDI recognises that the AI Regulation already defines certain oversight responsibilities for data protection authorities in specific areas. Under Article 74(8) of the AI Regulation, data protection authorities are designated as responsible for market surveillance of high-risk AI systems deployed in fundamental rights-sensitive sectors including criminal enforcement, migration and justice. However, the authority identifies that responsibility for broader AI product deployment in the public sector, such as in education, remains undefined.

Despite the absence of formal national designation, the HmbBfDI confirms it is building internal capacity and expertise in preparation for its anticipated oversight role. The authority states that staff are being trained to conduct complex AI system assessments. The HmbBfDI notes that from 2 August 2025, new transparency requirements for AI models under the AI Regulation place obligations on AI providers.
recvrZHA4A9KKkisK.body: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

reca2jBN9fv3SfjoC.orig_title: EuGH-Urteil in der Rechtssache Russmedia: Weichenstellung für die Verantwortlichkeit von Hosting-Anbietern | HmbBfDI
reca2jBN9fv3SfjoC.gen_title: Court of Justice of the EU rules hosting providers bear data protection responsibility for user-generated content in Russmedia case
reca2jBN9fv3SfjoC.summary:
On 2 December 2025, the Court of Justice of the European Union delivered judgment in case C-492/23 (Russmedia), establishing that hosting providers may bear joint responsibility under data protection law for unlawful content posted by users. The Hamburg and Berlin data protection authorities welcomed the ruling as strengthening the rights of affected individuals and issued guidance on the resulting obligations for platform operators publishing user-generated content.

The case concerned a woman whose photograph and telephone number were used without her knowledge or consent in an advertisement on an online marketplace offering alleged sexual services. The court determined that a hosting provider bears data protection responsibility even without exercising influence over advertisement content, contrary to the advocate general's conclusions. The Hamburg and Berlin authorities state the provider's decisions regarding the purposes and means of processing personal data when supplying the platform were sufficient to trigger fundamental obligations under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

The court found that the hosting provider had reserved broad rights in its terms and conditions to use, distribute, transmit, reproduce, modify, translate and share published content, and to delete it at any time without stating reasons. The authorities determined this supported a finding of responsibility. Consequently, hosting services must identify content containing sensitive data within the meaning of Article 9(1) GDPR before publication and verify whether the publisher is the person whose data appears in the advertisement. If not, they must verify consent or another legal basis exists for publication.

The court ruled hosting providers must ensure only users whose identity has been verified beforehand can publish content, and must take appropriate technical and organisational measures to prevent advertisements containing sensitive data from being unlawfully copied and republished on other websites. The Hamburg and Berlin authorities concluded this establishes responsibility begins before publication, not merely upon receiving notice of a violation. The ruling clarifies that liability exemptions under the E-Commerce Directive, continued in the Digital Services Act Article 16, do not exclude GDPR responsibility, and hosting providers cannot rely on such exemptions regarding GDPR obligations.
reca2jBN9fv3SfjoC.body: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit
