The authority states it funds liquidators through grants from the Assetless Administration Fund and takes action against registered liquidators including administrative action.
recI3KbV83OvcCPcc.body: Australian Securities and Investments Commission Press Releases

rec3hB2aeLlWXzAqm.orig_title: National Assembly passes Law on Cybersecurity
rec3hB2aeLlWXzAqm.gen_title: Vietnam National Assembly passes Law on Cybersecurity with 434 votes in favour
rec3hB2aeLlWXzAqm.summary:
On 10 December 2025, the National Assembly of Vietnam passed the Law on Cybersecurity with 434 out of 443 present deputies voting in favour. The Ministry of Public Security states the law comprises eight chapters and 45 articles and will take effect from 1 July 2026.

The Ministry of Public Security, as the drafting body, notes that the 2025 Law on Cybersecurity merges the 2018 Law on Cybersecurity and the 2015 Law on Cyberinformation Security. The Ministry states the law does not alter the functions or responsibilities of ministries and sectors, nor introduce new policies. In presenting the report on the draft law, Minister of Public Security General Luong Tam Quang noted that the drafting process incorporated opinions from National Assembly deputies and adhered to the principle of consolidating existing cybersecurity legislation.

The Ministry of Public Security states that, following National Assembly deputies' recommendations, it reviewed and incorporated relevant provisions of the United Nations Convention against Cybercrime (the Hanoi Convention) in a manner consistent with domestic law. The Ministry notes that the draft law now includes additional regulations on international cooperation in combating cybercrime and on cybersecurity protection measures. General Luong Tam Quang stressed that ensuring cybersecurity is a critical, regular, urgent and strategic task, and a responsibility shared by the entire political system and the people.

Under Government assignment, the Ministry of Public Security will serve as the standing agency coordinating the National Cybersecurity Incident Response Alliance comprising relevant authorities and enterprises. The Ministry is required to ensure swift and effective response to cybersecurity incidents and threats to information systems of ministries, sectors, localities and key enterprises. The Ministry will also ensure the collection of data and electronic evidence in accordance with the Criminal Procedure Code to support the investigation and handling of cybercrime.

recbzMWA98wXkKAc0.orig_title: Judgment of the Court in Case C-422/24 Storstockholms Lokaltrafik
recbzMWA98wXkKAc0.gen_title: Court of Justice rules body camera data collection during ticket inspections qualifies as direct collection under GDPR
recbzMWA98wXkKAc0.summary:
On 18 December 2025, the Court of Justice of the European Union delivered judgment in Case C-422/24 Storstockholms Lokaltrafik, determining that data obtained through body cameras worn by ticket inspectors during passenger inspections constitutes direct collection from the data subject under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), triggering immediate information disclosure obligations.

The dispute arose after the Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection fined a Stockholm public transport company for operating body cameras during ticket inspections without providing passengers with adequate information. The company argued it had collected data indirectly, a distinction that would alter the timing and scope of information obligations under the GDPR. The Swedish court referred the question to the Court of Justice for preliminary ruling.

The Court of Justice determined that data obtained through body camera observation is collected directly from the data subject, regardless of whether the subject knowingly provided the data or took any particular action. The Court found that the classification of data collection as 'direct' does not require either knowledge or active participation from the data subject. By contrast, the Court notes, indirect data collection occurs when the controller obtains data from another source without direct contact with the data subject.

Regarding implementation of information obligations, the Court held that controllers may adopt a multi-layered approach compliant with Article 13 GDPR when collecting data directly. The Court states that the most important information may be displayed on a warning sign, whilst other mandatory information may be provided in an easily accessible place in an appropriate and complete manner. The mandatory information includes the controller's identity and contact details, processing purposes, legal basis, data recipients, storage duration, and rights to access and erasure.

The judgment clarifies that the presence of body cameras during ticket inspections triggers direct collection obligations, and establishes that tiered information provision satisfies GDPR transparency requirements where implementation occurs through accessible signage and supplementary documentation rather than individual notice to each filmed passenger.
recbzMWA98wXkKAc0.body: Court of Justice of the European Union

recUYeNd7kkFwTINs.orig_title: Synthèse des contributions de la consultation publique sur le projet de recommandation relative au recueil du consentement multi-terminaux
recUYeNd7kkFwTINs.gen_title: CNIL publishes summary of public consultation responses on cross-device consent recommendation
recUYeNd7kkFwTINs.summary:
On 15 January 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL) published a synthesis of contributions received during its public consultation on a draft recommendation concerning the collection of cross-device consent, which ran from 24 April 2025.

The CNIL received contributions from 10 stakeholders, comprising 3 professional associations, 1 civil society organisation, and 6 private companies. The consultation sought to identify interpretation difficulties and verify the operational feasibility of the draft recommendation against technical and practical constraints faced by organisations. The CNIL states that the feedback enabled it to verify the recommendation's operational character and to evolve the text to address concerns most frequently raised by contributors.

Regarding the recommendation's scope, contributors proposed extending it to cover consent for offline data usage and to include cross-device consent in non-logged environments. The CNIL determined that it would not modify the scope. On offline data: the authority notes that consent given via a consent management platform (CMP) generally covers treatment of offline purchase data when such data constructs user profiles for online personalisation, provided individuals receive adequate information to ensure valid consent. The recommendation does not extend to consent required under other legal provisions typically collected outside the CMP context, such as account creation forms. On non-logged environments: the CNIL maintains its exclusion of such settings from the recommendation, finding that cross-device consent identification would require intrusive processing and raises specific legal questions.

On the definition of cross-device consent and lawfulness conditions, the CNIL states that most contributors agreed such consent is permissible subject to conditions detailed in the draft recommendation. One contributor argued the practice is unlawful under applicable regulations. Another requested clarification on user information requirements regarding consent scope, noting this could imply users could choose between single-device and cross-device consent. The CNIL clarified that data protection rules (GDPR and the French Data Protection Act) do not preclude cross-device consent, though lawfulness depends on implementation conditions specified in the recommendation. The authority determined that controllers may offer only a cross-device model provided recommendation conditions are met.

Regarding user information, contributors disagreed on whether a notification banner displayed after authentication on new devices is necessary. One contributor considered it unnecessary if adequate information is provided at consent collection via the CMP; another deemed interactive functionality mandatory to satisfy affirmative consent requirements. The CNIL confirmed the banner recommendation aims to ensure consent remains informed through multi-stage information, both at collection and post-authentication on new authenticated devices.
recUYeNd7kkFwTINs.body: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rec80x97wcg5UvSHT.orig_title: 個人情報保護法いわゆる３年ごと見直しの制度改正方針概要-個人情報保護委員会-
rec80x97wcg5UvSHT.gen_title: Japan's Personal Information Protection Commission announces three-yearly legislative review with expanded consent exemptions and enforcement powers
rec80x97wcg5UvSHT.summary:
On 9 January 2026, Japan's Personal Information Protection Commission (PPC) released its policy framework for the triennial review of the Personal Information Protection Act (Act), required under the Act's 2020 amendment (transitional provision article 10). The PPC conducted hearings with stakeholder organisations and experts to assess implementation and identify reform priorities, taking account of rapid advances in information and communication technology and international developments.

The PPC's reform framework introduces expanded exemptions from personal consent requirements. The authority proposes that third-party provision of personal data and acquisition of publicly available sensitive personal information require no consent when used solely for statistical information creation, including AI development where properly classified as statistical work. The PPC finds that for off-purpose use, sensitive information acquisition and third-party provision, consent may be waived where the handling is manifestly consistent with the individual's intentions and does not harm their rights or interests based on the circumstances of acquisition. The authority also proposes relaxing the consent requirement where handling protects life or advances public health, and explicitly including medical institutions within the academic research exception.

The framework establishes enhanced protections for minors and sensitive biometric data. The PPC determines that for individuals under 16 years old, consent and notification obligations must target the legal representative, whilst requirements for data deletion requests are to be relaxed. A new duty provision will require entities to prioritise the best interests of minors. For facial feature data, the authority mandates disclosure of certain handling details, relaxes deletion request conditions, and prohibits opt-out based third-party provision.

Enforcement measures are substantially expanded. The PPC proposes streamlined command authority to halt violations, authority to recommend or order fact notification or public disclosure protecting individual rights, and new grounds to request third parties assisting violations to cease. For prohibited database provision, the authority proposes extending penalties to cases involving intent to harm and increasing statutory sentences. The framework introduces administrative monetary penalties proportionate to financial benefits obtained from serious violations causing individual harm. New penalties are proposed for fraudulent personal information acquisition.

recR8JVEyaE1oOmv7.orig_title: Leading digital Europe: BEREC adopts Strategy 2026-2030
recR8JVEyaE1oOmv7.gen_title: BEREC adopts Strategy 2026-2030 for electronic communications regulation
recR8JVEyaE1oOmv7.summary:
On 9 December 2025, the Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications (BEREC) adopted its Strategy 2026-2030, consolidating and updating three previous strategic documents to guide its regulatory priorities for the coming five years.

BEREC states that the strategy builds on core pillars addressing market and technological developments, policy and legislative changes, and institutional and international cooperation. The authority identifies strategic priorities centred on fostering connectivity, competition, open digital ecosystems and end-user empowerment, whilst guiding the development of sustainable, secure and resilient digital networks. The Strategy 2026-2030 consolidates BEREC's Strategy 2021-2025, its Medium-Term Strategy for relations with other institutions 2022-2025, its Medium-Term Strategy for International Cooperation 2022-2025, and the Action Plan 2030. BEREC notes that the strategy may be revised before 2030 if substantial regulatory changes occur, particularly relating to its mandate and anticipated review of the legal framework.

BEREC's Work Programme 2026 translates strategic objectives into specific actions and deliverables. The authority identifies continued focus on gigabit broadband connectivity expansion, 5G rollout across EU regions, and end-user empowerment in electronic communications. BEREC indicates it will provide input and analysis on the forthcoming Digital Networks Act proposal. The Work Programme builds on mandatory tasks under the European Electronic Communications Code (Art. 22 EECC) and anticipates work related to the DNA. BEREC states it remains committed to fostering debate and providing advice to the European Parliament, Council and Commission on electronic communications and digital services, whilst enhancing consistent application of regulation to develop the internal market and strengthen stakeholder interaction.

BEREC has approved a draft Report on switching and termination of contracts for public consultation, examining the switching process and end-user rights throughout that process. The authority notes that switching efficiency, simplicity and transparency are essential for building end-user trust and ensuring effective competition. BEREC has also launched updated Guidelines on Geographical surveys of broadband network deployment for consultation, following conclusions from its implementation report that existing Guidelines required adjustment. The revised Guidelines aim to provide clearer guidance and improve comparability of survey results across Member States. The authority notes it launched a digital fraud awareness campaign in October to inform European users about online risks and encourage reporting of incidents to national authorities.
recR8JVEyaE1oOmv7.body: Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications

recN423GLT2EyrdmY.gen_title: NDPC engages stakeholders on AI governance and crisis communication data safeguards
recN423GLT2EyrdmY.summary:
The Nigeria Data Protection Commission (NDPC) participated in two separate stakeholder engagement events to address data governance frameworks and personal data protection in emerging technology contexts.

On 8 December 2025, the NDPC was represented at the Nigeria Youth Internet Governance Forum (NYIGF) by Mr Abubakar Mahmud, an officer from the Commission's IT & Cybersecurity Department. During a panel session titled "Artificial Intelligence Data Governance & Regulation", Mr Mahmud addressed the application of data protection principles to artificial intelligence systems. The NDPC indicated it has translated relevant regulatory frameworks to address AI governance requirements, though specific details of those translations were not disclosed in the Commission's statement.

In a separate engagement, the NDPC was represented by Mrs Adaobi Nwankwo, Head of the Commission's Innovation Unit, at a National Symposium on Digital Innovation in Crisis Communication. The symposium examined "Leveraging Emerging Technologies to Transform Crisis Communication: Data Privacy and Digital Trust in Crisis Communication Systems". Mrs Nwankwo participated in a panel session addressing the intersection of data protection obligations and emergency communication systems.

Both engagements reflect the NDPC's focus on data governance in contexts beyond traditional personal data processing. The Commission's participation in youth-focused forums signals engagement with stakeholder education on AI regulation, whilst its representation at the crisis communication symposium indicates attention to data safeguarding requirements when personal data is processed in emergency or disaster response scenarios. The events demonstrate the NDPC's multi-stakeholder approach to regulatory engagement, including engagement with young professionals through the NYIGF and participation in cross-sector discussions on digital innovation and data protection integration within crisis management infrastructure.

recQ94pxDydS1NLA9.orig_title: Microsoft Word - Mémoire pour l'examen de la LDIPVP - Ombud du Nouveau-Brunswick - 2025.docx
recQ94pxDydS1NLA9.gen_title: New Brunswick Ombudsman submits legislative review recommendations for access to information and privacy law
recQ94pxDydS1NLA9.summary:
On 12 September 2025, the Office of the Access to Information and Privacy Commissioner (New Brunswick) submitted a formal memorandum to the Minister of Finance and Treasury Board outlining recommendations for amendments to the Loi sur le droit à l'information et la protection de la vie privée (LDIPVP). The submission was prepared by Marie-France Pelletier, Ombudsman of New Brunswick, in response to a legislative review of the provincial access to information and privacy statute.

The Ombudsman identified her office as uniquely positioned to make recommendations based on statutory oversight powers granted under section 64.1(1) of the LDIPVP. The memorandum draws on the office's experience in monitoring compliance with the Act and research into best practices observed across Canadian provinces and territories.

The submission contains three parts addressing distinct areas for legislative improvement. Part 1 focuses on amendments to current LDIPVP provisions, including recommendations to expand the definition of "government organisation" and "personal information" to reflect contemporary governance and data protection needs. The Ombudsman also recommends introducing definitions addressing artificial intelligence in government operations, and proposes modifications to response timelines for access requests, the grounds for dismissing applications, and exemptions relating to Cabinet confidentiality and third-party commercial interests.

Part 2 addresses recommended changes to the Règlement général (General Regulation) enacted under the LDIPVP, specifically regarding notification procedures when court proceedings are initiated and the expansion of privacy breach definitions to encompass lost or stolen information. The Ombudsman recommends establishing a formal procedure for the office to initiate appeals and proposes that all judicial decisions and litigation involving the LDIPVP be reported to the Ombudsman's office.

Part 3 sets out broader policy recommendations, including enhanced oversight powers for the Ombudsman, consultation status for the office on legislation affecting access to information and privacy rights, standing as an intervenor in court proceedings, and binding recommendation or order-making authority. The Ombudsman also addresses artificial intelligence governance, documentation obligations for government organisations, and the establishment of a public interest override provision within the LDIPVP framework.
recQ94pxDydS1NLA9.body: Office of the Access to Information and Privacy Commissioner (New Brunswick)

recThmVxUmWGPkHSD.orig_title: Belgium’s ethical hackers invited to strengthen public digital security
recThmVxUmWGPkHSD.gen_title: Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium launches ethical hacking initiative for federal systems
recThmVxUmWGPkHSD.summary:
The Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB) has launched #HackTheGovernment2025, inviting ethical hackers to identify vulnerabilities in selected federal government IT systems between 12 and 26 November 2025.

On 13 November 2025, the CCB announced the second edition of its national initiative coordinating collaboration between public institutions and the cybersecurity community. The programme invites both emerging and experienced ethical hackers to test designated systems including those operated by the CCB itself, the Defence Department, and the Federal Public Service for Digital Agenda (FPS BOSA). Participants are required to identify and report any discovered vulnerabilities responsibly under coordinated vulnerability disclosure principles.

The CCB states that the initiative aims to strengthen digital resilience across Belgium's federal public services by transforming cybersecurity from an isolated technical challenge into a comprehensive national responsibility. The authority emphasises that collaboration with the ethical-hacking community represents a critical strategy for enhancing public sector digital resilience. The programme creates an inclusive platform welcoming both emerging cybersecurity talents and established experts to test and develop their skills responsibly in service of the public interest.

The CCB notes that all findings will be handled according to coordinated vulnerability disclosure principles, ensuring that any identified issues are swiftly addressed by the relevant public services before malicious actors can exploit them. The initiative underscores the shared responsibility of securing Belgium's digital infrastructure through openness, collaboration, and trust between government institutions, the cybersecurity community, and citizens. Beyond formal rewards, the CCB recognises the community spirit fostered by bringing together diverse participants to identify weaknesses in critical public systems proactively.

rec24YME8vI9DmBnG.orig_title: The National Cyber and Information Security Agency of the Czech Republic Co-Seals Publications on Modern Defensible Architecture
rec24YME8vI9DmBnG.gen_title: Czech Cybersecurity Authority Co-Seals Modern Defensible Architecture Publications with International Partners
rec24YME8vI9DmBnG.summary:
On 30 October 2025, the National Cyber and Information Security Agency of the Czech Republic (NÚKIB) co-sealed three publications on modern defensible architecture alongside the Australian Signals Directorate and eight other international cybersecurity authorities.

NÚKIB joined the Canadian Centre for Cyber Security, the National Cyber Security Centre of New Zealand, Japan's Computer Emergency Response Team and National Police Agency, the National Cybersecurity Office and National Intelligence Service of the Republic of Korea, and Germany's Bundesamt für Sicherheit in der Informationstechnik in endorsing the publications. The three-part series addresses foundational principles, senior decision-making frameworks and investment planning for modern defensible architecture implementation.

The first publication, Foundations for Modern Defensible Architecture, outlines baseline secure design and architecture activities that enable organisations to respond to current and emerging cyber threats. NÚKIB states that the foundations provide a cohesive approach for designing, building, maintaining, updating and enhancing digital systems, with each foundation representing an organisational goal or capability that facilitates adoption of zero trust architecture and technologies.

The second publication, Modern Defensible Architecture for Senior Decision Makers, assists executive-level stakeholders in understanding contemporary threat landscapes and how modern defensible architecture supports organisational defence. NÚKIB notes that the guidance presents key factors and questions senior decision makers are required to consider before investing in and implementing modern defensible architecture.

The third publication, Investing in Modern Defensible Architecture, targets ICT managers and enterprise architects. NÚKIB indicates this guidance supports development of modern defensible architecture investment roadmaps and enables readers to make informed decisions on investment opportunities, design considerations and gaps, as well as identify appropriate personnel, skills and technologies.

Lukáš Kintr, Director of NÚKIB, stated that the organisation was pleased to contribute to the third co-sealed series and that the documents represent an important step toward enabling organisations to design and build IT environments that minimise risks to critical systems. NÚKIB finds that the fundamentals and recommendations within the publications align with long-term security standards promoted by the agency.

rec6VNOGQzj25a3it.orig_title: Microsoft Word - ODPC – GUIDANCE NOTES ON HISTORICAL AND STATISTICAL PURPOSES  .docx
rec6VNOGQzj25a3it.gen_title: ODPC issues guidance on lawful bases and controller obligations for historical and statistical data processing
rec6VNOGQzj25a3it.summary:
On 6 November 2025, the Office of the Data Protection Commissioner (ODPC) of Kenya published guidance notes establishing requirements for processing personal data for historical and statistical purposes under the Data Protection Act 2019.

The ODPC identifies seven lawful bases available for such processing: consent, legal obligation, public interest, performance of a task by a public authority, functions of a public nature, legitimate interests, and historical and statistical purposes themselves. The ODPC recognises that further processing of personal data originally collected for other purposes may be compatible with historical and statistical activities, provided controllers satisfy specified conditions.

The guidance establishes mandatory obligations for data controllers undertaking historical or statistical processing. The ODPC requires controllers to identify and apply a lawful basis for processing, implement data minimisation principles, maintain data quality standards, and provide transparent communication to data subjects regarding processing activities. The ODPC further specifies that controllers must ensure data security safeguards, comply with purpose limitation and storage limitation principles, and maintain accountability through documented compliance measures.

The ODPC mandates that controllers respect data subject rights, including access, rectification, and erasure rights where applicable. The ODPC notes that data protection impact assessments are required before commencing high-risk processing, and controllers must notify the ODPC of any personal data breaches within prescribed timeframes. The ODPC emphasises that where controllers engage data processors or collaborate with other controllers, written agreements must govern such arrangements.

The guidance establishes exemptions from certain requirements where processing is undertaken for historical or statistical purposes. The ODPC permits further processing where anonymisation or secure data handling measures are implemented, and permits publication of non-identifiable data without individual consent. The ODPC requires controllers to conduct public interest assessments when relying on public interest exemptions.

The ODPC has issued an accompanying compliance checklist to assist controllers in implementing the requirements outlined in the guidance notes.
rec6VNOGQzj25a3it.body: Office of the Data Protection Commissioner

recLMNrZaXHprn1Z2.orig_title: Opinions of the Advocate General in joined cases C-258/23, C-259/23, C-260/23 Imagens Médicas Integradas
recLMNrZaXHprn1Z2.gen_title: Advocate General finds competition authorities may seize business emails without prior judicial authorisation
recLMNrZaXHprn1Z2.summary:
On 23 October 2025, the Court of Justice of the European Union received an Opinion from Advocate General Laila Medina in joined cases C-258/23, C-259/23 and C-260/23 concerning the seizure of business emails by the Portuguese Competition Authority during competition investigations. The matter arose after companies under investigation objected to email seizures, arguing that prior authorisation from an investigating judge, rather than the Public Prosecutor's Office, was required to protect the fundamental right to secrecy of correspondence.

Advocate General Medina proposes that EU law does not preclude a national competition authority from seizing emails without prior judicial authorisation (Art. 7 Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union). The Advocate General distinguishes the cases from the Bezirkshauptmannschaft Landeck judgment, which concerned access to mobile telephone data in criminal investigations. Medina finds that business email seizures by competition authorities target internal market infringements and relate to legal persons, with only ancillary effects on individuals, unlike mobile telephone access which can provide an in-depth picture of private life.

Regarding interference with the fundamental right to the protection of personal data (Art. 8 Charter of Fundamental Rights), Advocate General Medina determines that proportionality is achieved through adequate procedural safeguards and subsequent judicial review during and at the conclusion of investigations (GDPR, Art. 32). The Advocate General finds that prior judicial authorisation is required only where emails are seized from private residences or to incriminate natural persons under criminal law. Medina adds that EU law permits Member States to establish mechanisms for prior authorisation by judicial authorities, including the Public Prosecutor's Office, if they elect to do so (Directive 2019/1).

The Opinion is non-binding. The Court of Justice will now deliberate on the cases, with judgment to follow at a later date.
recLMNrZaXHprn1Z2.body: Court of Justice of the European Union

recmaad9GuJvoewh0.orig_title: Director of NÃKIB LukÃ¡Å¡ Kintr Visited Australia to Strengthen Cooperation in Cyber Security
recmaad9GuJvoewh0.gen_title: NÃKIB Director visits Australia to advance bilateral cyber security cooperation
recmaad9GuJvoewh0.summary:
Lukáš Kintr, Director of the Czech National Cyber and Information Security Agency (NÃKIB), undertook an official working visit to Australia in October 2025 to strengthen bilateral cooperation on cyber security matters. The visit, the first by NÃKIB leadership to Australia, included consultations with key Australian security institutions and senior government officials across multiple departments.

During the visit to Canberra, the NÃKIB delegation met with representatives of the Australian Cyber Security Centre, which operates under the Australian Signals Directorate, and held a bilateral meeting with the National Cyber Security Coordinator, Lieutenant General Michelle McGuinness CSC. The delegation also participated in a roundtable discussion at the Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade, chaired by Hugh Watson, Acting Ambassador for Cyber Affairs and Critical Technologies, and met with senior officials from the Department of Home Affairs and the Department of Cyber Security. A reception for members of the Australian cyber community was hosted at the Czech Embassy in Australia, opened by Ambassador Jana Tyrer.

The delegation subsequently visited Sydney to observe the operations of the Joint Policing Cybercrime Coordination Centre (JPC3), led by the Australian Federal Police, which NÃKIB notes serves as an exemplary model of inter-agency collaboration between law enforcement authorities and the private sector in combating cybercrime.

NÃKIB states that the main topics discussed during the meetings included threats to critical infrastructure posed by state-sponsored actors, the promotion of trustworthy and secure technologies, and the implications of artificial intelligence development for cyber security. The discussions also addressed protection of energy infrastructure and security of connected vehicles, which NÃKIB identifies as areas of growing strategic importance in both Europe and the Indo-Pacific region. The visit follows the Czech Republic's appointment of its first Cyber Attaché to the region in 2023, which NÃKIB characterises as reflecting the importance of shared democratic values and mutual trust in addressing cross-border cyber threats. NÃKIB notes that the Czech Republic will host the NATO Cyber Champions Summit in 2026.

recYIm1t3kZC4Wtu7.orig_title: Facial recognition technology: fundamental rights considerations in the context of law enforcement
recYIm1t3kZC4Wtu7.gen_title: Fundamental Rights Agency publishes facial recognition technology assessment for law enforcement
recYIm1t3kZC4Wtu7.summary:
On 27 November 2019, the Fundamental Rights Agency (FRA) published a focus paper examining the fundamental rights implications of facial recognition technology (FRT) used by law enforcement and border management authorities across the European Union.

The FRA notes that FRT enables comparison of digital facial images to determine whether they depict the same person. The agency identifies 'live facial recognition technology', which compares footage from video cameras (CCTV) with images held in databases, as a particular application. The FRA observes that examples of national law enforcement authorities in the EU deploying such technology remain sparse, although several are conducting tests of its capabilities.

The FRA recognises that EU law classifies facial images as 'sensitive data' constituting a form of biometric data under the legal framework. The agency identifies several fundamental rights challenges arising from live FRT deployment. The FRA notes that whilst accuracy of facial matching continues to improve, the risk of errors persists, with the agency determining that certain minority groups face heightened error rates. The FRA further identifies that individuals whose facial images are captured and processed may remain unaware of this activity, thereby preventing them from challenging potential misuses of their biometric information.

The FRA's assessment acknowledges that facial images are readily captured in public places, creating operational ease for law enforcement deployment but compounding privacy concerns. The paper outlines and analyses these fundamental rights challenges and their implications for compliance with EU human rights standards. The FRA's analysis informs discussions around the development of regulatory frameworks governing artificial intelligence and automated decision-making systems, particularly those utilising biometric data in law enforcement contexts. The paper was subsequently made available in French and German translations on 1 March 2022.

recN73xuUTfVWT18N.orig_title: Tidspress, lykkehjul og bildemanipulering i markedsfÃ¸ring kan bli forbudt
recN73xuUTfVWT18N.gen_title: Norwegian Communications Authority backs ban on dark patterns in digital marketing under Digital Services Act
recN73xuUTfVWT18N.summary:
The Norwegian Communications Authority (Nkom) has submitted a consultation response supporting restrictions on manipulative design techniques in digital marketing under the European Union's Digital Services Act (DSA). Nkom states that such techniques exploit user vulnerabilities and prevent informed decision-making.

On 14 October 2025, Nkom submitted its formal hearing statement on the implementation of DSA requirements into Norwegian law. The authority finds that prohibited marketing practices should include: images or videos displayed so rapidly that users cannot consciously perceive them, gamification mechanics such as spinning wheels, and time-limited offers with countdown timers that create false urgency. Nkom determines these techniques constitute manipulative design that misleads consumers and restricts their ability to make free and informed choices.

Kamilla Sharma, head of market and services at Nkom, states that manipulative design is widespread practice difficult for users to detect and resist. The authority notes that users are misled not through lack of knowledge but because the underlying technology is highly advanced, employs large datasets and exploits human vulnerabilities. Nkom finds that the DSA must establish clear prohibitions on these techniques to strengthen user rights across all digital platforms regardless of their physical location.

Nkom emphasises particular protection for vulnerable groups. The authority finds that minors and other vulnerable populations require enhanced safeguards against manipulation techniques that exploit their susceptibilities. Nkom states that behaviour-based marketing directed at minors, whereby platforms collect information about children's online behaviour and use it for targeted advertising, breaches rights to equal treatment and consumer protection. The authority determines that the largest digital platforms, including Meta, Snapchat, Amazon and Temu, must bear greater responsibility for how third-party advertisers conduct marketing activities on their services. According to Nkom's submission, digital advertising investments in Norway reached approximately 19 billion Norwegian kroner in 2024.

rec6toAYoPFyr48o5.orig_title: Cybersikkerhed i overvågningsudstyr – vejledning til indkøb, drift og bortskaffelse
rec6toAYoPFyr48o5.gen_title: Centre for Cyber Security Denmark publishes guidance on surveillance equipment security for procurement, operation and disposal
rec6toAYoPFyr48o5.summary:
On 28 February 2023, the Centre for Cyber Security Denmark (Center for Cybersikkerhed) published guidance addressing cyber security risks in surveillance equipment used by public and private organisations. The authority presents concrete recommendations spanning the full lifecycle of surveillance equipment, from procurement and configuration through operation to secure disposal.

The Centre for Cyber Security Denmark identifies surveillance equipment, defined as surveillance cameras and their firmware and software, as Internet of Things (IoT) devices that present multiple security vulnerabilities. The authority notes that surveillance equipment connected to internet infrastructure creates several distinct risks: remote unauthorised viewing of surveillance feeds, potential compromise of organisational networks through infiltration via the equipment, and exposure of sensitive data such as images of persons, products or industrial processes. The authority recognises that organisations must conduct risk assessments based on the sensitivity of data collected.

The authority provides six overarching recommendations. Organisations must ensure that information security in supplier relationships is appropriate. Software must be kept updated across surveillance systems. Network segmentation is required to isolate surveillance equipment from other critical infrastructure. Organisations must maintain records of surveillance equipment locations. Default passwords on surveillance equipment must be changed. Surveillance equipment must be disposed of securely to prevent data recovery and misuse.

The guidance addresses specific operational areas in detail: supplier relationship management, software updates, network segmentation, equipment configuration, and secure disposal procedures. The Centre for Cyber Security Denmark identifies the target audience as staff responsible for procurement and operation of surveillance systems, as well as management responsible for strategic decisions regarding surveillance equipment deployment. The authority emphasises that comprehensive risk management of surveillance equipment is essential to prevent both direct compromise of surveillance feeds and secondary attacks on organisational networks leveraging compromised equipment as entry points.

recKULdjQdfjlUmDH.gen_title: Norwegian Communications Authority reports 70% public support for strict artificial intelligence regulation
recKULdjQdfjlUmDH.summary:
On 1 October 2025, Nasjonal kommunikasjonsmyndighet (Nkom), the Norwegian Communications Authority, published findings from a survey conducted by Respons Analyse showing that 70% of Norwegians support strict laws and regulations governing how artificial intelligence can be used by individuals and companies. The survey, conducted between 19 and 30 June 2025 among 1,062 respondents aged 18 and over, found that only 8% disagreed with the proposition that strict rules should apply to artificial intelligence use. Nkom notes that the survey demonstrates the public perceives artificial intelligence as a powerful tool that is useful but also carries risks.

Nkom states it does not intend to leave the development of artificial intelligence solely to technology companies. The authority's division director, Espen Slette, commented that the findings represent feedback the authority takes seriously. The survey revealed that more women than men, by a margin of 7 percentage points, desire stricter regulation of artificial intelligence.

Nkom has been assigned supervisory responsibility for implementing the European Union's artificial intelligence regulation (AI Act). The new rules establish differentiated requirements for measures depending on the risk category to which artificial intelligence use belongs. To balance innovation opportunities for businesses with user safety, Nkom states it will work with AI-Norge, a industry body, to establish a regulatory artificial intelligence sandbox where companies can test new solutions in controlled environments before market release. Nkom finds this approach may help avoid the need to withdraw solutions later due to high-risk applications.

The authority observes that building trust in artificial intelligence is achieved through ensuring responsible use and reducing risk, comparing the situation to the introduction of traffic rules following the adoption of combustion engines in vehicles. Additionally, Nkom notes that 95% of Kommune-Norge, the association of Norwegian municipalities, seeks clearer direction from the government and state regarding digital transformation, according to the report 'IT in Practice'.

recZEZjyZ1ne2wg7i.orig_title: What gets measured gets managed: How the OECD and European Commission quantify AI investment
recZEZjyZ1ne2wg7i.gen_title: OECD and European Commission release harmonised framework for measuring AI investment across EU
recZEZjyZ1ne2wg7i.summary:
On 26 September 2025, the OECD and the European Commission released a harmonised measurement framework to quantify artificial intelligence spending across the European Union and benchmark it against leading economies including the United States, the United Kingdom, Canada, and Japan. The framework, titled Advancing the Measurement of Investments in Artificial Intelligence, establishes a standardised, transparent approach to measuring AI investment that has previously lacked consistency and independent verification.

The OECD and European Commission define AI investment broadly, encompassing research and development as well as complementary assets such as skills, data infrastructure, computing hardware, and organisational capital. The methodology applies a two-step approach combining macroeconomic data from sources including Eurostat and the EUKLEMS & INTANProd databases with AI intensity coefficients derived from patent data and education statistics to isolate genuine AI-related spending from broader information and communications technology or R&D expenditures.

According to the framework's estimates, total AI investment across the EU27 reached approximately EUR 257 billion in 2023, the most recent year for which estimates are available, ranging from EUR 220 to EUR 294 billion depending on the assumptions used. The OECD and European Commission find that private investment dominates, accounting for 73 per cent of total AI spending. The report identifies skills development as the largest investment area at 41 per cent, followed by data and equipment at 37 per cent, R&D at 13 per cent, and other intellectual property products at 9 per cent. Germany, France, and the Netherlands lead in overall investment, particularly in AI skills and infrastructure, whilst Ireland emerges as the top investor on a per capita basis. The OECD and European Commission note that following modest growth up to 2019, the post-2019 era marked acceleration in AI investments across all categories, particularly in software and development as well as AI-enabling infrastructure.

The framework provides a baseline for the European AI Observatory, a platform launched by the European Commission to monitor AI development, impact and trends across sectors as part of its Apply AI Strategy.

recrL2wL7NzcNpCZZ.gen_title: ENISA identifies public administration as most targeted sector in EU threat landscape 2025
recrL2wL7NzcNpCZZ.summary:
On 24 October 2025, the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) published its Threat Landscape 2025 assessment, documenting threat patterns across EU Member States and identifying public administration as the predominant target sector, accounting for 38.2% of recorded incidents.

ENISA finds that essential entities across five priority sectors—public administration, transport, digital infrastructure and services, finance, and manufacturing—represent 53.7% of total recorded incidents. The agency determines that public administration dominates the sectorial overview, with 38.2% of all incidents, predominantly characterised by low-impact distributed denial of service (DDoS) campaigns accounting for 94.8% of incidents in that sector, though ransomware strains Akira and SafePay disproportionately affect municipalities. ENISA identifies the transport sector as the second most targeted at 7.5%, with state-nexus intrusion sets displaying particular focus on the maritime sector alongside air and logistics operations.

ENISA's analysis of tactics, techniques and procedures identifies phishing as the dominant initial intrusion vector, accounting for approximately 60% of cases including malspam, vishing, and malvertising. The agency determines that vulnerability exploitation represents 21.3% of initial access vectors, with 68% of such incidents leading to malware deployment as follow-up activity. ENISA notes that mobile devices, internet-exposed services, and operational technology systems remain high-value targets across threat categories, and observes that both state-aligned intrusion sets and cybercriminal operators increasingly leverage artificial intelligence for productivity and optimisation of malicious activities.

ENISA assesses that state-aligned cyberespionage activities persist at steady tempo, with APT28, APT29, and Sandworm reportedly the most active intrusion sets targeting public administration, defence, and telecommunications entities. The agency finds that foreign information manipulation and interference campaigns increasingly target EU audiences, with the Matryoshka Information Manipulation Set reported as most active. ENISA determines that ransomware, despite an 11% decrease compared to the previous threat landscape assessment, remains the most impactful cybercrime tool, with initial access brokers continuing to trade low-cost, high-volume VPN and RDP access.

recPWORSL4Xz2E7fc.gen_title: Brazil establishes national artificial intelligence strategy under MCTI governance framework
recPWORSL4Xz2E7fc.summary:
On 6 April 2021, the Brazilian Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation (MCTI) established the Brazilian Artificial Intelligence Strategy (Estratégia Brasileira de Inteligência Artificial, EBIA) through Portaria MCTI nº 4.617, subsequently amended by Portaria MCTI nº 4.979 of 13 July 2021. The EBIA sets out the state's coordinated approach to developing artificial intelligence research, innovation and deployment across Brazil whilst promoting ethical and responsible use of AI systems.

The MCTI states that the EBIA is grounded in five principles endorsed by the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development: inclusive growth, sustainable development and wellbeing; human-centred values and equity; transparency and explainability; robustness, security and protection; and accountability. The strategy identifies nine thematic pillars comprising three transversal axes (legislation, regulation and ethical use; AI governance; international aspects) and six vertical axes (digital skills qualification; workforce and training; research, development, innovation and entrepreneurship; application in productive sectors; public sector application; public security).

The MCTI states that the EBIA's objectives include contributing to ethical principles for responsible AI development and use, promoting sustained investment in AI research and development, removing innovation barriers, building professional capacity in the AI ecosystem, stimulating Brazilian AI innovation in international contexts, and fostering cooperation between public and private entities, industry and research centres. The MCTI notes the strategy was developed through three stages: specialised AI consultancy engagement, national and international benchmarking, and public consultation. The MCTI reports that approximately 1,000 contributions were received during public consultation conducted between 12 December 2019 and 3 March 2020.

Under Portaria MCTI nº 4.617, the MCTI is required to create governance instances to prioritise, implement, monitor and update strategic actions; coordinate implementation of the EBIA; invite public, private and academic institutions to support strategic action; prepare evaluation reports on implementation; publish reports on its website; and assess update frequency for the strategy. The MCTI states that EBIA governance comprises the MCTI, the MCTI/EMBRAPII Network for Digital Technologies and Innovation, and invited institutions.
recPWORSL4Xz2E7fc.body: Brazilian Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation

recTtgS7WFOMP5m9U.orig_title: Czech Government Approves New National Cyber Security Strategy
recTtgS7WFOMP5m9U.gen_title: Czech Government approves National Cyber Security Strategy effective 2026
recTtgS7WFOMP5m9U.summary:
On 3 September 2025, the Czech Government approved a new National Cyber Security Strategy (NCSS) to take effect from 2026, replacing the previous strategy in force since 2021. The National Cyber and Information Security Agency (NÃKIB) states that the strategy responds to deteriorating global security conditions and recent technological changes, whilst emphasising enhanced cooperation at national and international levels.

NÃKIB, as the lead authority responsible for the NCSS, notes that the strategy was developed with input from dozens of public and private sector organisations through public consultation processes. The agency states that implementation will occur via an action plan currently under preparation, which will assign specific tasks to responsible institutions to fulfil individual strategic objectives. NÃKIB finds that implementation of these tasks will be evaluated annually and reported to the Czech Government.

The NCSS identifies three principal strategic areas. First, secure strategic infrastructure: NÃKIB recognises the need to strengthen resilience of strategic infrastructure and enhance the state's ability to detect and counter cyber threats. Second, whole-of-society preparedness and development: the agency finds that this encompasses developing citizens' digital competences, increasing the number and motivation of cyber security professionals, and supporting innovation. Third, international cooperation and pursuit of interests: NÃKIB states that Czechia will pursue an active role within the EU, NATO and other international organisations in its own defence and in protecting an open and free digital space.

According to the strategy, NÃKIB determines that Czechia must make greater use of new technologies to secure organisations, prepare for potential transitions to crisis states, and improve working conditions for cyber security experts in public administration. The agency finds that Czechia must develop secure alternatives to risky technologies, build new platforms for information sharing, and pursue an internationally coordinated approach to deterring malicious state actors. NÃKIB Director Lukáš Kintr stated that the strategy confirms Czechia's capacity to respond to the current security environment whilst seizing opportunities presented by new technologies and widespread digitalisation, emphasising the necessity to detect threats and vulnerabilities in advance rather than respond passively.

recKeZjsihB8fzGsm.gen_title: Saarland LfDI publishes guidance on information obligations for third-country data transfers in medical research
recKeZjsihB8fzGsm.summary:
On 18 September 2025, the Saarland LfDI, on behalf of the Konferenz der unabhängigen Datenschutzaufsichtsbehörden des Bundes und der Länder (Conference of Independent Data Protection Authorities), published recommendations regarding information obligations when personal data is transferred to third countries or international organisations for medical research purposes.

The guidance addresses two layers of information requirements under the GDPR. First, controllers must comply with the specific information obligations under Articles 13 and 14 GDPR when data transfers to third countries are planned. The Saarland LfDI specifies that data subjects must receive general information about the planned third-country transfer, including identification of the recipient country or countries, any onward transfers to additional third countries, and the legal basis for transfer under Chapter V GDPR, whether an adequacy decision, standard contractual clauses, binding corporate rules or other mechanisms.

Second, the authority identifies distinct information requirements depending on the transfer mechanism. Where transfers rely on appropriate safeguards under Article 46 GDPR, controllers must inform data subjects that the recipient country lacks an adequacy decision and must provide access to or identify the location of the safeguards themselves. Where transfers depend on derogations under Article 49 GDPR, controllers must disclose that data in the third country may lack any meaningful protection or protection equivalent to EU standards, specify the precise Article 49 derogation being relied upon, and where applicable, identify the specific public interest under Article 49(1)(d) and 49(4).

Where transfers rest on explicit consent under Article 49(1)(a) GDPR, the guidance establishes enhanced requirements for informed consent. Controllers must transparently, intelligibly and concretely inform data subjects about the situation in the third country, the absence of guarantees under EU law, and the resultant absence of adequate data protection levels. The authority requires controllers to identify in good faith all deficits known or which should be apparent from relevant publications of the European Data Protection Board and other authoritative sources, enabling data subjects to assess risks to their data.

rec4hKrBOYHeO7Ij1.orig_title: Judgment of the Court (First Chamber) of 4 September 2025. — European Data Protection Supervisor v Single Resolution Board. — Appeal – Protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data – Procedure for granting compensation
rec4hKrBOYHeO7Ij1.gen_title: Court of Justice upholds EDPS decision that Single Resolution Board breached data protection obligations in bank resolution procedure
rec4hKrBOYHeO7Ij1.summary:
On 4 September 2025, the Court of Justice of the European Union (First Chamber) allowed an appeal by the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) and set aside a General Court judgment that had annulled an EDPS decision finding the Single Resolution Board (SRB) had failed to fulfil obligations under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 relating to the processing of personal data.

The case concerned five complaints submitted by shareholders and creditors of a banking institution following its resolution. The EDPS adopted a decision on 24 June 2020 addressing those complaints, and the SRB subsequently requested review. On 24 November 2020, the EDPS issued a revised decision. The General Court annulled that revised decision on 26 April 2023, prompting the EDPS to appeal to the Court of Justice.

The Court of Justice examined whether the SRB had complied with its obligations under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725, particularly Article 15(1)(d) concerning the obligation to inform data subjects. The case turned on the application of data protection principles to pseudonymised data transmitted to third parties during the bank resolution process. The Court found that the regulatory framework requires that pseudonymised data which could be attributed to a natural person by the use of additional information must be considered information on an identifiable natural person and therefore constitutes personal data (Art. 3(1) and Art. 3(6), Regulation (EU) 2018/1725).

The Court stated that Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 must be interpreted homogeneously with Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR) where the provisions follow the same principles. The judgment confirms that the EDPS correctly determined the SRB had failed to meet its data protection obligations in processing the personal data of the affected shareholders and creditors during the bank resolution procedure, particularly regarding transparency and notification requirements.
rec4hKrBOYHeO7Ij1.body: Court of Justice of the European Union

recCOcoKhQ1pkWsnY.orig_title: サイバー対処能力強化法及び同整備法について
recCOcoKhQ1pkWsnY.gen_title: Japan enacts Cyber Response Capacity Enhancement Act and related legislative reforms
recCOcoKhQ1pkWsnY.summary:
On 23 May 2025, the Japanese government enacted the Act on Prevention of Damage from Fraudulent Activities against Critical Electronic Computers (Law No. 42 of 2025) and the related legislative reform act (Law No. 43 of 2025), establishing a legal framework for active cyber defence across government and critical infrastructure sectors.

The Information-technology Promotion Agency confirms that the legislation was deliberated and amended by the Diet before passing on 16 May 2025. The National Cyber Security Strategy (decided 16 December 2022) and expert committee recommendations (compiled 29 November 2024) informed the acts' development. The Cabinet Office National Cyber Coordination Room indicates that the laws address four key areas: strengthening public-private partnership, establishing legal authority for communications information use, enabling access and neutralisation measures against attack servers, and reorganising the National centre of Incident Readiness and Strategy (NISC) with new centralised policy coordination functions.

Under the enhancement act, critical infrastructure operators must now report to their sector regulator and the Prime Minister when deploying specific critical electronic computers and when becoming aware of cybersecurity incidents or related causative events. The Prime Minister is authorised to establish an information sharing and countermeasure coordination council comprising government agencies, critical infrastructure operators and vendor representatives, who must maintain confidentiality obligations. The legislation empowers the Prime Minister and relevant sector ministers to provide vulnerability information to electronic computer suppliers and, in cases affecting critical infrastructure, to request necessary preventative measures.

The related reform act establishes legal authority for communications information use to detect attack servers and enables neutralisation measures against confirmed attack infrastructure. The acts incorporate constitutional protections requiring that powers be exercised strictly within the minimum necessary scope to achieve the stated purpose and that communication secrecy and other constitutionally guaranteed rights not be unjustly restricted. Implementation of public-private cooperation provisions will occur within eighteen months of publication through orders to be prescribed by cabinet ordinance.

rec9zRGZX8N3LRB9L.orig_title: NÃKIB, in cooperation with U.S. agencies CISA and NSA, joins the international document A Shared Vision of Software Bill of Materials for Cybersecurity
rec9zRGZX8N3LRB9L.gen_title: Czech cybersecurity authority endorses international software bill of materials guidance
rec9zRGZX8N3LRB9L.summary:
On 3 September 2025, the National Cyber and Information Security Agency of the Czech Republic (NÚKIB) joined an international guidance document titled "A Shared Vision of Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for Cybersecurity", issued by the U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) and the U.S. National Security Agency (NSA) in cooperation with cybersecurity authorities from 17 additional countries and territories. NÚKIB has been an active member of the Global SBOM Forum since early 2025 on behalf of the Czech Republic.

The joint guidance document recognises that a Software Bill of Materials operates as a machine-readable record of all components and libraries used in software development. NÚKIB states that as modern software increasingly depends on external and open-source components, SBOM represents a fundamental step toward greater supply chain transparency and improved protection against cyber threats. The authority notes that when a vulnerability is discovered in a particular component, SBOM enables rapid identification of its location and allows targeted remedial measures, resulting in faster updates, more stable digital services, and more efficient software maintenance and auditing expenditure.

The authoring organisations determined that widespread SBOM adoption is essential to creating software aligned with the secure-by-design principle, whereby security is integrated from the outset of development. The document emphasises the necessity of aligning technical standards across countries and sectors to ensure SBOM functions consistently, remains interoperable, and can be implemented at scale. The guidance notes that a common framework will reduce complexity, increase efficiency, and strengthen trust in the digital environment.

Lukáš Kintr, Director of NÚKIB, stated that modern software increasingly comprises hundreds of components from various sources and libraries, and that SBOM brings essential transparency to this environment. The authority regards SBOM as a key step toward creating secure and resilient software from the design phase, contributing to an environment in which citizens and institutions can rely with greater confidence on technologies powering modern software.

recX605TpaZtxZKhd.orig_title: Der Bundesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit
recX605TpaZtxZKhd.gen_title: German federal data protection authority publishes consultation response on AI models and personal data
recX605TpaZtxZKhd.summary:
On 31 August 2025, the Bundesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit (BfDI), Germany's federal data protection authority, published an anonymised consultation response addressing the handling of personal data in artificial intelligence models in compliance with data protection law.

The BfDI issued the response as part of a formal consultation procedure titled "datenschutzkonformer Umgang mit personenbezogenen Daten in KI-Modellen" (data protection compliant handling of personal data in AI models). The response, designated as anonymised statement number 2, was made available as a downloadable PDF document on the authority's website.

The consultation process initiated by the BfDI seeks to establish guidance on how organisations can lawfully process personal data when developing, training and deploying artificial intelligence systems. The authority's response contributes to the development of practical standards for data protection compliance in the AI context, addressing questions about the permissible use of personal data in machine learning and model development.

The BfDI's role encompasses oversight of data protection compliance across multiple sectors within Germany's jurisdiction. The authority supervises public bodies including administrative agencies, courts, parliaments and public foundations, as well as social security organisations such as health insurance providers. Private enterprises are generally subject to supervision by state-level authorities, with certain exceptions falling under federal jurisdiction. The BfDI also provides guidance on data protection obligations in specialised sectors including media, broadcasting and religious organisations, which operate under distinct regulatory frameworks with dedicated data protection officers.

The publication of this consultation response reflects the BfDI's engagement with evolving data protection challenges posed by artificial intelligence deployment. The anonymised nature of the submission preserves confidentiality while contributing to the broader regulatory dialogue on AI governance and personal data protection in Germany.
recX605TpaZtxZKhd.body: Bundesbeauftragte fur den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit

rec0lEMqlZzE6zBXQ.orig_title: KI-Modelle und personenbezogene Daten - Stellungnahme Friedrich-Alexander-Universität, Prof. Dr. Pesch
rec0lEMqlZzE6zBXQ.gen_title: Friedrich-Alexander-Universität submits statement on AI models and personal data to German federal data protection authority
rec0lEMqlZzE6zBXQ.summary:
On 31 August 2025, the Bundesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit (BfDI), the German Federal Data Protection and Freedom of Information Commissioner, published a statement from Professor Dr. Pesch of Friedrich-Alexander-Universität as part of a public consultation procedure on data protection-compliant handling of personal data in artificial intelligence models.

The BfDI initiated a consultation process seeking input from academic and industry stakeholders on the lawful processing of personal data within AI model development and deployment. The submission from Friedrich-Alexander-Universität represents academic perspective on the intersection of AI technology and data protection obligations under German and European law.

As an independent federal authority, the BfDI supervises data protection compliance across German public administration, federal agencies, and certain other entities falling under federal jurisdiction. The publication of Professor Pesch's statement reflects the BfDI's engagement with external expertise to inform its guidance and potential regulatory positions on AI and personal data issues. The consultation procedure addresses practical and legal challenges organisations face when deploying AI systems while maintaining compliance with data protection principles.

The statement has been made publicly available as part of the BfDI's transparent consultation process, allowing stakeholders to access academic analysis on data protection requirements applicable to AI models. This consultation contributes to developing clearer standards for organisations implementing AI systems across Germany, particularly regarding lawful bases for processing, data minimisation, purpose limitation, and transparency obligations that arise in machine learning contexts.
rec0lEMqlZzE6zBXQ.body: Bundesbeauftragte fur den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit

recXwFpxCgXiXL0oD.orig_title: National Cyber and Information Security Agency
recXwFpxCgXiXL0oD.gen_title: Czech cybersecurity agency warns of data transfer and remote access threats from China
recXwFpxCgXiXL0oD.summary:
On 3 September 2025, the National Cyber and Information Security Agency (NÚKIB), pursuant to Section 12(1) of Act No. 181/2014 Coll. on cyber security, issued a formal warning of a cyber security threat consisting of the transfer of system and user data to the People's Republic of China, its Special Administrative Regions, or entities based in those territories, and the remote administration of technical assets carried out from those territories or by entities based there.

NÚKIB evaluated the threat as High, determining it to be likely to very likely. The Agency concluded that obliged authorities and persons required to implement security measures under the Cyber Security Act must evaluate this threat within their risk management frameworks at an equivalent High level, in accordance with Section 5(1)(d) of Decree No. 82/2018 Coll. on security measures and cyber security incidents. Where organisations employ alternative risk evaluation methods under Section 5 of Annex 2 to the Cyber Security Decree, NÚKIB states the threat must be assessed at a comparable severity level.

In its reasoning, NÚKIB identified the trustworthiness of China's legal environment as a critical factor. The Agency found that the People's Republic of China's legal framework, including the 2015 National Security Law, the 2017 National Intelligence Law and the 2014 Counter-Espionage Law, grants government authorities significant tools to enforce cooperation from private companies in state security and espionage activities. NÚKIB notes that the National Intelligence Law Article 7 imposes a duty on all citizens and organisations to support national intelligence activities, whilst the Counter-Espionage Law enables state authorities to require companies to provide information on foreign clients suspected of espionage, with companies having limited control over such disclosures.

NÚKIB determined that the broad and vague definitions within China's legal framework, particularly regarding national security and espionage, allow state authorities to require companies to provide extensive information with minimal independent judicial oversight. The Agency based its assessment on facts established during its exercise of powers, supplemented by classified and unclassified information from domestic and foreign partners, including reference to findings highlighted in the Security Information Service's 2024 annual report.

recanAYY5Ck2jljoF.gen_title: Hamburg DPA fines credit collection firm €900,000 for retaining personal data beyond deletion deadlines
recanAYY5Ck2jljoF.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) has imposed an administrative fine of €900,000 (approximately €900,000) on a Hamburg-based credit collection service provider for unlawfully retaining personal data records beyond statutory deletion periods.

On 12 November 2024, the HmbBfDI announced the enforcement action following a sector-wide inspection of major firms in the credit collection industry. The HmbBfDI notes that Hamburg is a leading European centre for debt collection services, where sensitive data concerning defaulting debtors is routinely processed and shared with credit reference agencies and address tracing services.

During on-site inspections conducted following preliminary written investigations, the HmbBfDI found that one company had retained a six-figure quantity of personal data records without legal basis until mid-November 2023, in breach of Article 5(1)(a) and Article 6(1) GDPR. The HmbBfDI determined that although the originally processed records had not been transferred to third parties during the retention period, some records remained in the company's database for up to five years after the statutory retention deadline had expired. The fine decision is final. The HmbBfDI states that the company acknowledged the violation, accepted the fine, and cooperated professionally with the authority during remediation, factors considered in the penalty calculation.

The HmbBfDI's investigation examined data storage and processing practices across multiple firms through detailed questionnaires and document requests covering processing activity registers, security measures documentation, and template correspondence. The HmbBfDI found that most companies demonstrated professional standards and sensitivity in data handling. Through dialogue, the authority achieved improvements in transparency towards data subjects, particularly regarding the formulation of meaningful data access responses under Article 15 GDPR and timely access procedures.

The HmbBfDI identified comparable deletion obligation deficiencies in a second inspected company; that enforcement proceeding remains ongoing. Thomas Fuchs, the HmbBfDI, stated that companies must delete personal data immediately or within established periods upon relationship termination, and that firms must conduct a prior assessment of which data are collected and retention duration limits.
recanAYY5Ck2jljoF.body: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

recqpMjs6HQAVdZ8y.gen_title: France PEReN releases international joint testing report on agentic AI safety across leakage, fraud and cybersecurity risks
recqpMjs6HQAVdZ8y.summary:
On 17 July 2025, France's PEReN released a joint evaluation report on agentic AI testing methodologies conducted across an international network of AI Safety Institutes and government mandated offices from Singapore, Japan, Australia, Canada, the European Commission, France, Kenya, South Korea and the United Kingdom.

The exercise assessed two primary strands of agentic safety risk: leakage of sensitive information and fraud, led by Singapore AISI, and cybersecurity threats, led by the UK AI Security Institute. PEReN states that the joint testing exercise evaluated how safe large language models are when deployed as autonomous agents and how effective those models are as judges of agent behaviour. The assessment covered nine languages, including Farsi, French, Hindi, Japanese, Kiswahili, Korean, Mandarin Chinese and Telugu.

The testing methodology involved presenting tasks to two agent models across the two risk categories, with each agent provided with tool selection capabilities to complete tasks through reasoning and tool calls. PEReN notes that agent trajectories, consisting of task prompts, tool calls with reasoning, and final outputs, were evaluated on a pass/fail basis against specific scenarios. For malicious tasks, the authority identifies two fail scenarios: complete execution of harmful activity and partial execution of harmful activity. Evaluations were conducted using both judge-LLMs and subsequent human annotation, with metrics centring on pass rates and discrepancy rates between judge model assessments and human evaluation.

PEReN emphasises that given the nascency of agentic testing methodology, test results and model capabilities are not the primary focus. The authority states the exercise's intent is to understand methodological issues in conducting agentic testing to inform future evaluation efforts and support the Network's collaboration in building common best practices for testing AI agents. The report includes language-specific deep dives examining leakage and fraud risks, additional analysis of prompt variations and virtual machine bugs within the cybersecurity evaluation strand, and an overarching conclusion addressing key findings across all tested domains.

recKxRS1uQyYMzUnA.orig_title: HmbBfDI stellt Tätigkeitsbericht Datenschutz 2023 vor | HmbBfDI
recKxRS1uQyYMzUnA.gen_title: HmbBfDI 2023 activity report finds rising data breaches and calls for unified AI supervision by data protection authorities
recKxRS1uQyYMzUnA.summary:
On 15 April 2024, the Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) presented its 2023 activity report to Hamburg's parliament president, detailing enforcement trends and regulatory priorities across the jurisdiction.

The HmbBfDI reports that complaint cases increased by approximately 20 per cent to 2,537 in 2023 from 2,160 in 2022, with the rise largely attributable to complaints concerning Meta and Google products and the HmbBfDI's lead responsibility for these companies in Germany. The authority also found that reported data breaches rose to 925 cases from 859 in 2022, whilst reported hacker attacks increased to 235 from 227 in the prior year. The HmbBfDI initiated 20 administrative fine proceedings in 2023. Thomas Fuchs, the data protection officer, notes that since 2019 Hamburg has recorded a significant rise in reported hacker attacks, from 74 cases in that year to 235 in 2023.

The HmbBfDI identifies inadequate handling of outdated personal data as a systemic issue across multiple sectors including debt collection services and hospitality. The authority finds that organisations routinely fail to delete personal data sets no longer required for processing purposes, such as identity document copies or financial records, in violation of data protection rules and reflecting poor data management practices. Fuchs states that organisations must establish deletion concepts as part of their foundational data protection infrastructure, requiring inventories of collected data and retention periods before collection begins. The authority determines that retention of data without concrete purposes violates the rights of data subjects.

Regarding artificial intelligence oversight, the HmbBfDI argues in its report that data protection authorities are the appropriate bodies to supervise AI system compliance, given their existing knowledge of public and private sector IT systems processing personal data. Fuchs contends that establishing separate supervision structures would increase bureaucratic burden. He calls for comprehensive data protection authority jurisdiction over AI applications to ensure unified control of risks affecting health, security and fundamental rights protection.
recKxRS1uQyYMzUnA.body: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

recJ4ZesixhFnYqPs.orig_title: Gastzugang im Onlinehandel – OLG Hamburg bestätigt HmbBfDI | HmbBfDI
recJ4ZesixhFnYqPs.gen_title: Hamburg data protection authority confirms guest checkout requirement for online retailers, with marketplace exceptions
recJ4ZesixhFnYqPs.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) has determined that online retailers must offer guest checkout options to comply with data protection requirements, following a review of Hamburg-based e-commerce platforms in January 2025. The HmbBfDI identified a major clothing retailer that did not provide guest ordering functionality and required customers to create permanent accounts. The authority instructed the company to introduce guest checkout, which it subsequently implemented.

On 27 February 2025, the Hanseatisches Oberlandesgericht (Hanseatic Higher Regional Court) confirmed the HmbBfDI's position in the case Az. 5 U 30/24, rejecting a claim by the Verbraucherzentrale Nordrhein-Westfalen that sought to compel Otto.de to offer guest checkout. The HmbBfDI provided expert testimony to the lower court, which the appellate court considered as "soft law" in reaching its decision.

The Data Protection Conference (DSK) established in a resolution of 24 March 2022 that online merchants must generally not force customers to create accounts, but must enable guest orders without permanent registration. The HmbBfDI finds that the principle of data minimisation requires processing only necessary data; customer accounts often contain unnecessary information and expose personal data to hacking risks that not all customers are willing to accept.

The HmbBfDI recognises exceptions where legitimate grounds exist. Otto.de, operating as an online marketplace hosting numerous third-party sellers, met the conditions for exemption. The authority finds that the centralised customer service and returns management through a single account can be implemented more efficiently, and Otto.de implemented alternative measures including automated deletion of inactive accounts after a specified period.
