The court determined that mandatory account creation does not breach data minimisation requirements when accounts are automatically deleted after inactivity and only necessary data is collected, with data erasure following warranty periods.

The majority of retailers reviewed during the control visit already offered guest checkout options, implementing the DSK requirement before or after the 2022 resolution. However, another platform claiming marketplace status was directed to provide concrete usage figures demonstrating comparability to established marketplaces; a general assertion of third-party merchant status is insufficient. The HmbBfDI will continue monitoring Hamburg-based online retailers' compliance with guest checkout obligations.
recJ4ZesixhFnYqPs.body: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

reccePaP1i8Ff5jHR.orig_title: Développement des systèmes d’IA : les recommandations de la CNIL pour respecter le RGPD
reccePaP1i8Ff5jHR.gen_title: CNIL publishes recommendations on GDPR compliance for AI system development
reccePaP1i8Ff5jHR.summary:
On 22 July 2025, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL) published its first recommendations on applying the GDPR to the development of artificial intelligence systems, establishing that GDPR compliance and AI innovation are compatible objectives.

The CNIL addresses a common concern among AI developers that GDPR requirements obstruct innovation in Europe. The authority clarifies that this perception is incorrect, but recognises that training datasets frequently contain personal data relating to real individuals. The CNIL states that use of such data creates risks to individuals that must be managed to ensure AI systems are developed in conditions respecting fundamental rights and privacy (Art. 5, 6 GDPR).

The recommendations apply to AI system development involving personal data processing, covering machine learning systems, operationally defined systems, and general-purpose AI systems. The CNIL finds the scope encompasses systems with one-time learning or continuous learning using operational data for improvement. The recommendations address the development phase only, defined as all stages prior to deployment: system design, dataset creation, and training. The CNIL notes that where personal data is used for AI development, both the GDPR and the EU AI Regulation, adopted in summer 2024, apply concurrently.

The CNIL establishes that systems relying on personal data must be developed with a defined, explicit and legitimate purpose or objective. The authority rejects arguments that purpose requirements are incompatible with AI training that may develop unanticipated characteristics, finding instead that purpose definition must be adapted to the AI context without elimination. For general-purpose AI systems, the CNIL requires developers to specify the system type and technically foreseeable functionalities rather than employing overly general descriptions such as "development and improvement of an AI system". Best practice includes identifying highest-risk capabilities, excluded functionalities, and usage conditions such as open source release, commercialisation, or API provision. For research-oriented AI systems, the CNIL permits less detailed initial objectives, with supplementary information clarifying the purpose as projects progress.

The recommendations require developers using personal data to determine their responsibilities under the GDPR, establishing whether they operate as controller or joint controller.
reccePaP1i8Ff5jHR.body: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

recJ0RaL8H1PJxzE2.orig_title: Contrôle de l’existence d’un appel en cours pendant l’utilisation d’une application bancaire : quels sont vos droits ?
recJ0RaL8H1PJxzE2.gen_title: CNIL clarifies data rights for banking app call-detection fraud prevention
recJ0RaL8H1PJxzE2.summary:
On 15 July 2025, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL) issued guidance on the lawfulness and data protection requirements for banking applications that detect incoming telephone calls to prevent fraud by identifying when customers may be under the influence of scammers during financial transactions.

The CNIL explains that some banks collect data on the existence and duration of telephone calls on a customer's device and cross-reference this information with transaction signals such as transfer amounts, new beneficiary additions, or transfers to higher-risk countries. The CNIL states that these systems detect suspicious situations in real time to block or alert customers to potentially fraudulent operations, but do not record or listen to call content.

Because these systems process personal data, they are subject to the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). The CNIL requires that banking applications obtain free and informed consent from customers before accessing telephone call information. Customers must retain the ability to refuse such access without degradation of service or increased fees. The CNIL determines that if a customer withholds consent, sensitive functions such as transfers or adding beneficiaries may be blocked in the mobile application, but the bank must provide alternative access through its website, telephone or branch networks. The CNIL recommends that frequently-used functions be available on the bank's website and less-common functions via telephone or branch.

The CNIL states that customers may withdraw consent at any time through a simple, clear mechanism, such as a link in the application leading to device settings. Customers retain rights to access information about how their data is used, to object to processing, and to correct personal data (GDPR Articles 15, 21, 16). The CNIL notes that banks may refuse certain requests to delete data if retention is required by European anti-fraud rules or to respond to customer complaints, but must clearly explain such refusals. If customers disagree with a bank's response or receive no response, they may lodge a complaint with the CNIL.
recJ0RaL8H1PJxzE2.body: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rec0LohLIW6BL0i5C.orig_title: AI Standards: Building trust, ensuring innovation in networked world
rec0LohLIW6BL0i5C.gen_title: ITU AI for Good hosts keynote on artificial intelligence standards and trust building
rec0LohLIW6BL0i5C.summary:
The International Telecommunication Union's AI for Good initiative is hosting a keynote session on 11 July 2025 addressing the development of artificial intelligence standards to build trust and enable innovation across networked systems. The 20-minute keynote presentation runs from 10:45 to 11:05 Central European Summer Time, with simultaneous access available to participants in multiple time zones including New York (EST), Beijing (CST), Los Angeles (PST) and Perth, Australia (AWST).

The event forms part of the ITU AI for Good Global Summit programme, which convenes stakeholders to discuss governance frameworks and technical standards for artificial intelligence deployment. The initiative operates under the umbrella of the UN's broader AI for Good programme, which coordinates international efforts to harness artificial intelligence for sustainable development objectives.

The keynote addresses the intersection of trust-building mechanisms and innovation enablement in networked artificial intelligence systems. Standards development represents a core mechanism through which international bodies seek to establish interoperability requirements, safety benchmarks and ethical principles for AI systems operating across borders. The ITU, as the UN's specialised agency for information and communication technologies, has positioned standards development as central to its AI governance approach.

Participation in the event is structured around regional classification categories established by the UN Statistics Division's M49 methodology. The ITU notes that regional classifications are utilised exclusively for ticketing purposes for the summit, unless otherwise specified. The event accommodates both in-person and online attendance, reflecting the hybrid delivery model adopted across the broader summit programme.

The keynote is one element of a broader summit schedule encompassing multiple sessions, workshops and panel discussions scheduled across 7 July 2025. The timing and structure of the keynote address, positioned as a centrepiece programme element, indicates the priority accorded to standards-setting discussions within the ITU's artificial intelligence governance agenda for 2025.

recCEtGByRYugZns7.gen_title: ENISA publishes European Cybersecurity Skills Framework with 12 professional role profiles
recCEtGByRYugZns7.summary:
The European Cybersecurity Agency (ENISA) has published the European Cybersecurity Skills Framework (ECSF), a practical tool establishing common role profiles for cybersecurity professionals across the EU. ENISA states the ECSF identifies 12 typical cybersecurity professional role profiles, each with defined titles, missions, tasks, skills, knowledge and competences. The framework serves as the EU reference point for defining and assessing relevant skills, as defined in the Cybersecurity Skills Academy policy initiative adopted by the European Commission in April 2023.

ENISA notes the ECSF summarises cybersecurity-related roles into 12 profiles individually analysed for responsibilities, skills, synergies and interdependencies. The agency identifies key functions of the framework as providing a common understanding of relevant roles and competencies, facilitating recognition of cybersecurity skills, and supporting the design of cybersecurity-related training programmes. ENISA presented the framework and user manual at the first ENISA cybersecurity skills conference in September 2022. The Commission subsequently designated the ECSF as the basis on which the Cybersecurity Skills Academy will define and assess relevant skills, monitor evolution of skill gaps and provide indications on new needs.

ENISA demonstrates the framework's ongoing development through successive conferences. The agency notes the 2nd edition conference in September 2023 in Segovia, Spain, and the 3rd Skills Conference, hosted with the Hungarian Presidency of the Council of the EU, confirmed the ECSF's maturity and versatility. ENISA identifies that the framework supports workforce planning aligned with NIS2 obligations, adapts to the AI domain, and enables skills attestation for cybersecurity professionals. ENISA documents availability of multiple resources including an interactive tool, role profiles document, user manual, and formats in xlsx and json. The agency notes mapping with ESCO, the multilingual classification of European Skills, Competences and Occupations, and an application to aviation developed by the European Union Aviation Safety Agency. ENISA states the ECSF conference 2026, hosted by Cypriot Presidency of the Council of the EU, will allow stakeholders to exchange experiences and share best practices.

rec17geN8WMkbtQin.gen_title: Centre for Cyber Security Denmark publishes sector-specific NIS 2 guidance for municipalities
rec17geN8WMkbtQin.summary:
On 1 July 2025, Styrelsen for Samfundssikkerhed (Centre for Cyber Security Denmark) published sector-specific guidance to municipalities on the implementation of the NIS 2 Act in a municipal context. The guidance is intended to support the 98 Danish municipalities in implementing NIS 2 obligations and clarifies how the law applies to municipal entities and related organisations.

The Centre for Samfundssikkerhed states in the guidance that it holds advisory responsibility for municipalities as the sector-responsible authority. The guidance supplements cross-cutting NIS 2 guidance and addresses the particular circumstances of Danish municipal administration. The Centre notes that municipalities operate across multiple sectors through decentralised institutions including schools, childcare facilities and various technical and environmental services. A substantial portion of municipal tasks are delivered by entities with varying degrees of attachment to the municipality, including municipal companies and private care homes.

The guidance clarifies that entities affiliated with municipalities may fall within the scope of NIS 2 if they deliver services within one or more sectors listed in Annexes I and II of the NIS 2 Act. The Centre states that such entities would then be subject to the competent authority for the relevant sector in which they operate, rather than municipal oversight. The guidance indicates that municipality coverage under NIS 2 applies to entities defined as legal or natural persons created and recognised under national law capable of exercising rights and assuming obligations.

The Centre specifies that the guidance is advisory in nature and does not provide definitive answers in all circumstances regarding scope or implementation. The Centre confirms that the supervisory authority will assess in each case whether an entity meets its obligations under NIS 2. The guidance addresses common IT solutions shared across municipalities and the risk-based approach to implementing cybersecurity measures, supplementing the cross-cutting guidance on implementing cybersecurity measures under NIS 2.

rec1gUnpaKQt3l1uS.orig_title: Guide to Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations
rec1gUnpaKQt3l1uS.gen_title: ICO publishes guide to Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations
rec1gUnpaKQt3l1uS.summary:
On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office published a guide to the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations (PECR) for organisations sending electronic marketing messages, using cookies, or providing electronic communication services to the public.

The ICO states that the guide is designed to assist organisations in applying the Regulations through practical examples and answers to frequently asked questions. The guidance covers electronic marketing communications transmitted by phone, fax, email or text message, as well as the use of cookies and the provision of electronic communication services to the public.

The ICO notes that the guide is under review and subject to change following the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 coming into law on the same date. The authority has indicated that organisations should refer to its Plans for new and updated guidance page for information about which guidance will be updated and the timing of those updates. The ICO recognises that the legislative framework is evolving and has committed to keeping its guidance current as new legal obligations take effect.

recsEpMBLMkVYbYcQ.orig_title: AI and data protection risk toolkit
recsEpMBLMkVYbYcQ.gen_title: ICO releases AI and data protection risk toolkit for organisations
recsEpMBLMkVYbYcQ.summary:
On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office published an AI and data protection risk toolkit designed to support organisations in identifying and mitigating risks to individuals' rights and freedoms arising from their own artificial intelligence systems.

The toolkit is provided as a practical resource to assist organisations in managing data protection compliance in the context of AI development and deployment. The ICO notes that the toolkit is available for download in Microsoft Excel format and is compatible with other spreadsheet programmes. The resource forms part of the ICO's broader guidance on artificial intelligence and data protection obligations.

The ICO acknowledges that its AI and data protection guidance is under review following the coming into force of the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 on 19 June 2025. The authority states that guidance in this area may be subject to change and directs organisations to its Plans for new and updated guidance page for information on which guidance will be updated and when updates will be published.

The toolkit represents the ICO's approach to providing organisations with structured support to address the intersection of artificial intelligence systems and data protection compliance. By offering a downloadable resource, the ICO enables organisations to conduct self-assessment of their AI-related data protection risks. The toolkit is intended to help organisations systematically evaluate how their AI systems may impact the rights and freedoms of individuals whose data they process, consistent with the UK General Data Protection Regulation framework.

receu4YfLqDNlrfEz.orig_title: Guidance on AI and data protection
receu4YfLqDNlrfEz.gen_title: ICO publishes restructured guidance on AI and data protection with new fairness content
receu4YfLqDNlrfEz.summary:
On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) published an updated version of its Guidance on AI and Data Protection, restructuring the material around core data protection principles and adding new sections addressing fairness in artificial intelligence systems.

The ICO states that the update responds to requests from UK industry to clarify requirements for fairness in AI, whilst supporting the UK government's pro-innovation approach to AI regulation. The authority notes that the restructuring moves existing content into new chapters organised by data protection principles, including accountability and governance, transparency, lawfulness, accuracy and fairness. The ICO indicates that this reorganisation reflects the foundational nature of data protection principles and acknowledges that further updates will be required as technology develops.

The revised guidance introduces a new standalone chapter on transparency in AI, with high-level content on the transparency principle as it applies to artificial intelligence systems. The authority has created a separate chapter on lawfulness in AI, incorporating existing content and adding new sections addressing AI and inferences, affinity groups and special category data. The ICO has also established a new chapter focused on accuracy and statistical accuracy, with content previously housed under fairness and transparency guidance now repositioned under the accuracy principle.

Significantly, the ICO has introduced a new chapter dedicated to fairness in AI, containing both new and existing content. The authority sets out data protection's approach to fairness and its application to AI, including a non-exhaustive list of legal provisions to consider. The ICO distinguishes between fairness, algorithmic fairness, bias and discrimination, and outlines high-level considerations for evaluating fairness and inherent trade-offs. The guidance addresses processing personal data for bias mitigation and technical approaches to mitigate algorithmic bias. Additionally, a new annex addresses fairness considerations across the AI lifecycle, from problem formulation to decommissioning, examining how fundamental aspects of AI development such as underlying assumptions and selection of target variables may impact fairness and possible sources of bias.

The ICO notes that the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 came into law on 19 June 2025, and states that this guidance is under review and may be subject to change. The authority continues to engage with the UK government and the Digital Regulation Cooperation Forum on regulatory reform proposals.

recxrafdYBtMUEafx.orig_title: Ny trusselsvurdering: Cybertruslen mod rumsektoren i Danmark – Styrelsen for Samfundssikkerhed
recxrafdYBtMUEafx.gen_title: Centre for Cyber Security Denmark assesses cyber threats to space sector as very high for espionage and crime
recxrafdYBtMUEafx.summary:
On 26 June 2025, the Centre for Cyber Security Denmark (Styrelsen for Samfundssikkerhed, SAMSIK) published its first threat assessment of cyber threats targeting Denmark's space sector, assessing distinct threat categories across espionage, crime, activism, destructive attacks and terrorism.

SAMSIK assesses the threat from cyber espionage against Denmark's space sector as very high. The authority finds that foreign states, particularly Russia and China, conduct cyber espionage to obtain insight into other states' space activities, steal new and innovative technology, and gain knowledge of defence capabilities. SAMSIK notes examples of cyber espionage directed at space organisations in Japan and the United States as evidence of this pattern.

The threat from cyber crime against the space sector is assessed as very high. SAMSIK finds that criminal hackers continuously attack organisations across Danish sectors, with particular concern regarding cybercriminals who conduct extortion attacks, compromise networks, encrypt valuable data, steal data and threaten to leak it. The authority notes that ransomware attacks and the sale of data and system access constitute a significant threat.

SAMSIK assesses the threat from cyber activism, particularly distributed denial of service (DDoS) attacks, as medium. The authority finds the threat level lower than for Denmark generally, as cyber activists likely do not attribute the same symbolic significance to the space sector as other sectors.

The threat from destructive cyber attacks is assessed as medium. SAMSIK finds that Russia, since early 2024, has demonstrated greater willingness to use hybrid measures with destructive effect against European NATO members. The authority assesses this includes destructive cyber attacks, with the likely purpose of influencing the population. SAMSIK notes that target selection will be influenced by where attackers have access or can readily obtain it. The authority states that the probability of destructive cyber attacks directed at Denmark could increase if the security policy situation escalates.

SAMSIK assesses the threat from cyber terrorism against Denmark's space sector as none, finding that cyber terrorism requires capabilities which militant extremists currently lack, and intent remains extremely limited.

recSpkBGHwLRjtp1d.orig_title: Sample Data protection impact assessment: Mobile gaming app
recSpkBGHwLRjtp1d.gen_title: ICO publishes sample DPIA for mobile gaming apps accessed by children
recSpkBGHwLRjtp1d.summary:
On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office published a sample Data Protection Impact Assessment intended as an example of good practice for companies developing mobile gaming applications for use by children in the United Kingdom. The ICO states that the sample DPIA applies to Standard 2 of the Children's Code, formally known as the Age-appropriate Design Code, which requires Data Protection Impact Assessments for Information Society Services likely to be accessed by children under age 18 in the UK.

The ICO notes that the sample DPIA is based on an imaginary product called "Cooking Numbers" and was developed in collaboration with Fundamentally Games, a gaming design consultancy. The ICO confirms that the product specification used to develop this sample DPIA will not be developed into an actual game and is intended for demonstration and learning purposes only. The sample is adapted from the ICO's existing DPIA template and follows the process outlined in the authority's DPIA guidance and the Children's Code requirements.

The ICO indicates that organisations should read the sample DPIA alongside the code's DPIA guidance and the criteria for acceptable DPIAs set out in European guidelines. The authority has also made available supporting design guidance, including worked examples based on the fictional product specification that illustrate data privacy moments and age-appropriate mindsets relevant to gaming applications. The ICO states these worked examples are designed to help companies identify what data they are processing and any associated risks.

The ICO notes that the publication of this guidance coincides with the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 coming into law on 19 June 2025, and indicates that existing guidance is under review and may be subject to change. The authority has signalled that its Plans for New and Updated Guidance page will communicate which guidance will be updated and timescales for those updates. The ICO welcomes feedback on the sample DPIA and invites recommendations for improvements.

recA54L4GboxHFKgo.orig_title: Guidance for the use of personal data in political campaigning
recA54L4GboxHFKgo.gen_title: Information Commissioner's Office publishes guidance on personal data use in political campaigning
recA54L4GboxHFKgo.summary:
On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) published guidance on the use of personal data in political campaigning, providing clarity on compliance with the UK General Data Protection Regulation (UK GDPR), the Data Protection Act 2018 (DPA) and the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations 2003 (PECR). The ICO states that the guidance does not introduce new obligations or responsibilities above existing data protection and electronic marketing laws, but instead offers practical advice to help those processing personal data for political campaigning purposes to comply with these frameworks.

The ICO notes that the guidance applies to controllers processing personal data for political campaigning purposes, which the authority defines as activity in support of, or against, a political party, a referendum campaign, a candidate standing for election, the success or failure of a recall petition, or as defined in the Political Parties and Referendums Act 2000 and the Representation of the People Act 1983. The guidance is aimed at registered political parties, candidates, referendum campaigners, non-party campaigners and recall petition campaigners. The ICO observes that the guidance does not cover internal party or campaign group elections, such as leadership elections.

The ICO recognises that political campaigning has become increasingly sophisticated with the development of new digital technologies and communication tools, and that campaigners now use advanced technology and commercial marketing techniques to understand potential voters and communicate political messages. The authority highlights that the often invisible nature of these techniques can affect public trust and confidence in how personal data is being used, and that this poses a risk which undermines the democratic process. The ICO determines that people can only make truly informed choices about who to vote for if they are sure their decisions have not been unfairly influenced. The authority emphasises that all organisations involved in political campaigning must work to the same data protection and direct marketing rules, regardless of the method or future technological developments employed.

The ICO notes that the Data (Use and Access) Act is coming into law on 19 June 2025 and that this guidance is under review and may be subject to change.

recSjXPVXUva35G9H.gen_title: ICO updates Children's code guidance following Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 commencement
recSjXPVXUva35G9H.summary:
On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) confirmed that its Children's code guidance and resources are under review following the commencement of the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025. The ICO stated that guidance may be subject to change and that its Plans for new and updated guidance page will detail which resources will be updated and when.

The ICO's Children's code guidance applies to organisations of all sizes and sectors providing online services likely to be accessed by children, including apps, online games, and web and social media sites. The ICO notes that the Children's code requires providers of such information society services (ISSs) to conform with fifteen standards for age-appropriate design. The guidance is structured to assist organisations in understanding their obligations, including through an introduction to the Children's code, a code of practice on age-appropriate design, and a Children's code strategy outlining enforcement priorities.

The ICO has published guidance addressing specific compliance areas, including the best interests of the child standard, the application of the code to services likely to be accessed by children even if not directly targeted at them, and age assurance requirements. The ICO's Commissioner has issued an opinion setting out expectations for how businesses must meet the code's age-appropriate application standard and expectations for age assurance data protection compliance. The ICO has also signed a joint statement on a common international approach to age assurance with other regulatory authorities, setting out key shared principles for signatory regulators.

Sector-specific resources have been published by the ICO, including frequently asked questions for education technologies and the digital news industry, and guidance for games designers covering risk assessments, age assurance, transparency, prevention of detrimental use of children's information, privacy settings, responsible profiling and positive nudge techniques. The ICO provides a Children's code self-assessment risk toolkit enabling organisations to conduct risk assessments of how the UK GDPR and the Children's code apply to their digital services, alongside Data Protection Impact Assessment templates and example assessments for online retail, connected toys and mobile games or apps.

rec5YXj65DKL4ZwNe.orig_title: Judgment of the Court (Tenth Chamber) of 22 May 2025. — European Commission v Republic of Bulgaria. — Failure of a Member State to fulfil obligations – Article 258 TFEU – Directive (EU) 2019/1024 – Open data and the re-use of public sector informatio
rec5YXj65DKL4ZwNe.gen_title: Court of Justice finds Bulgaria failed to transpose open data directive and orders financial penalties
rec5YXj65DKL4ZwNe.summary:
On 22 May 2025, the Court of Justice of the European Union (Tenth Chamber) determined that the Republic of Bulgaria failed to fulfil its obligations under Article 17 of Directive (EU) 2019/1024 on open data and the re-use of public sector information by failing to adopt or communicate the necessary legislative, regulatory and administrative measures for transposition (Art. 258 TFEU).

The Court found that Bulgaria omitted to adopt the legislative, regulatory and administrative provisions required to comply with the open data directive, or alternatively failed to communicate such provisions to the European Commission. The directive, adopted on 20 June 2019, establishes minimum rules and practical arrangements to facilitate the re-use of documents held by public sector bodies across EU Member States, including executive, legislative and judicial bodies. The Court noted that since the initial rules on public sector information re-use were adopted, the volume of data worldwide has increased exponentially, alongside rapid evolution in data analysis, processing and artificial intelligence technologies that enable creation of new services and applications based on data use, aggregation and combination.

The Court ordered Bulgaria to pay a lump sum to the Commission calculated as the higher of two amounts: a daily sum of EUR 1,800 multiplied by the number of days between the expiration of the transposition deadline and the date the infringement ended or, if persisting, the date of judgment in this case; or a minimum lump sum of EUR 504,000. Additionally, should the breach continue until the date of judgment, Bulgaria is required to pay a periodic penalty payment of EUR 9,720 per day from the date of judgment until the Member State complies with Directive 2019/1024. The Court also ordered Bulgaria to bear the costs of the proceedings. The Commission had sought these financial penalties under Article 260(3) TFEU in a case filed on 14 April 2023.
rec5YXj65DKL4ZwNe.body: Court of Justice of the European Union

rec17ctEvWNxxKRmt.orig_title: Judgment of the Court (Tenth Chamber) of 22 May 2025. — European Commission v Kingdom of the Netherlands. — Failure of a Member State to fulfil obligations – Article 258 TFEU – Directive (EU) 2019/1024 – Open data and the re-use of public sector info
rec17ctEvWNxxKRmt.gen_title: Court of Justice finds Netherlands failed to transpose open data directive and communicate measures
rec17ctEvWNxxKRmt.summary:
On 22 May 2025, the Court of Justice of the European Union (Tenth Chamber) found that the Kingdom of the Netherlands failed to fulfil its obligations under Article 17 of Directive (EU) 2019/1024 on open data and the re-use of public sector information by not adopting the necessary legislative, regulatory and administrative measures to comply with the directive and by not communicating those measures immediately to the European Commission.

The European Commission had brought proceedings under Article 258 TFEU against the Netherlands, alleging incomplete transposition of Directive 2019/1024 and failure to communicate transposition measures. The directive, adopted on 20 June 2019, establishes rules for the re-use of public sector information held by public bodies and information obtained with public funds, with the aim of stimulating digital innovation and economic growth across the European Union.

The Court determined that the Netherlands had not adopted the legislative and administrative provisions required to bring its law into conformity with the directive. The Court noted that the directive introduces fundamental changes to the legal framework governing public sector information, including provisions on real-time access to dynamic data through appropriate technical means, increased provision of valuable public data for re-use including from public undertakings and research bodies, and considerations of data protection in accordance with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). The directive updates the earlier 2003/98/CE framework to account for advances in digital technology and artificial intelligence.

The Commission sought a lump sum payment of either a daily amount of €13,900 multiplied by the number of days between the expiration of the transposition deadline and the judgment (or ending of the breach), or a minimum fixed sum of €3,892,000, whichever is higher. The Commission also sought a periodic penalty payment of €75,060 per day from the date of judgment until the Netherlands complied with its obligations under the directive. The Court's judgment addressed the failure in transposition and the Member State's obligation under Article 260(3) TFEU regarding financial penalties for non-compliance.
rec17ctEvWNxxKRmt.body: Court of Justice of the European Union

rec4DHU7COG8UlpTR.gen_title: European Court of Human Rights finds Russia violated rights of candidates excluded from elections based on vague extremism allegations
rec4DHU7COG8UlpTR.summary:
On 27 May 2025, the European Court of Human Rights (Third Section) determined in Selishcheva and Others v. Russia that the Russian Federation violated Article 8 (right to private life), Article 10 (freedom of expression) and Article 11 (freedom of assembly) by refusing to register ten independent candidates in municipal elections based on alleged involvement with organisations designated as extremist.

The ECHR found that Russia collected and stored applicants' political data shared with electoral authorities without a clear legal framework governing collection and use (Art. 8 GDPR principles). The Court determined that applicants had no means to access or challenge sensitive political data that had been gathered ad hoc with little transparency, retention periods or deletion rules, and without independent review mechanism. The authority noted that data collection created an open-ended interference not justified by violent conduct or genuine security concerns, and that the interference was not "in accordance with law" or "necessary in democratic society".

Regarding Articles 10 and 11, the ECHR found that electoral candidacy was denied based on retroactive penalisation for lawful activities. The Court determined that measures were not foreseeable, imposing an impossible burden on applicants to anticipate future restrictions. The authority noted that the vague concept of "involvement" allowed broad disqualifications without proof of extremism, and that lawful activities, including participation in unauthorised protests in January 2021 against the imprisonment of opposition politician Aleksey Navalnyy and support for the opposition Novosibirsk Coalition 2020, were deemed involvement in extremist organisations.

The ECHR found that no proportionality assessment or genuine extremist links had been established. The Court determined that legal uncertainty from vague statutory provisions created a "chilling effect" on political participation and freedom of expression. The authority concluded that the interference was not "prescribed by law" or "necessary in democratic society" under Articles 10 and 11. The judgment became final on 27 August 2025.

recwsrVsYZvuimLMk.orig_title: IEEE Provides Strategic Expertise as Indonesia Adopts First Age-Appropriate Design Regulation in Asia
recwsrVsYZvuimLMk.gen_title: Indonesia adopts first Asian age-appropriate design regulation with IEEE technical input
recwsrVsYZvuimLMk.summary:
Indonesia has passed the Government Regulation on Governance of Electronic Systems in Child Protection, the first age-appropriate design regulation of its kind in Asia and the Global South, with technical expertise provided by the IEEE Standards Association.

On 14 May 2025, the IEEE Standards Association announced its collaboration with Indonesian policymakers on the regulation. IEEE SA states that it advanced frameworks for age-appropriate design of internet platforms through insights grounded in IEEE initiatives and standards on children's data governance. The regulation establishes enforceable requirements for digital platforms to protect children's privacy, safety and well-being, including protections against online addiction.

The regulation imposes several specific requirements on online service providers. IEEE SA notes that the regulation requires providers to prioritise the best interests of children in platform design and practices, building on the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child and its General Comment No. 25. The regulation mandates a high level of privacy by default and prohibits profiling and precise geolocation of children. IEEE SA states that the regulation also bans manipulative design practices, including nudging to weaken privacy settings or requesting more personal data than necessary.

The Indonesian regulation aligns with advanced legal frameworks such as the UK and California Age-Appropriate Design Codes, according to IEEE SA. The regulation incorporates provisions from internationally recognised standards including IEEE 2089, the IEEE Standard for Age Appropriate Digital Services Framework, and IEEE 2089.1, the IEEE Standard for Online Age Verification. IEEE SA notes that embedding these standards ensures online services follow globally vetted guidelines for protecting children's data and experiences. IEEE SA has recently launched a certification programme to assist industry in conforming to age verification best practices. Sophia Muirhead, IEEE Executive Director and Chief Operating Officer, states that the collaboration demonstrates IEEE's mission to support societal needs through combining global perspective and technical knowledge with Indonesian authorities' dedication. Alpesh Shah, IEEE SA Managing Director, characterises the engagement as a model for collaborative policy development requiring cooperation across technical, governmental and civil society domains.

recF6rAjozDq25ZLE.orig_title: State Regulators Form Bipartisan Consortium to Collaborate on Privacy Issues - privacy.ca.gov
recF6rAjozDq25ZLE.gen_title: California Privacy Protection Agency and seven state regulators establish Consortium of Privacy Regulators
recF6rAjozDq25ZLE.summary:
On 16 April 2025, the California Privacy Protection Agency and state Attorneys General from California, Colorado, Connecticut, Delaware, Indiana, New Jersey, and Oregon announced the formation of the Consortium of Privacy Regulators, a bipartisan collaborative body established to coordinate implementation and enforcement of state privacy laws.

The eight regulators have entered into a memorandum of understanding outlining the Consortium's goals, which include facilitating discussions of privacy law developments and shared priorities with a focus on consumer protection across jurisdictions. Michael Macko, the California Privacy Protection Agency's head of enforcement, stated that the Consortium reflects the regulators' shared commitment to advance consistent, streamlined enforcement of privacy protections to address real-world privacy harms.

The Consortium was established to share expertise and resources among participating jurisdictions and to coordinate efforts to investigate potential violations of applicable laws. The California Privacy Protection Agency notes that although each state has its own privacy law, they share certain fundamental features to protect privacy, including rights to access, delete, and stop the sale of personal information, as well as similar obligations on businesses. These commonalities, the Agency determines, pave the way for like-minded applications across jurisdictions and enable the Consortium to work together on the common goal of promoting consumer privacy rights.

The California Privacy Protection Agency emphasises that consumer privacy protection is increasingly important given rapid changes in technology and uses of personal information. The Agency recognises that whilst consumer data can fuel innovation and serve the needs of customers and clients, it is also used to target, exploit, and expose consumers' highly personal information in ways that can be dangerous and costly. The Consortium holds regular meetings and coordinates enforcement, as appropriate, based on the members' common interests. The California Privacy Protection Agency website provides information about board meetings, announcements, and the rulemaking process, as well as resources for businesses and individuals seeking to understand their privacy rights and responsibilities.
