rec0bkaRpvKYUAHhA.gen_title: European Commission launches public consultation on hospital cybersecurity action plan and calls for advisory board members
rec0bkaRpvKYUAHhA.summary:
On 14 May 2025, the National Cybersecurity Authority of the Czech Republic announced that the European Commission has initiated a public consultation on a European action plan for cybersecurity in hospitals and healthcare providers, with submissions open until 30 June 2025.

The action plan, published on 15 January 2025, addresses cybersecurity in the health sector across four principal areas: building capacity to prevent cybersecurity incidents; improving information sharing and threat detection capabilities; enhancing incident response and recovery capacity; and identifying ways to counter potential cybersecurity threat actors. The NÃKIB notes that the action plan identifies deficiencies and challenges within the healthcare sector and responds with concrete initiatives at European Union level, alongside recommendations for activities by member states at national level.

The NÃKIB confirms that the public consultation is not administered by the National Cybersecurity Authority and directs interested parties to the European Commission's digital strategy website for full details. The authority welcomes potential interest from Czech entities in contributing to the consultation process, which may influence the final form of recommendations for EU entities and member states, as well as activities outlined in the action plan.

Concurrently, the European Commission has issued a call for applications to join a newly established Health Cybersecurity Advisory Board, which will support the Commission and the European Union cybersecurity support centre for hospitals and healthcare providers established within the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) during implementation of the action plan. The NÃKIB states that the advisory board will also support collaboration between public and private sectors. The authority expresses support for potential interest from Czech entities in board membership, noting that participation may contribute to prioritising and effectively implementing activities outlined in the plan and strengthening public-private sector collaboration. Board members will undertake advisory roles and identify and share best practices in cybersecurity, as well as share information with healthcare entities. Applications from individuals and organisations working across healthcare and cybersecurity sectors closed on 23 May 2025.

recvNBkYeoGHdVEqx.gen_title: AEPD releases data anonymisation tool for organisations
recvNBkYeoGHdVEqx.summary:
On 9 April 2025, the Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD) released a data anonymisation tool designed to assist organisations in achieving anonymisation of personal data in compliance with data protection obligations under Spanish and European law.

The tool is provided as an Excel-based application available for download from the AEPD website. The AEPD has encrypted the file to prevent antivirus software from quarantining the spreadsheet. Users must extract the file using 7-Zip or equivalent software with the password "DIT-AEPD". The AEPD notes that Excel may block macro execution, requiring users to unblock the file through Windows file properties before use.

The AEPD specifies system requirements for the tool's operation. The anonymisation tool was developed and tested on Microsoft Windows with Excel versions 2010, 2016 and 2019. The AEPD states that the tool may not function correctly on other Excel versions or operating systems. Users are required to ensure their device's regional settings are configured to "Español (España)" via Windows Region settings for the tool to operate properly.

To verify authenticity and confirm the downloaded file has not been altered, the AEPD provides verification instructions using Windows Command Prompt. Users must execute the command "certutil -hashfile herramienta-anonimizacion-pdpc-es.zip sha256" and confirm the output hash matches "30ab954cc35f67bc2666a8ac6d94676fceb314e9552863592c0895bccf846695". The AEPD considers this hash verification essential to ensuring the file's integrity and origin.

The tool represents the AEPD's contribution to supporting data controllers and processors in meeting their legal obligations regarding personal data processing. The provision of this technical resource demonstrates the authority's commitment to facilitating compliance with anonymisation requirements under applicable data protection frameworks.
recvNBkYeoGHdVEqx.body: Agencia Espanola de Proteccion de Datos

recJss5qr8B0hIMrI.gen_title: Hamburg data protection authority publishes guidance on EU Data Act implementation from September 2025
recJss5qr8B0hIMrI.summary:
On 29 April 2025, the Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (Hamburg Data Protection and Freedom of Information Commissioner) published information on the European Union's Data Act, which becomes directly applicable law from September 2025. The authority notes that the Data Act introduces new obligations requiring organisations to share data whilst maintaining data protection compliance.

The Data Act targets existing monopolies on access to data generated by internet-connected devices, commonly referred to as the Internet of Things. The Hamburg authority explains that the regulation enables data users, including individuals and business customers of connected products, to determine who may access data generated during device use. Third parties, such as independent repair shops, aftermarket parts manufacturers, and research institutions, gain rights to access device-generated data previously controlled exclusively by original equipment manufacturers. The authority illustrates this through examples including vehicle maintenance data accessible to independent garages, smart toothbrush usage data available to dental research facilities, and container sensor data usable by shipping companies and parts manufacturers.

The Hamburg authority identifies data access as the core obligation under the Data Act. Access rights apply to all data types, including both personal and non-personal data. However, the authority finds that personal data may only be shared where data protection law permits. The authority states that in many cases, no legal basis exists under the General Data Protection Regulation for disclosing personal business data, meaning Data Act obligations principally affect non-personal data. The authority determines that data protection personnel within organisations must assess whether personal data exists and whether sharing complies with the GDPR before granting access. Where technically feasible and relevant, access must be provided directly through an interface or customer portal; otherwise, organisations must respond to individual access requests. The authority notes its own role transforms, as data protection authorities will increasingly determine whether data access must be granted according to Data Act requirements in disputes between organisations.
recJss5qr8B0hIMrI.body: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

recJdppFF21auMEi3.orig_title: Cyber Security and Resilience of smart cars
recJdppFF21auMEi3.gen_title: ENISA publishes good practices on cyber security and resilience of smart cars
recJdppFF21auMEi3.summary:
The European Union Agency for Network and Information Security (ENISA) has published guidance identifying good practices to ensure the security of smart cars against cyber threats whilst maintaining vehicle safety. The study, published in December 2016, catalogues sensitive assets in smart cars and maps corresponding threats, risks, mitigation factors and security measures.

ENISA convened experts from automotive manufacturers, suppliers, telecommunications providers, security firms and government agencies to develop the guidance. The agency consulted with specialists including representatives from Fiat Chrysler Automobile Group, Continental Teves, NXP Semiconductors, Kaspersky Lab, Nokia Solutions and Networks, and national authorities including France's Gendarmerie Nationale and the Netherlands' RDW vehicle authority. This collaborative approach enabled ENISA to synthesise industry know-how and expertise across multiple disciplines relevant to vehicle security.

The resulting publication organises good practices into three categories: Policy and standards, Organisational measures, and Security functions. ENISA notes that smart car security must address a dual imperative: protecting vehicles against cyber threats whilst simultaneously guaranteeing the safety outcomes critical to vehicle operation. The guidance identifies sensitive assets present in smart cars across multiple control systems, including powertrain control, chassis control, body control and infotainment control subsystems.

The study represents ENISA's assessment of the state of security practices applicable to connected vehicles at the time of publication. ENISA states that the publication reflects the views and interpretations of its authors and editors and is intended for information purposes. The agency notes it may update the guidance periodically. The publication is made available free of charge and ENISA disclaims responsibility for use made of the information contained within it.

recJzSRFGsH9LjZBU.orig_title: Judgment of the Court (First Chamber) of 3 April 2025. — L. H. v Ministerstvo zdravotnictví. — Request for a preliminary ruling from the Nejvyšší správní soud. — Reference for a preliminary ruling – Protection of natural persons with regard to the pr
recJzSRFGsH9LjZBU.gen_title: Court of Justice clarifies GDPR application to personal data of legal persons' representatives in public documents
recJzSRFGsH9LjZBU.summary:
On 3 April 2025, the Court of Justice of the European Union (First Chamber) ruled on the interpretation of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) in relation to the disclosure of personal data concerning representatives of legal persons contained in official documents held by public authorities.

The case, L.H. v Ministerstvo zdravotnictví, arose from a Czech dispute concerning the Minister of Health's refusal to disclose information identifying representatives of legal persons named in contracts for COVID-19 screening tests and related certificates. The Czech Supreme Administrative Court sought preliminary guidance on whether such data fell within GDPR protection and, if so, on what basis disclosure could be lawfully made.

The Court examined Articles 4, 6(1)(c) and 6(1)(e) of the GDPR in light of the principle of public access to official documents. The Court found that personal data identifying representatives of legal persons, even when those persons act in a representative capacity, remains subject to GDPR protection. The data subject status of an individual representative does not depend on whether their identity is disclosed in connection with a legal person's business activities.

The Court determined that disclosure of such personal data by a public authority may be lawful under Article 6(1)(c) GDPR, which permits processing necessary for compliance with a legal obligation, or Article 6(1)(e) GDPR, which permits processing necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest. However, the Court recognised that GDPR protection must be balanced against the fundamental right of public access to official documents, as contemplated in Recital 154 of the GDPR. Member State law may establish circumstances under which such disclosure is permitted, provided such law reconciles public access principles with data protection rights in accordance with the principle of proportionality.

The judgment affirmed that GDPR protections apply consistently across Member States whilst permitting national laws to specify the conditions under which disclosure of personal data in official documents serves a public interest objective.
recJzSRFGsH9LjZBU.body: Court of Justice of the European Union

recfuzEbsQUVJNEyZ.gen_title: AISI Japan publishes overview of known attacks on AI systems and their impacts
recfuzEbsQUVJNEyZ.summary:
On 19 March 2025, the AI Safety Institute Japan (AISI) published a document categorising known attacks targeting AI systems and documenting their impacts across development and operational environments.

AISI identifies attacks that exploit the specific characteristics of artificial intelligence models, distinguishing between those affecting conventional cybersecurity and those requiring attention unique to AI incorporation. The institute notes that attacks may occur during training phases, such as data poisoning and model poisoning, or during inference phases, including model extraction and adversarial evasion. The document states that real-world impacts of such attacks include training data leaks from AI systems used in medical diagnosis that violate privacy, and abnormal AI outputs in automated driving systems that can cause traffic accidents.

AISI structures its analysis around two system environments: development (training) and operation. The institute identifies 11 categories of attack impact: model leakage, training data leakage, application information leakage, input information leakage, internal data leakage, model malfunction, interpretability malfunction, safeguard bypass, system compromise, internal data corruption, and computational waste. The document maps 11 distinct attack types (labelled A through K) to these impacts, noting that some impacts may occur at multiple points within a system. AISI's framework categorises these impacts across confidentiality, integrity and availability dimensions.

The institute states that attacks specifically involving intervention in AI model training or inference processes, or exploitation or tampering of inputs and outputs, fall within its scope. By contrast, theft of AI models through unauthorised network access exploiting device vulnerabilities falls outside the document's focus, as such attacks do not involve direct interference with training or inference processes. AISI notes that measures against conventional cybersecurity attacks remain necessary and that AI-specific security measures are essential to address evolving threat vectors.

rec0GMebjdoPpbbIW.orig_title: Largest cyber security operation ever in Belgium: 2410 organizations from critical sectors take action
rec0GMebjdoPpbbIW.gen_title: Belgium registers 2410 critical sector organisations under NIS2 directive
rec0GMebjdoPpbbIW.summary:
The Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB) has registered 2410 organisations from critical sectors under the Network and Information Security Directive 2 (NIS2) since its introduction in October 2024. Belgium became the first European member state to fully implement the NIS2 directive, which requires organisations in energy, transport, healthcare, digital infrastructure, government and manufacturing to enhance cybersecurity and resilience measures. An estimated 2500 organisations fall within NIS2 scope, meaning the vast majority have registered with the CCB by the mandatory registration deadline.

On 17 March 2025, the CCB confirmed that more than 4500 organisations across all sectors have registered on the Safeonweb@Work platform, with 2410 from sectors designated as critical under NIS2 legislation. Miguel De Bruycker, director general of the CCB, stated that organisations in Belgium are working to elevate cybersecurity standards and that the CCB expects registrations and incident reports to continue increasing before stabilising in subsequent months.

Incident reporting to the CCB has risen significantly since NIS2 implementation. The authority received 556 cyber incident reports over an 18-month period, averaging 25 reports monthly from August 2023 through September 2024. Following NIS2's introduction, the CCB received 226 incidents between October 2024 and the registration deadline, representing an average of 45 monthly reports and an 80 per cent increase compared to the pre-NIS2 period. The CCB notes that the increase reflects improved reporting obligations under NIS2 rather than a rise in actual cyber attacks on Belgian organisations. De Bruycker identified that underreporting occurred previously, as organisations either lacked awareness of reporting requirements or did not recognise their obligation to notify the authority.

Registered organisations, whether NIS2-designated or voluntary registrants, receive benefits including priority vulnerability alerts, cyber threat notifications, security assessment reports, self-assessment questionnaires and ready-made policy templates. NIS2 organisations receive priority access to these services. The Port of Ostend CEO cited the registration's utility following a cyber attack in February 2025, stating that CCB intervention through the registration system enabled rapid and efficient response.

recwQLMntQbMsUCTp.gen_title: ENISA hosts 10th eHealth Security Conference in Bucharest on NIS2 implementation and healthcare cyber threats
recwQLMntQbMsUCTp.summary:
The EU Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) is organising the 10th eHealth Security Conference on 16 September 2025 in Bucharest, in collaboration with the Romanian National Cyber Security Directorate (DNSC), the European Cybersecurity Competence Centre (ECCC), and Bucharest University of Economic Studies. The full-day physical event will address cybersecurity challenges facing the healthcare sector and implementation of regulatory requirements.

On 26 March 2025, ENISA announced the conference structure, which will focus on the application of the NIS2 Directive to the health sector alongside related developments in eHealth policy, including the European Health Data Space (EHDS). The event is designed to bring together healthcare IT professionals, clinical information security specialists, medical device manufacturers, national healthcare authorities, compliance and privacy professionals, standardisation bodies and academic representatives to exchange insights on emerging cyber threats.

The conference agenda comprises four sessions. Session 1 addresses policy developments, including the EU action plan for hospital and healthcare provider cybersecurity and NIS2 implementation challenges from an entity perspective. Session 2 examines the evolving threat landscape, with contributions from the German Federal Office for Information Security (BSI) on medical device vulnerabilities and cyber resilience approaches from Romania, Moldova and Ukraine. Session 3 focuses on ENISA's efforts supporting implementation of the EU action plan for healthcare cybersecurity, including challenges identified by Romania's cyber security authority. Session 4 covers cooperation frameworks, good practices and funding opportunities, including European Cybersecurity Competence Centre calls for proposals and cyber security projects targeting healthcare supply chain resilience and patient data protection.

ENISA notes that medical data and systems have become a major target of cybercrime, with the healthcare ecosystem facing risks from ransomware, malicious actors and phishing campaigns. The conference will facilitate exchange of best practices and establish professional networks amongst participants working to strengthen cybersecurity across healthcare's public and private entities.

recPj5zSOhL0tH9ny.orig_title: California Privacy Protection Agency - Stipulated Final Order
recPj5zSOhL0tH9ny.gen_title: CPPA issues Stipulated Final Order against Background Alert for data broker registration violation
recPj5zSOhL0tH9ny.summary:
On 26 February 2025, the California Privacy Protection Agency adopted a Stipulated Final Order against Background Alert, Inc. in Case No. ENF24-D-BA-23, finding that the company failed to register as a data broker as required under California Civil Code § 1798.99.82(c) (the Delete Act).

Background Alert operates backgroundalert.com, an online people search platform that allows consumers to search for individuals using their first name, last name, and state. The CPPA determined that Background Alert's business model involves compiling reports by searching public records such as birth records, death records, arrest records, marriage and divorce records, professional licences, and sex offender databases. The company markets its services by claiming it can search "literally billions" of such records to provide comprehensive reports on individuals.

The CPPA found that Background Alert's service extends beyond merely providing public records. The agency determined that the company generates Inferences, defined under California Civil Code § 1798.140(r), as a significant component of its business model. The CPPA identified that Background Alert searches for "patterns" in public records and draws conclusions to identify potential family members and others who "may somehow be associated with" searched-for individuals. The agency noted that determining such associations requires Background Alert to infer relationships based on patterns identified in public records.

The CPPA concluded that these Inferences constitute Personal Information under the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), as defined in Civil Code § 1798.140(v)(1). The agency found that Inferences derived from postal addresses and employment-related information used to create consumer profiles fall within the statutory definition adopted by the Delete Act under Civil Code § 1798.99.80(a). The relevant period for the agency's investigation was 1 January 2023 through 11 February 2025. The Order became effective immediately upon adoption on 26 February 2025.

rec311qgRXvi47liu.orig_title: Manual do Usuário Externo do SEi!-ANPD e do Módulo de Consulta Pública
rec311qgRXvi47liu.gen_title: ANPD publishes user manual for electronic filing system and public search module
rec311qgRXvi47liu.summary:
On 14 March 2025, the Autoridade Nacional de Proteção de Dados (ANPD) published an updated user manual for external users of the SEi!-ANPD electronic filing system and its associated public search module. The manual, version dated 4 March 2025, provides comprehensive guidance on accessing and utilising ANPD's digital processes platform.

The ANPD manual covers external user registration procedures, including account creation, login and password recovery functions. The guidance sets out the steps required for users to register in the system and details the process for modifying existing user account information. Users encountering difficulties during registration are directed to contact the ANPD via email at protocolo@anpd.gov.br.

The manual details electronic petitioning functionality, enabling external parties to submit both new process filings and intercurrent petitions within existing proceedings. The ANPD specifies maximum file sizes for document uploads and provides guidance on optical character recognition (OCR) processing for digitised PDF documents. The manual addresses external access to processes, external signature capabilities, and the control of legal representation directly within the SEi! system, including procedures for appointing and revoking electronic powers of attorney for both individuals and legal entities.

Section 7 of the manual outlines electronic notification procedures, explaining how users receive email notification of formal communications, the method for complying with electronic notifications, and the process for responding to notified matters requiring a reply. The ANPD notes that the system will send reiterative emails regarding notifications that require substantive responses.

The manual includes a dedicated module for public search functionality within SEi! ANPD. The ANPD describes search capabilities allowing users to locate documents and processes by process number, document number, free text search, originating unit, process type, document type, and time period. The guidance explains the submission and results display functionality within the public search module, enabling external access to published ANPD decisions and proceedings within the scope authorised for public disclosure.
rec311qgRXvi47liu.body: Autoridade Nacional de Protecao de Dados

recT3SdYnlYi6q5bc.orig_title: A Governance Framework for National Cybersecurity Strategies
recT3SdYnlYi6q5bc.gen_title: ENISA publishes governance framework for national cybersecurity strategies
recT3SdYnlYi6q5bc.summary:
On 28 February 2023, the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) published a governance framework designed to support EU Member States in establishing and deploying effective governance structures for the implementation of national cybersecurity strategies (NCSS).

ENISA developed the framework following consultation with cybersecurity experts and officials from across the EU Member States. The agency notes that the publication outlines key findings from a comprehensive study examining good practices in governance setup and deployment across European countries. The framework identifies principal elements of governance that Member States have established to ensure effective implementation of current and future national cybersecurity strategies.

The publication represents ENISA's interpretation and views on governance practices in EU cybersecurity strategy implementation. ENISA states that the framework is intended for information purposes and must be made accessible free of charge. The agency notes that the publication does not necessarily represent state-of-the-art approaches and may be updated from time to time. ENISA clarifies that the document does not constitute a legal action unless formally adopted pursuant to Regulation (EU) No 2019/881.

The framework draws on contributions from cybersecurity authorities and policy officials across multiple Member States, including Austria, Belgium, Croatia, Cyprus, Czech Republic, Denmark, Estonia, Finland, Germany, Greece, Ireland, Italy, Lithuania, Malta, the Netherlands, Slovakia, Spain and Sweden. ENISA notes that some experts who contributed to the study preferred to remain anonymous.

The publication includes a collection of good practices covering different governance elements that European countries have implemented. ENISA indicates that a more detailed examination of governance frameworks appears in its accompanying report "Building Effective Governance Frameworks for the Implementation of National Cybersecurity Strategies". The framework is catalogued as TP-04-22-152-EN-N with ISBN 978-92-9204-589-0 and DOI 10.2824/211856.

recFKMJtr0IjS426M.gen_title: California Privacy Protection Agency settles with data broker Background Alert over registration failures
recFKMJtr0IjS426M.summary:
On 26 February 2025, the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) Board approved a settlement agreement with Background Alert, Inc., a data broker operating in California, requiring the company to cease its data broker operations for three years and to pay a $50,000 fine if it violates the agreement terms.

The CPPA Enforcement Division alleged that Background Alert created and sold consumer profiles through its website, backgroundalert.com, by amassing billions of public records and drawing inferences to identify individuals and generate profiles about consumers. The settlement resolves allegations that Background Alert failed to register with the CPPA between February 1 and October 8, 2024, in violation of the Delete Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.99.82(c)). Under the Delete Act, data brokers are required to register with the CPPA and pay an annual fee. The Enforcement Division found that Background Alert marketed its services by stating "It's scary how much information you can dig up on someone," drawing inferences about consumers to profile them.

The CPPA states that Background Alert must cease its operations through 2028 or face enforcement of the $50,000 fine. Michael Macko, the Agency's head of enforcement, noted that the settlement reflects the CPPA's commitment to ensuring data brokers comply with their obligations under the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) and the Delete Act. The CPPA determined that if a business operates as a data broker, it has responsibilities under California's comprehensive privacy law and must register accordingly.

This settlement is part of an ongoing investigative sweep of data broker registration compliance that the CPPA announced in October 2024. Businesses that operated as data brokers in 2024 were required to register with the CPPA by January 31, 2025, or face fines of $200 per day. The Delete Act requires data brokers to register and pay annual fees that fund the California Data Broker Registry and the development of the Delete Request and Opt-Out Platform (DROP), a mechanism to allow consumers to direct all data brokers to delete their personal information through a single request.

recc2YH99GE6NHVes.orig_title: Tilpass digitale tenester til grenseoverskridande informasjonsutveksling innanfor EØS-området
recc2YH99GE6NHVes.gen_title: Norwegian Digitalisation Agency sets requirement for state entities to align digital services with cross-border EEA information exchange
recc2YH99GE6NHVes.summary:
On 18 February 2025, the Norwegian Digitalisation Agency (Digdir) updated its guidance requiring state authorities to adapt digital services to facilitate cross-border information exchange within the European Economic Area. The requirement applies to state entities, whilst municipalities receive a recommendation to the same effect.

Digdir states that digital services must, where appropriate, be adapted to enable information exchange across national borders. The agency finds that this adaptation is necessary to allow public authorities, businesses and citizens to perform tasks digitally within the EEA region. The requirement is grounded in the Digitalisation Circular (Digitaliseringsrundskrivet), which establishes the framework for digital governance and interoperability across Norwegian public administration.

The guidance was originally established on 17 December 2021 and has now been updated with effect from 18 February 2025. Digdir identifies the adaptation of digital services for cross-border exchange as falling within the category of data sharing and use, specifically addressing cross-border services. The agency frames this as part of its broader mandate to coordinate digitalisation across the public sector and establish standards for digital architecture.

The distinction between the mandatory requirement for state authorities and the recommendation for municipalities reflects the hierarchical structure of Norwegian governance, with central state bodies subject to binding requirements under the Digitalisation Circular. Digdir provides further guidance and support through its national architecture team, available at nasjonalarkitektur@digdir.no, and references the Digital Europe Programme as an additional resource for implementation.

The update does not introduce new substantive obligations but rather clarifies and reinforces existing requirements concerning digital service interoperability. Digdir notes that entities may contact the agency with examples of implementation, indicating an ongoing engagement with public bodies regarding practical application of the cross-border information exchange standard.

recLMjzBRxuPTwfq4.orig_title: Kurzmeldungen - EDSA veröffentlicht Expertise zu Aspekten in KI-Anwendungen
recLMjzBRxuPTwfq4.gen_title: EDPB publishes expertise on data protection aspects of artificial intelligence applications
recLMjzBRxuPTwfq4.summary:
The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) has published expert guidance on data protection requirements applicable to artificial intelligence systems, following an initiative by the German Federal Data Protection Officer (Bundesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit, BfDI).

On 6 February 2025, the EDPB released the expertise through its Support Pool of Experts programme. The BfDI states that the guidance addresses challenges arising when personal data is processed through artificial intelligence procedures, with particular focus on compliance with data protection legal requirements throughout such processing activities.

Dr Kris Shrishak, the expert commissioned to conduct the investigation, examined multiple dimensions of artificial intelligence application governance. The EDPB's analysis covers the identification and mitigation of discriminatory bias and discrimination risks within algorithmic systems, recognising these as material compliance challenges for organisations deploying artificial intelligence. The guidance further addresses the effective implementation of data subject rights across the different lifecycle phases of artificial intelligence applications, from development and deployment through to operational use and decommissioning.

The expertise recognises that when personal data undergoes processing via artificial intelligence techniques, organisations must observe applicable data protection requirements at each stage of the technology's lifecycle. The EDPB notes that data subject rights, including access, rectification, erasure and objection rights, present particular implementation challenges when exercised in relation to artificial intelligence systems, requiring specific technical and procedural approaches to ensure meaningful protection.

The complete guidance document is publicly available on the EDPB's website through its Support Pool of Experts projects section, where member states' data protection authorities and other stakeholders may access the detailed analysis and recommendations. The publication reflects ongoing regulatory focus on ensuring that algorithmic decision-making and artificial intelligence deployment remain compatible with the European Union's data protection framework.
recLMjzBRxuPTwfq4.body: Bundesbeauftragte fur den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit

recBerT1hnaJVZsOw.gen_title: CPPA and South Korea's PIPC sign declaration of cooperation on privacy enforcement
recBerT1hnaJVZsOw.summary:
On 10 January 2025, the California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) and the Personal Information Protection Commission (PIPC) of the Republic of Korea signed a declaration of cooperation to advance cross-border collaboration on privacy and data protection enforcement.

Dr. Haksoo Ko, Chairperson of the PIPC, and Mr. Ashkan Soltani, Executive Director of the CPPA, executed the declaration, which establishes a framework for both authorities to collaborate on safeguarding personal information. The CPPA states that the declaration enables the authorities to share best practices, knowledge and information on procedures and methods, including in the course of their investigations. The agreement further permits the authorities to share annual enforcement plans and annual activity reports, facilitate meetings between staff members, and develop appropriate mechanisms for mutual collaboration.

The PIPC noted that the cooperation aligns with its ongoing efforts to diversify and expand its global partnership network. In a statement, Dr. Ko said the agreement is intended to enhance benefits for citizens in both jurisdictions and provide an opportunity to strengthen cooperation on addressing privacy issues. The CPPA's Executive Director emphasised that international cooperation is essential in addressing privacy challenges in an interconnected world, and that exchanging best practices and strengthening collective efforts enables both authorities to enhance their impact in safeguarding privacy on a global scale.

Both authorities play a key role in the global privacy and data protection landscape. The CPPA is responsible for promoting education and awareness of consumers' privacy rights and businesses' responsibilities under the California Consumer Privacy Act. The partnership reflects the increasing need for cross-border cooperation in the digital era and demonstrates the shared commitment of both jurisdictions to privacy and data protection.

rec4GICv1s4XR6SH6.orig_title: From deepfake scams to biased AI: How incident reporting can help us keep ahead of AI’s harms
rec4GICv1s4XR6SH6.gen_title: OECD develops common reporting framework for AI incidents across jurisdictions
rec4GICv1s4XR6SH6.summary:
On 31 January 2025, the OECD AI Observatory published a common reporting framework for AI incidents, establishing a standardised approach to document and track AI-related harms across industries and countries. The framework defines an AI incident as any event in which an AI system's development, use, or malfunction causes harm, either directly or indirectly.

The OECD notes that AI incidents are escalating in scale and complexity. The Observatory identifies deepfake fraud as a growing threat: in 2023, France alone recorded over 130,000 online scams, an 8 per cent increase from the previous year, with similar trends across Europe and the United States. The OECD documents cases including a woman defrauded of €830,000 through deepfake videos and AI-generated images impersonating a celebrity, illustrating how AI-powered scams have become more convincing and harder to detect.

Beyond fraud, the OECD observes that AI incidents arise from both malicious intent and flawed design. The framework identifies bias in algorithmic systems as a persistent concern. The OECD cites instances including a major technology company that scrapped an AI-powered recruitment tool after discovering discrimination against women, and another firm that faced a six-figure fine when its AI system unfairly excluded women over 55 and men over 60. The Observatory also documents a case where faulty facial recognition wrongly linked an individual to a theft, resulting in his arrest and ten days in jail despite living in a different city. In healthcare, the OECD notes that one AI system unintentionally prioritised White patients over Black patients, though intervention by developers reduced the bias by over 80 per cent.

The OECD states that the common reporting framework enables policymakers to make better-informed decisions, identify high-risk AI systems faster, and improve global knowledge-sharing. The framework provides a consistent yet flexible mechanism to document incidents across diverse legal and policy environments, supporting coordinated responses across countries and regulatory bodies. The OECD's AI Incidents Monitor tracks and analyses AI-related incidents reported in the media to inform the development and implementation of governance responses.
